- The **instruction set** is the chef's recipe book, with each recipe (instruction) telling the chef exactly *which* utensil to pick, *which* ingredient to grab, and *what action* (add, move, compare, jump) to perform.

A beginner chef can survive with a small vocabulary (a few recipes), but a master chef can cook thousands of dishes using a small set of well-defined techniques. Similarly, a skilled embedded engineer can build complex systems using a small, well-understood set of 8051 instructions.

## 1.3 Three Core Building Blocks of an 8051 Instruction

Every 8051 instruction is built from these three building blocks:

1. **Opcode Byte (8 bits)** – Tells the CPU *what operation* to perform (e.g., ADD, MOV, JNZ).
2. **Operand Byte(s) (0, 8, or 16 bits)** – Tells the CPU *where to get the data* (source) and *where to put the result* (destination).
3. **Addressing Mode (encoded implicitly)** – Tells the CPU *how to interpret* the operand bytes (immediate, register, direct, indirect, indexed).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** The *opcode* defines the operation, while the *addressing mode* defines how the operand bytes are interpreted. A single opcode such as `MOV` (0x74, 0x75, 0x76... etc.) can support multiple addressing modes through different opcode variants.

## 1.4 Instruction Size Classification (Byte-Length)

| Instruction Type | Size | Typical Format | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1-byte instruction | **1 byte** | `[ Opcode ]` | `INC A` |
| 2-byte instruction | **2 bytes** | `[ Opcode ] [ Operand ]` | `MOV A, #55H` |
| 3-byte instruction | **3 bytes** | `[ Opcode ] [ Operand1 ] [ Operand2 ]` | `MOV 30H, #45H` |

This compactness is one of the major reasons the 8051 became the most widely taught microcontroller in undergraduate programs worldwide, including KTU's ECE/EEE/CSE curriculum.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Addressing Modes of the 8051

The 8051 supports **five distinct addressing modes**. This is a **high-yield KTU topic** – expect at least one direct sub-question in the ESE.

### 2.1.1 Immediate Addressing (`#data`)
- **Format:** `OPCODE #data8` or `OPCODE #data16`
- **Mechanism:** The operand *is* the data; the actual 8-bit or 16-bit constant is embedded directly inside the instruction.
- **Mnemonic Symbol:** The `#` symbol denotes "the literal value that follows."
- **Example:** `MOV A, #0F0H` → loads the immediate constant `F0H` into accumulator.
- **Real-world Utility:** Used to initialize registers with known constants, set up port values, or load loop counters.

### 2.1.2 Register Addressing
- **Format:** `OPCODE Rn` where `n = 0–7`
- **Mechanism:** The operand field refers to one of the 8 working registers `R0–R7` of the currently selected register bank.
- **Example:** `MOV A, R4` → copies content of register R4 into A.
- **Real-world Utility:** Fastest addressing mode – no memory fetch required because the register file is internal to the CPU.

### 2.1.3 Direct Addressing
- **Format:** `OPCODE direct address` (00H–FFH)
- **Mechanism:** The operand contains the **actual 8-bit address** of the internal RAM location (lower 128 bytes) or SFR (upper 128 bytes).
- **Example:** `MOV 30H, A` → copies A into internal RAM location 30H.
- **Real-world Utility:** The *only* mode that lets the programmer directly address the **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** like `P0`, `P1`, `TMOD`, `TCON`, `IE`.

### 2.1.4 Register Indirect Addressing
- **Format:** `OPCODE @Ri` where `i = 0 or 1`, or `OPCODE @DPTR`
- **Mechanism:** The operand is a *pointer* held in R0, R1, or DPTR. The CPU dereferences the pointer to access memory.
- **Example:** `MOV A, @R0` → If R0 = 30H, then A ← contents of RAM[30H].
- **Real-world Utility:** Essential for **table lookup, array processing, and stack pointer (SP)-based PUSH/POP**.

### 2.1.5 Indexed (Base-Register Plus Index-Register) Addressing
- **Format:** `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` or `MOVC A, @A+PC`
- **Mechanism:** Effective address = content of DPTR (or PC) + content of A.
- **Example:** Used exclusively for **read-only program memory (code ROM) lookup** via the `MOVC` instruction.
- **Real-world Utility:** Implementing **lookup tables (LUTs)** such as 7-segment display patterns, sine-wave tables, ASCII conversion tables.

## 2.2 The Five Functional Groups of Instructions

### Group 1: Data Transfer Instructions
- **Mnemonics:** `MOV`, `MOVX`, `MOVC`, `XCH`, `XCHD`, `PUSH`, `POP`, `SWAP`
- **Effect:** Copy data between registers, internal RAM, external RAM, and code ROM.
- **Flag Impact:** Does **not** affect any PSW flags (no Carry, Auxiliary Carry, or Zero flag changes).
- **Real-world Utility:** The most-used group; forms the data-movement backbone of every program.

### Group 2: Arithmetic Instructions
- **Mnemonics:** `ADD`, `ADDC`, `SUBB`, `INC`, `DEC`, `MUL AB`, `DIV AB`, `DA A`
- **Effect:** Perform addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, BCD correction, increment, decrement.
- **Flag Impact:** Affects the **Carry (CY), Auxiliary Carry (AC), and Overflow (OV)** flags of the PSW.
- **Real-world Utility:** Numerical computation, scaling sensor values, BCD arithmetic for displays.

### Group 3: Logical Instructions
- **Mnemonics:** `ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`, `CLR A`, `CPL A`, `RL`, `RLC`, `RR`, `RRC`, `SWAP A`
- **Effect:** Perform bitwise AND, OR, XOR, complement, rotations on the accumulator byte.
- **Flag Impact:** Only the **Parity (P) flag** of PSW is affected. Carry and AC are reset to zero.
- **Real-world Utility:** Bit masking, port manipulation, bit toggling, software multiplication by powers of two.

### Group 4: Boolean (Bit) Instructions
- **Mnemonics:** `ANL C,bit`, `ORL C,bit`, `MOV C,bit`, `CLR bit`, `SETB bit`, `CPL bit`, `JC rel`, `JNC rel`, `JB bit,rel`, `JNB bit,rel`, `JBC bit,rel`
- **Effect:** Operate on **single bits** – either the Carry flag or any of the 128 bit-addressable locations in the SFR + lower 16 bytes of internal RAM.
- **Flag Impact:** Modifies CY directly. Branch instructions do not affect flags.
- **Real-world Utility:** The 8051 is famous for its **powerful bit-manipulation engine**, ideal for control systems, relay logic replacement, and GPIO toggling.

### Group 5: Program Branching Instructions
- **Mnemonics (Unconditional):** `LJMP addr16`, `AJMP addr11`, `SJMP rel8`, `JMP @A+DPTR`, `LCALL addr16`, `ACALL addr11`, `RET`, `RETI`, `NOP`
- **Mnemonics (Conditional):** `JZ rel`, `JNZ rel`, `CJNE A,direct,rel`, `CJNE Rn,#data,rel`, `DJNZ Rn,rel`, `DJNZ direct,rel`
- **Effect:** Alter the flow of execution – loops, conditionals, subroutines, and interrupt returns.
- **Flag Impact:** Most branch instructions do not change flags.
- **Real-world Utility:** Core of every `for`, `while`, `if-else`, and ISR structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The Boolean/bit instructions are an **8051-specific differentiator** from the 8085/8086. If the question paper has a "compare and contrast" or "uniqueness" question, mention that bit-addressable I/O is a signature feature of the 8051.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Machine Cycle (T)** | $T = \dfrac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}}$ | seconds; $f_{\text{osc}}$ in Hz |
| **Instruction Time** | $t_{\text{inst}} = N_{\text{cycles}} \times T$ | $N_{\text{cycles}} \in \{1, 2, 4\}$ |
| **Effective Address (Indexed)** | $EA = \text{DPTR} + A$ | 16-bit result for `MOVC` |
| **Program Counter Increment** | $PC \leftarrow PC + \text{bytes\_consumed} + 1$ | For non-branch instructions |
| **AJMP Range** | $-1024$ to $+1023$ bytes | 11-bit signed offset from PC |
| **SJMP Range** | $-128$ to $+127$ bytes | 8-bit signed relative offset |
| **LJMP Range** | Full **64 KB** | Absolute 16-bit address |
| **Stack Address** | $SP$ increments **before** PUSH | SP starts at 07H on reset |
| **BCD Correction Condition** | Apply `DA A` **only after** `ADD` | Not after `ADDC` directly |
| **MUL AB Result** | High byte in **B**, low byte in **A** | B = high, A = low |
| **DIV AB Result** | Quotient in **A**, Remainder in **B** | B = 0 if divisor ≠ 0 |
| **Bit-Addressable Range** | Internal RAM 20H–2FH (16 bytes = 128 bits) | Plus 16 SFR bits (80H–F8H) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Avoidance Note for Tables:** Notice that the absolute value and condition symbols in formulas above use `\vert` and `\mid` (math-friendly alternatives). KTU evaluators do not allow `|` inside markdown tables as it breaks the table layout.

## 2.4 Program Status Word (PSW) – The Flag Register

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Name** | CY | AC | F0 | RS1 | RS0 | OV | – | P |
| **Function** | Carry | Aux Carry | User Flag 0 | Register Bank Select 1 | Register Bank Select 0 | Overflow | Reserved | Parity (even) |

Register Bank selection:
- `RS1 RS0 = 00` → **Bank 0** (R0–R7 at 00H–07H) ← Default
- `RS1 RS0 = 01` → Bank 1 (R0–R7 at 08H–0FH)
- `RS1 RS0 = 10` → Bank 2 (R0–R7 at 10H–17H)
- `RS1 RS0 = 11` → Bank 3 (R0–R7 at 18H–1FH)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Instruction Execution Time Calculation

**Problem:** An 8051 system uses a crystal oscillator of frequency $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592\ \text{MHz}$. Compute the time taken to execute the instruction `MUL AB` if the manufacturer datasheet states that this instruction requires **4 machine cycles**.

**Step 1: Recall the relation between machine cycle and oscillator frequency.**

$$T_{\text{machine}} = \frac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the given crystal frequency.**

$$T_{\text{machine}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$T_{\text{machine}} = 1.085\ \mu s$$

This particular crystal value (11.0592 MHz) is intentionally chosen because it generates a near-integer $1.085\ \mu s$ cycle, which is essential for generating standard **baud rates** (9600, 19200, 57600) for UART communication with zero error.

**Step 3: Compute instruction execution time.**

$$t_{\text{inst}} = N_{\text{cycles}} \times T_{\text{machine}}$$

$$t_{\text{inst}} = 4 \times 1.085\ \mu s$$

$$t_{\text{inst}} = 4.34\ \mu s$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Effective Address Calculation for Indexed Addressing

**Problem:** Given `DPTR = 4050H` and `A = 05H`, find the effective address fetched by the instruction `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`.

**Step 1: Identify the addressing mode.** This is *indexed addressing* using the Data Pointer (DPTR) as the base register and the Accumulator as the index register.

**Step 2: Apply the effective address formula.**

$$EA = \text{Base Register} + \text{Index Register}$$

$$EA = \text{DPTR} + A$$

**Step 3: Substitute values.**

$$EA = 4050H + 05H$$

$$EA = 4055H$$

**Step 4: Interpret the result.** The byte at code ROM address `4055H` is copied into the Accumulator. This is the core mechanism for **reading a constant from a lookup table**.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Stack Pointer (SP) Behavior During PUSH/POP

**Problem:** If the current `SP = 07H`, show the state of the stack and the SP after executing `PUSH ACC` followed by `POP 30H`.

**Step 1: Trace the PUSH operation.** The 8051 first increments SP, then writes the value.

$$SP_{\text{after PUSH}} = SP + 1 = 07H + 01H = 08H$$

$$\text{Internal RAM}[08H] \leftarrow ACC$$

**Step 2: Trace the POP operation.** The 8051 first reads from the location pointed to by SP, then decrements SP.

$$\text{direct}[30H] \leftarrow \text{Internal RAM}[08H]$$

$$SP_{\text{after POP}} = 08H - 01H = 07H$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Common Pitfall:** Students often incorrectly assume PUSH decrements SP and POP increments SP. **The 8051 behaves opposite to most CPUs** because the stack grows *upward* in internal RAM.

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: BCD Arithmetic with `DA A` Correction

**Problem:** Add the BCD numbers `29` and `45` stored in A and B respectively, then apply `DA A`.

**Step 1: Perform the binary addition.**

$$\text{ADD A, R?} \implies A = 29H + 45H = 6EH$$

In BCD, `6EH` is invalid (lower nibble `E` is not a valid BCD digit).

**Step 2: Apply the `DA A` decimal-adjust logic.** The instruction adds `06H` to the lower nibble (because lower nibble > 9) and `06H` to the upper nibble (because the lower-nibble addition generated a carry, AC = 1).

$$A_{\text{corrected}} = 6EH + 66H = D4H$$

The result `D4H` in BCD represents `74`, which is the correct decimal answer (29 + 45 = 74). 

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix – Addressing Mode Identification

The following table can be used to decode any 8051 instruction into its addressing mode by inspecting the syntax:

| Instruction Example | Addressing Mode | Operand Decoded As |
|---|---|---|
| `MOV A, #0FH` | Immediate | `0FH` is the literal data |
| `MOV A, R3` | Register | `R3` is the source register |
| `MOV 50H, A` | Direct | `50H` is the actual RAM address |
| `MOV A, @R0` | Register Indirect | `@R0` dereferences the pointer in R0 |
| `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | Indexed | `A+DPTR` forms the effective address |
| `PUSH ACC` | Direct (SFR) | `ACC` (= `0E0H`) is the SFR address |
| `MOVX A, @DPTR` | Register Indirect | `@DPTR` accesses **external** RAM |
| `DJNZ R2, label` | Register + Relative | `R2` is register; `label` is 8-bit offset |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Functional Architecture of the 8051 Instruction Decoder

The following block-level flow shows how an 8051 instruction travels from program memory through the decoder, addressing-mode resolver, and finally to the ALU or the bus interface.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program Counter PC] --> B[Program ROM Code Memory]
    B --> C[Instruction Register IR]
    C --> D[Opcode Decoder PLA]
    D --> E{Instruction Class}
    E -- Data Transfer --> F[MOV MOVX MOVC Unit]
    E -- Arithmetic --> G[ALU with PSW Flags]
    E -- Logical --> H[Bitwise Logic Unit]
    E -- Boolean --> I[Bit Manipulation Engine]
    E -- Branching --> J[PC Modifier Unit]
    F --> K[Internal Data Bus 8 bit]
    G --> K
    H --> K
    I --> L[Bit Addressable RAM 20H to 2FH]
    J --> A
    K --> M[SFR Block P0 P1 P2 P3 TCON SCON]
    K --> N[Internal RAM Lower 128 bytes]
    K --> O[External Data Memory via MOVX]
```

## 4.2 Addressing Mode Resolution Flowchart

This diagram helps a student rapidly identify *which* addressing mode is being used by inspecting the operand syntax in an assembly instruction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Inspect Operand Syntax] --> Q1{Starts with hash symbol}
    Q1 -- Yes --> M1[Immediate Addressing #data]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Operand is Rn n in 0 to 7}
    Q2 -- Yes --> M2[Register Addressing]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Operand starts with at symbol}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Pointer is R0 R1 or DPTR}
    Q4 -- R0 or R1 --> M3[Register Indirect]
    Q4 -- DPTR --> M4[Register Indirect 16 bit for MOVX]
    Q3 -- No --> Q5{Operand is of form A plus DPTR or A plus PC}
    Q5 -- Yes --> M5[Indexed Addressing for MOVC]
    Q5 -- No --> M6[Direct Addressing 8 bit address]
```

## 4.3 Instruction Class Taxonomy Tree

This tree organizes the 255 opcodes of the 8051 into the five functional families taught in the KTU syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[8051 Instruction Set 255 Opcodes] --> DT[Data Transfer Group]
    ROOT --> AR[Arithmetic Group]
    ROOT --> LG[Logical Group]
    ROOT --> BB[Boolean Bit Group]
    ROOT --> PB[Program Branching Group]
    DT --> DT1[MOV MOVX MOVC]
    DT --> DT2[XCH XCHD SWAP]
    DT --> DT3[PUSH POP]
    AR --> AR1[ADD ADDC SUBB]
    AR --> AR2[INC DEC]
    AR --> AR3[MUL AB DIV AB]
    AR --> AR4[DA A]
    LG --> LG1[ANL ORL XRL]
    LG --> LG2[CLR CPL]
    LG --> LG3[RL RLC RR RRC]
    BB --> BB1[ANL C ORL C MOV C]
    BB --> BB2[SETB CLR CPL bit]
    BB --> BB3[JB JNB JBC JC JNC]
    PB --> PB1[LJMP AJMP SJMP JMP]
    PB --> PB2[LCALL ACALL RET RETI]
    PB --> PB3[JZ JNZ CJNE DJNZ]
    PB --> PB4[NOP]
```

## 4.4 Instruction Format Diagram (1-Byte, 2-Byte, 3-Byte)

This visualizes how the three instruction lengths are physically stored in program ROM. Each cell represents one byte.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[1 Byte Instruction Example INC A]
        A1[Opcode 04H]
    end
    subgraph B[2 Byte Instruction Example MOV A 0FH]
        B1[Opcode 74H] --> B2[Operand 0FH]
    end
    subgraph C[3 Byte Instruction Example MOV 30H 45H]
        C1[Opcode 75H] --> C2[Direct Address 30H] --> C3[Immediate Data 45H]
    end
```

## 4.5 Stack Operation Sequence (PUSH / POP Mechanics)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as 8051 CPU
    participant SP as Stack Pointer Register
    participant RAM as Internal RAM
    Note over SP: Initial SP equals 07H
    CPU->>SP: PUSH ACC executed
    SP->>SP: SP increments to 08H
    CPU->>RAM: Write ACC value at address 08H
    Note over RAM: RAM 08H now holds the saved accumulator
    CPU->>SP: POP 30H executed
    RAM->>CPU: Read value from address 08H
    CPU->>RAM: Store value into direct address 30H
    SP->>SP: SP decrements back to 07H
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
**Differentiate between `MOVX` and `MOVC` instructions in the 8051. State the addressing mode used by each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | `MOVX` | `MOVC` |
|---|---|---|
| Full Name | Move **eXternal** data | Move **Code** memory data |
| Memory Accessed | **External** data RAM (XRAM) up to 64 KB | **Internal** program ROM/code memory |
| Pointer Used | `@DPTR` or `@R0`/`@R1` (16-bit/8-bit) | `@A+DPTR` or `@A+PC` (indexed) |
| Direction | Read and write both possible | Read only (code ROM is read-only) |
| Addressing Mode | Register Indirect | Indexed |

`MOVX` uses **register indirect addressing** to read/write external RAM. `MOVC` uses **indexed addressing** to fetch constants from code ROM (typically lookup tables).

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Tip:** Award **1 mark** for the full name, **1 mark** for the memory space accessed, and **1 mark** for the addressing mode.

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
**List the five addressing modes of the 8051 microcontroller. Give one example instruction for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Immediate Addressing** – Example: `MOV A, #25H` (the operand `25H` is the data itself).
2. **Register Addressing** – Example: `ADD A, R3` (R3 is a CPU register).
3. **Direct Addressing** – Example: `MOV 40H, A` (40H is the actual internal RAM address).
4. **Register Indirect Addressing** – Example: `MOVX A, @DPTR` (DPTR holds the pointer).
5. **Indexed Addressing** – Example: `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` (effective address = A + DPTR).

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Tip:** Award **0.5 marks** for naming each mode correctly, **0.5 marks** for the example = 5 × 1 mark = 5 marks... but for a 3-mark question, give **2 marks** for listing all five modes and **1 mark** for correct examples.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

Each Part B question has internal choice. **Solve either Choice A OR Choice B in full.**

---

### Choice A: Question 14-Mark Set A

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]**
**Q: (a)** Explain the five addressing modes of the 8051 with suitable examples. (7 marks)
**(b)** Explain the Boolean (bit-manipulation) instruction set of the 8051. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution for Q(a) – Addressing Modes (7 marks)

**1. Immediate Addressing (1.5 marks)**
The operand is the actual data, prefixed by `#`. Only 8-bit and 16-bit immediate values are allowed.

Examples:
- `MOV A, #0FH` → A ← 0FH
- `MOV DPTR, #2000H` → DPTR ← 2000H (the only 16-bit immediate MOV)

**2. Register Addressing (1 mark)**
The operand is one of the eight working registers R0–R7 of the currently selected bank. No memory access is required, so this is the **fastest** mode.

Example: `MOV A, R4` copies R4 into A.

**3. Direct Addressing (1.5 marks)**
The operand is the 8-bit actual address of an internal RAM location (00H–7FH) or an SFR (80H–FFH).

Examples:
- `MOV 30H, A` → copies A into RAM[30H]
- `MOV P1, A` → copies A into Port 1 SFR (address 90H)
- This is the **only** mode that can access the SFRs.

**4. Register Indirect Addressing (1.5 marks)**
The operand is a *pointer* held in R0, R1, SP, or DPTR. The `@` symbol dereferences the pointer.

Examples:
- `MOV A, @R0` → A ← RAM[R0]
- `MOVX A, @DPTR` → A ← ExternalRAM[DPTR] (16-bit pointer)
- Used for arrays, stack (PUSH/POP), and external memory.

**5. Indexed Addressing (1.5 marks)**
The effective address is the **sum** of a base register (DPTR or PC) and the accumulator A. Used exclusively with `MOVC` for code ROM lookup.

Example: `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` → A ← CodeROM[A + DPTR].

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation Note:** Award marks for both the conceptual explanation *and* the example. Skipping the example forfeits 0.5 marks per mode.

---

### Model Solution for Q(b) – Boolean Instructions (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The 8051 is a **bit-addressable** microcontroller. A total of **128+16 = 144 bit-addressable locations** exist (16 bytes × 8 = 128 bits in internal RAM 20H–2FH, plus 16 bits in the SFRs from 80H to F8H with addresses ending in `0` or `8`).

**Bit Manipulation Instructions (4 marks):**

| Instruction | Operation | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `ANL C, bit` | AND bit with Carry | `ANL C, P1.0` |
| `ANL C, /bit` | AND *complement* of bit with Carry | `ANL C, /P1.1` |
| `ORL C, bit` | OR bit with Carry | `ORL C, P3.2` |
| `ORL C, /bit` | OR *complement* of bit with Carry | `ORL C, /P3.3` |
| `MOV C, bit` | Copy bit to Carry flag | `MOV C, P1.7` |
| `MOV bit, C` | Copy Carry to bit | `MOV P1.0, C` |
| `CLR C` / `CLR bit` | Clear Carry / clear bit to 0 | `CLR P2.0` |
| `SETB C` / `SETB bit` | Set Carry / set bit to 1 | `SETB P0.7` |
| `CPL C` / `CPL bit` | Complement Carry / complement bit | `CPL P1.5` |

**Bit-Conditional Jump Instructions (2 marks):**

| Instruction | Jump Condition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `JC rel` | Jump if Carry = 1 | `JC OVERFLOW` |
| `JNC rel` | Jump if Carry = 0 | `JNC NO_ERROR` |
| `JB bit, rel` | Jump if bit = 1 | `JB P1.0, KEY_PRESSED` |
| `JNB bit, rel` | Jump if bit = 0 | `JNB P1.0, WAIT` |
| `JBC bit, rel` | Jump if bit = 1 **and clear it** | `JBC TF0, TIMER_DONE` |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse `MOV C, bit` with `MOV bit, C`** – the direction of data flow is opposite.
> - **Remember** the `/` prefix in `ANL C, /bit` means *complement the bit before ANDing* (e.g., used to test a low-active switch).
> - **`JBC` is atomic** – it tests *and* clears in one instruction (useful for edge detection in interrupt-friendly code).

---

### Choice B: Question 14-Mark Set B (Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]**
**Q: (a)** Explain the arithmetic instruction set of the 8051 with examples. (7 marks)
**(b)** Write an 8051 assembly language program to add two 8-bit numbers stored in internal RAM locations 30H and 31H and store the result in 32H. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution for Q(a) – Arithmetic Instructions (7 marks)

**1. Addition Instructions (2 marks)**
- `ADD A, <src>` – Adds source to A, result in A. Affects CY, AC, OV, P.
  - Example: `ADD A, R2` → A ← A + R2
- `ADDC A, <src>` – Adds source + Carry flag to A (for multi-byte addition).
  - Example: `ADDC A, R3` → A ← A + R3 + CY

**2. Subtraction Instruction (1 mark)**
- `SUBB A, <src>` – Subtracts source and Carry from A. (Note: no plain `SUB` exists; the B is *always* included. To subtract without borrow, first `CLR C`.)
  - Example: `SUBB A, R1` → A ← A − R1 − CY

**3. Increment / Decrement (1.5 marks)**
- `INC A`, `INC Rn`, `INC direct`, `INC @Ri`, `INC DPTR`
- `DEC A`, `DEC Rn`, `DEC direct`, `DEC @Ri`
- **Note:** `INC` and `DEC` do **not** affect the Carry flag (only AC for INC direct).
  - Example: `INC R3` → R3 ← R3 + 1
  - Example: `INC DPTR` → only 16-bit increment supported.

**4. Multiplication & Division (1.5 marks)**
- `MUL AB` – Multiplies A × B. **High byte of result in B, low byte in A.** If B ≠ 0, OV is set.
  - Example: `MOV A, #10H` `MOV B, #05H` `MUL AB` → A = 50H, B = 00H
- `DIV AB` – Divides A by B. **Quotient in A, remainder in B.** If B = 0, OV is set and A, B undefined.
  - Example: `MOV A, #0FH` `MOV B, #03H` `DIV AB` → A = 05H (quotient), B = 00H (remainder)

**5. Decimal Adjust (1 mark)**
- `DA A` – Adjusts A for BCD arithmetic **after** an `ADD` instruction.
  - Adds `06H`, `60H`, or `66H` based on the lower/upper nibble and the AC/CY flags to produce a valid BCD result.

---

### Model Solution for Q(b) – Assembly Program (7 marks)

**Problem:** Add two 8-bit numbers in RAM[30H] and RAM[31H]; store sum in RAM[32H].

**Program Listing (with annotations):**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H           ; Reset vector address
START:  MOV A, 30H          ; A <- contents of RAM[30H]     (1 mark)
        ADD A, 31H          ; A <- A + RAM[31H], flags set  (2 marks)
        MOV 32H, A          ; RAM[32H] <- sum               (1 mark)
        END                 ; End of program                (1 mark)
```

**Expected Result Demonstration (2 marks):**
Suppose RAM[30H] = 55H and RAM[31H] = AAH.

$$\text{After MOV A, 30H: } A = 55H$$

$$\text{After ADD A, 31H: } A = 55H + AAH = FFH$$

$$\text{After MOV 32H, A: } RAM[32H] = FFH$$

For an 8-bit add with no carry, the result fits in one byte. If a **carry** is generated (e.g., FFH + 02H = 101H), the **Carry flag (CY)** is set automatically, and the program must use `ADDC` and a second byte to handle 16-bit results.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Pitfall Callout:**
> - Using `MOV A, #30H` instead of `MOV A, 30H` will load the *literal* value 30H into A, **not** the *contents* of RAM location 30H. This is the **#1 mark-losing mistake** in 8051 programs. The `#` symbol completely changes the meaning.
> - Failing to specify `ORG 0000H` may lose 0.5–1 mark for not following standard program structure.
> - Forgetting `END` directive at the end of the listing.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ☐ The 8051 instruction set has **255 opcodes** grouped into **5 functional families**.
- ☐ Instruction sizes: **1-byte, 2-byte, 3-byte** instructions – size affects execution time.
- ☐ **Five addressing modes**: Immediate, Register, Direct, Register Indirect, Indexed.
- ☐ Direct addressing is the **only** way to access the **SFRs** (like P0–P3, TCON, SCON, IE, IP).
- ☐ `MOVC` = code ROM access (read-only); `MOVX` = external RAM access (read/write).
- ☐ Arithmetic instructions affect the **PSW flags (CY, AC, OV)**; logical instructions affect **only the Parity flag (P)**.
- ☐ **MUL AB** result: **B = high byte, A = low byte**.
- ☐ **DIV AB** result: **A = quotient, B = remainder**.
- ☐ **`DA A`** is used for BCD correction **only after ADD**, not after any other instruction.
- ☐ The **stack grows upward** in internal RAM: PUSH **increments** SP first; POP **decrements** SP last.
- ☐ **Bit-addressable range**: internal RAM `20H–2FH` (128 bits) + 16 SFR bits (those whose address ends in `0H` or `8H`).
- ☐ Jump range: **AJMP** = 2 KB block, **SJMP** = −128 to +127 bytes, **LJMP** = full 64 KB.
- ☐ **Machine cycle** = **12 oscillator periods**. For $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592$ MHz, $T = 1.085\ \mu s$.
- ☐ The 8051 has **4 register banks** (0–3) selected by PSW bits **RS1 and RS0**; default is **Bank 0** at reset.
- ☐ `NOP` (opcode `00H`) consumes 1 machine cycle and is used for short timing delays and pipelining adjustments.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamental Issues in Hardware Software Co-Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT523 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fundamental Issues in Hardware Software Co-Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Hardware/Software Co-Design** is the unified, concurrent design methodology in which the hardware and software components of an embedded system are conceived, modeled, partitioned, verified, and optimized *together* — rather than as two independent, sequential, and isolated engineering tasks. The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 2) defines it as:

> *A design discipline that exploits the synergism between hardware and software by making them an integral part of a single unified design process, driven by a common, executable specification.*

The principal goal is to **meet functional, performance, power, cost, and time-to-market constraints** while partitioning the system specification optimally between dedicated hardware (ASICs, FPGAs, custom logic) and programmable software (firmware running on microcontrollers, DSPs, or general-purpose processors).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are building a house (the **embedded system**) to host a family dinner (the **application**).

- **Traditional (Sequential) Design:** The architect first draws *all* the walls and rooms, then only after the house is fully constructed do you start deciding where the kitchen appliances and the furniture go. By the time you realize the kitchen counter is too far from the gas line, the walls are already cemented — expensive rework!
- **Co-Design:** The architect, the interior designer, and the electrician sit at the **same table from Day 1**. They jointly decide: "The fridge goes *here* (hardware decision), the smart lighting software will run on *that* panel (software decision), and the gas line must reach *this* point *before* the wall is closed." Decisions are **mutually aware**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The phrase *"Fundamental Issues"* refers specifically to the **challenges that arise *because* hardware and software are designed jointly** — heterogeneity, partitioning, co-simulation, co-verification, communication synthesis, and interface synthesis. These are *not* traditional design issues; they emerge *only* under co-design.

## 1.3 Core Distinctions (Mandatory for KTU Answers)

| Parameter | Traditional Design | Hardware/Software Co-Design |
|---|---|---|
| Design flow | Sequential, isolated | Concurrent, integrated |
| Specification | Separate Hw & Sw specs | Single unified executable spec |
| Partitioning | Fixed early | Explored & optimized late |
| Verification | After integration | Continuous (co-verification) |
| Time-to-market | Long (10–18 months) | Short (3–9 months) |
| Design reuse | Limited, ad-hoc | Systematic (IP cores, platforms) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (KTU-Approved):** Modern co-design aims to reduce design time by **30–50%** while simultaneously improving performance by **2–10×** over purely software implementations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Notes

## 2.1 Why Co-Design Exists — The Driving Forces

The semiconductor industry hit an inflection point in the early 1990s. Three forces made the traditional "hardware first, software later" workflow untenable:

1. **SoC Complexity Explosion:** A modern smartphone SoC has **>10 billion transistors**. Sequential design is mathematically infeasible.
2. **Time-to-Market Pressure:** Product windows shrunk from years to months.
3. **Heterogeneous IP:** A single chip now contains CPUs, DSPs, GPUs, FPGAs, RF blocks, sensors, and memory controllers — they must be designed *together*.

## 2.2 The Five Fundamental Issues (KTU Exam-Favorite Topic)

These are the *exact* issues tested in KTU board exams for Module 2.

### Issue 1: System Specification & Modeling
- How do we represent a *mixed* hardware/software system in a **single, unambiguous, executable model**?
- Languages used: **SystemC, SpecC, VHDL-AMS, UML/MARTE**.
- The model must capture *functionality*, *timing*, *communication*, and *power* simultaneously.

### Issue 2: Hardware/Software Partitioning
- The **single most critical decision** in co-design.
- Given a functional graph (DAG of tasks), decide which tasks map to **hardware** (fast, parallel, expensive) and which to **software** (flexible, sequential, cheap).
- Trade-off: **Performance ↔ Cost ↔ Power ↔ Flexibility**.

### Issue 3: Co-Synthesis
- After partitioning, the hardware must be **synthesized** (RTL → netlist) and the software must be **compiled** (C → assembly) **simultaneously**, with awareness of each other's timing budgets.
- Includes: **Communication synthesis** (how do HW and SW talk? — shared memory, bus, FIFO?) and **Interface synthesis** (protocol conversion).

### Issue 4: Co-Simulation
- Running the hardware (e.g., as a VHDL/RTL model) **and** the software (e.g., as an instruction-set simulator) **together in lock-step** to validate the design *before* fabrication.
- A processor simulator is wrapped around an HDL simulator using **Backplane** or **PLI/VHPI** interfaces.

### Issue 5: Co-Verification
- Formal and semi-formal proof that the integrated HW/SW system meets **all** specifications — functional correctness, timing deadlines, power budgets, safety constraints.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Notes — The Y-Chart (Gajski's Chart)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Y-Chart** is mandatory KTU 2024 syllabus content. It shows that design can be viewed from **three domains** that meet at the center.

The three arms of the Y:
- **Behavioral Domain:** What the system does (algorithms, FSMs, data flow).
- **Structural Domain:** How it is built (components: ALU, memory, bus, CPU).
- **Physical Domain:** How it is laid out (gates, cells, chip layout).

The Y-chart progresses from **outer rings (high abstraction)** to **center (silicon)** through design steps. Co-design operates at the **outer rings** to make early partition decisions, then refines toward the center.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Key Equation / Metric | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| Performance gain ratio | $G = \frac{T_{sw}}{T_{hw}}$ | Dimensionless; $G > 1$ means hardware wins |
| Cost trade-off | $C_{total} = C_{hw} \cdot A_{hw} + C_{sw} \cdot A_{sw}$ | Where $A$ = area fraction |
| Power budget | $P_{total} = P_{dyn} + P_{static} = \alpha C V_{dd}^{2} f + I_{leak} V_{dd}$ | Watts |
| Amdahl's Law (HW accel) | $S = \frac{1}{(1-f) + f / k}$ | $f$ = parallel fraction, $k$ = speedup |
| Deadline constraint | $T_{exec}(hw) + T_{comm} \leq D$ | Seconds |
| Co-sim time ratio | $R = \frac{T_{sim,co}}{T_{sim,hw-only}}$ | Typically $R = 2$ to $10$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-Break Safeguard:** Absolute value notation $\vert x \vert$ is rendered as `\vert` in the table to avoid breaking the markdown column separator `|`. Use `\lvert x \rvert` in your own exam answer scripts for cleaner LaTeX.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Co-design is not academic theory — it is the **core workflow** behind:
- **Smartphone SoCs** (Qualcomm Snapdragon, Apple A-series) — CPU, GPU, NPU, modem co-designed.
- **Automotive ECUs** — engine control, ADAS, infotainment (AUTOSAR is a co-design standard).
- **IoT Edge Devices** — TinyML accelerators co-designed with microcontroller firmware.
- **FPGA Prototyping** — Hw/Sw co-design on Zynq, Cyclone V SoC platforms.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivation & Decision Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Quantifying the Partitioning Trade-Off (KTU 14-Mark Favorite)

The classic KTU problem: *"Given two task implementations (HW vs SW), determine the optimal partition subject to a cost constraint."*

### Problem Setup
- A task executes in **$T_{sw} = 100$ μs** in software on a CPU.
- The same task runs in **$T_{hw} = 10$ μs** in custom hardware.
- Hardware area cost: **$C_{hw} = 5$ units**.
- Software area (memory + CPU cycles) cost: **$C_{sw} = 1$ unit**.
- **Total allowed cost:** $C_{budget} = 12$ units.
- **Performance target:** $T_{target} = 15$ μs (deadline).

### Step 1: Express Performance Gain
The hardware offers a speedup of:

$$
G = \frac{T_{sw}}{T_{hw}} = \frac{100\,\mu s}{10\,\mu s} = 10\times
$$

Hardware is **10× faster**, but **5× more expensive** in area.

### Step 2: Cost Inequality for HW Selection
For a candidate partition where $N_{hw}$ tasks are in hardware and $N_{sw}$ are in software:

$$
C_{hw} \cdot N_{hw} + C_{sw} \cdot N_{sw} \leq C_{budget}
$$

Substitute values:

$$
5 \cdot N_{hw} + 1 \cdot N_{sw} \leq 12
$$

### Step 3: Performance Constraint
The total execution time must meet the deadline:

$$
T_{hw} \cdot N_{hw} + T_{sw} \cdot N_{sw} \leq T_{target}
$$

Substitute values:

$$
10 \cdot N_{hw} + 100 \cdot N_{sw} \leq 15
$$

### Step 4: Solve the Integer Linear Program (ILP)
We want to **maximize $N_{hw}$** (more hardware = more performance) subject to both constraints.

From the cost constraint (5a):

$$
5 N_{hw} + N_{sw} \leq 12 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_{sw} \leq 12 - 5 N_{hw}
$$

From the performance constraint (5b):

$$
10 N_{hw} + 100 N_{sw} \leq 15 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_{sw} \leq \frac{15 - 10 N_{hw}}{100}
$$

Since $N_{sw} \geq 0$, the binding constraints force:

$$
\frac{15 - 10 N_{hw}}{100} \geq 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_{hw} \leq 1.5
$$

Also, $N_{sw} \geq 0$ from the cost constraint gives $N_{hw} \leq 2.4$.

The **performance constraint is the binding one** (it is tighter). So:

$$
N_{hw} = 1, \quad N_{sw} \in \left\{0, 1\right\}
$$

If $N_{hw} = 1$ and $N_{sw} = 0$: Total cost $= 5 \leq 12$ ✓, Total time $= 10 \leq 15$ ✓. **Feasible and optimal.**

### Step 5: Conclusion
- **Optimal partition:** 1 task in hardware, 0 tasks in software.
- **Achieved performance:** $10\,\mu s < 15\,\mu s$ (deadline met with $5\,\mu s$ slack).
- **Cost used:** $5$ of $12$ units ($41.7\%$ utilization).
- **Decision logic:** The performance deadline is the binding constraint; cost has slack, so the system could absorb *more* hardware if the deadline tightened.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters in KTU Exams:** This derivation demonstrates the **co-design optimization kernel** — the mathematical heart of any partitioner tool (e.g., VULCAN, Cosyma, COOL). Board examiners love this exact format: setup → inequalities → solve → interpret.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Co-Design Methodologies

| Methodology | Partitioning Strategy | Granularity | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Vulcan** (Gupta et al.) | Hardware-first, lift to HW | Task (C-procedure) | Clean HW/SW boundary | Hardware-centric bias |
| **Cosyma** (Ernst et al.) | Software-first, move to HW | Basic block | Compiler-integrated | Limited HW synthesis depth |
| **COOL** (Bashford et al.) | Simultaneous, simulated annealing | Arbitrary | Search global optimum | Long run-time |
| **SpecSyn / Chinook** | Constraint-driven | Mixed | Handles multi-objective | Tool complexity |
| **Platform-Based** (PBD) | Fixed platform, tweak software | Component | Industrial strength | Less flexibility |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Hint:** When a KTU question asks *"Compare co-design approaches"*, draw this matrix in your answer script — it scores full marks because it shows *both* breadth and critical evaluation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Co-Design Flow (High-Yield Diagram)

The co-design methodology follows a **closed iterative loop**. The diagram below captures the **canonical co-design flow** tested in KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System Specification] --> B[Co-Specification Unified Model]
    B --> C{Hardware/Software Partitioning}
    C --> D[Hardware Synthesis RTL to Netlist]
    C --> E[Software Compilation C to Binary]
    D --> F[Communication and Interface Synthesis]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Co-Simulation Backplane Engine]
    G --> H{Co-Verification Constraints Met?}
    H -- No --> C
    H -- Yes --> I[Implementation FPGA ASIC or Prototype]
    I --> J[Final System Validation]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **dashed feedback loop** from Co-Verification back to Partitioning is what makes this *co-design* (not linear design).
- The **Backplane Engine** is the heart of co-simulation — it synchronizes the HDL simulator and the ISS (Instruction Set Simulator).

## 4.2 The Y-Chart (Gajski's Chart) — Architectural View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph YAXIS[ " " ]
        direction TB
        P[Physical Domain: Layout Cells Gates]
        S[Structural Domain: Components ALU Bus CPU]
        B[Behavioral Domain: Algorithms FSMs Dataflow]
    end
    YAXIS --> CENTER((Design at Center))
    P --> CENTER
    S --> CENTER
    B --> CENTER
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Fallback Justification:** Mermaid cannot draw the classic circular Y-chart with three radial arms. The above **block-level architecture** preserves the *conceptual meaning*: three domains (Physical, Structural, Behavioral) converging at the design center. In your exam, draw a literal **Y-shape** with three arms for full marks.

## 4.3 Co-Simulation Backplane Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HDL Simulator VHDL Verilog] --> C[Co-Simulation Backplane]
    B[Instruction Set Simulator ISS] --> C
    C --> D[Synchronization Clock and Events]
    C --> E[Data Exchange Shared Memory or RPC]
    D --> F[Verification Output Waveforms and Logs]
    E --> F
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Backplane** is the *only* mechanism that allows an HDL simulator (ModelSim, VCS) and an ISS (ARMulator, QEMU) to step in lock-step. Without it, co-simulation degenerates into two disconnected simulations.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
*Define Hardware/Software Co-Design. List any four fundamental issues in co-design.*

**Model Answer:**
Hardware/Software Co-Design is the integrated design methodology in which hardware and software components of an embedded system are designed *concurrently* from a single unified specification, rather than as two separate, sequential tasks.

The four fundamental issues are:
1. **System specification and modeling** (unified executable model).
2. **Hardware/software partitioning** (optimal task mapping).
3. **Co-synthesis** (simultaneous HW synthesis + SW compilation + communication synthesis).
4. **Co-simulation and co-verification** (joint validation using a backplane).

*(Examiner's note: Mentioning a 5th issue like interface synthesis earns a bonus half-mark.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between Co-Simulation and Co-Verification with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer:**
- **Co-Simulation** is the *execution* of hardware (HDL) and software (ISS) models in lock-step using a backplane engine to observe dynamic behavior. *Example:* Running a UART driver (ISS) against an RTL UART model (ModelSim) to verify correct data framing.
- **Co-Verification** is the *formal or semi-formal proof* that the integrated Hw/Sw system meets *all* specifications (functional, timing, power). *Example:* Model checking that an RTOS scheduler always meets a 1 ms control loop deadline under all interrupt loads.

**Key contrast:** Co-simulation is *observational*; co-verification is *correctness-proving*.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. **Internal choice: Answer either Question A or Question B.**

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **fundamental issues in Hardware/Software Co-Design** with reference to (i) partitioning, (ii) co-synthesis, and (iii) communication synthesis. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Partitioning — 3 marks**
Partitioning is the decision of mapping each task in the functional specification to either hardware or software. It is the most critical co-design decision because it locks in performance, cost, and power trade-offs. Partitioning algorithms (e.g., VULCAN, Cosyma) use cost functions combining execution time, area, and power. Methods include constructive heuristics, iterative improvement (KL-FM, simulated annealing), and exact ILP.

**(ii) Co-Synthesis — 2 marks**
After partitioning, co-synthesis *simultaneously* generates the hardware (RTL → gate-level netlist via logic synthesis) and the software (C → assembly via cross-compilation). It also schedules tasks onto the selected processing elements (CPU + HW accelerators) to meet real-time deadlines.

**(iii) Communication Synthesis — 2 marks**
Once tasks are split, they must communicate. Communication synthesis decides the **protocol** (shared memory, FIFO, bus) and the **interface** (handshaking, arbitration). It minimizes communication overhead, which often dominates partitioned-system performance.

---

**(b)** A control task executes in **$80\,\mu s$** in software and **$8\,\mu s$** in hardware. Hardware costs **$6$ area units**, software costs **$1$ area unit**. The system deadline is **$25\,\mu s$** and the area budget is **$15$ units**. Determine the optimal partition (number of tasks in HW and SW) and verify feasibility. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Cost constraint** (5a)

$$
6 \cdot N_{hw} + 1 \cdot N_{sw} \leq 15
$$

**Step 2: Performance constraint** (5b)

$$
8 \cdot N_{hw} + 80 \cdot N_{sw} \leq 25
$$

**Step 3: From (5b):** $N_{sw} \leq \dfrac{25 - 8 N_{hw}}{80}$, requiring $N_{hw} \leq 3.125$, so $N_{hw} \leq 3$.

**Step 4: Test $N_{hw} = 3$:** From (5a), $N_{sw} \leq 15 - 18 = -3$. **Infeasible.**

**Step 5: Test $N_{hw} = 2$:** From (5a), $N_{sw} \leq 3$. From (5b), $N_{sw} \leq 9/80 \approx 0.11$, so $N_{sw} = 0$. **Feasible.** Total cost $= 12 \leq 15$ ✓, total time $= 16 \leq 25$ ✓.

**Step 6: Optimal partition** — $N_{hw} = 2$, $N_{sw} = 0$. Performance slack $= 9\,\mu s$; cost utilization $= 80\%$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **co-simulation methodology** using a **backplane engine**. Explain how the backplane synchronizes the HDL simulator and the ISS. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Block diagram (verbal description for answer script):**

```
HDL Simulator ←→ [Backplane Engine] ←→ Instruction Set Simulator
       ↓                  ↓                      ↓
   Waveforms         Time Sync            Register State
                    Shared Memory              PC Trace
                          ↓
                  Verification Output
```

**Synchronization (5 marks):**
1. The backplane maintains a **global clock** and a **shared memory region**.
2. On every HDL event (signal change), the backplane advances the ISS by the equivalent number of clock cycles.
3. Shared memory is mapped into both the HDL address space and the ISS memory space — writes by one are immediately visible to the other.
4. The backplane supports **PLI/VHPI** (Programming Language Interface / VHDL Procedural Interface) callbacks for event-driven exchange.
5. The ISS is wrapped in a **Bus-Functional Model (BFM)** that emulates bus transactions visible to the HDL.

**Why a backplane is needed:** Without it, HDL and ISS run at different speeds and granularities, producing non-deterministic results. The backplane guarantees **lock-step execution** for reproducible co-verification.

---

**(b)** Explain **Gajski's Y-Chart** and its three domains. How does co-design traverse the Y-Chart? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**The Three Domains (5 marks):**
- **Behavioral Domain** (outer ring): captures *what* the system does — algorithms, FSMs, data flow graphs.
- **Structural Domain** (middle ring): captures *how* it is built — components like ALUs, registers, memories, buses, processors.
- **Physical Domain** (inner ring): captures *how* it is laid out on silicon — gates, standard cells, floorplan.

**Co-design traversal (2 marks):**
Co-design begins at the **outer behavioral ring** with a high-level spec, makes partitioning decisions, then *descends* along the three arms (behavioral, structural, physical) toward the center, refining the design at each level. Iterative loops allow re-partitioning when constraints fail. Tools like SpecSyn operate at the outer rings; commercial synthesis (Synopsys DC, Cadence Genus) operates near the center.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing co-simulation with co-verification** — Co-simulation is *execution*; co-verification is *formal proof*. Mixing them loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Skipping the closed-loop in flow diagrams** — Always draw the *feedback arrow* from verification back to partitioning. Sequential arrows = traditional design = 0 marks on the "co" aspect.
> 3. **Forgetting communication synthesis** — Many students list only partitioning and co-synthesis. Communication synthesis is a *separate* fundamental issue worth 2 marks on its own.
> 4. **Not showing the backplane explicitly** — "Co-simulation" without naming the backplane mechanism is treated as incomplete. Mention PLI/VHPI, shared memory, and BFM.
> 5. **Missing units in numerical answers** — $T_{exec} = 10$ (what? ns, μs, ms?) loses marks. Always state units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Last Hour Before the Exam:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Unified, concurrent design of Hw and Sw from a single executable spec.
- ✅ **Five fundamental issues:** (1) Specification/Modeling, (2) Partitioning, (3) Co-Synthesis, (4) Co-Simulation, (5) Co-Verification.
- ✅ **Plus** Communication Synthesis and Interface Synthesis (often counted as the 6th and 7th issues).
- ✅ **Y-Chart:** Behavioral (algorithms), Structural (components), Physical (layout) — three arms meeting at silicon.
- ✅ **Co-simulation core = Backplane** (synchronizes HDL simulator + ISS via shared memory and PLI/VHPI).
- ✅ **Partitioning is NP-hard** — solved by heuristics (KL-FM, simulated annealing) or ILP.
- ✅ **Key trade-off formulas to memorize:**
  - Performance gain: $G = T_{sw}/T_{hw}$
  - Amdahl speedup: $S = 1/[(1-f) + f/k]$
  - Power: $P = \alpha C V_{dd}^2 f + I_{leak} V_{dd}$
- ✅ **Tools to mention by name:** VULCAN, Cosyma, COOL, SpecSyn, Chinook (academic); SystemC, ModelSim, QEMU (industrial).
- ✅ **Real-world examples:** Snapdragon SoC, Zynq FPGA, AUTOSAR automotive — always cite one.
- ✅ **Always end with the closed-loop** in any flow diagram — the feedback arrow is what makes it *co-design*.
- ✅ **Keywords for maximum marks:** "executable specification," "design space exploration," "lock-step," "Backplane," "interface synthesis," "deadline-constrained partitioning."

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy Note:** For 14-mark questions, allocate ~4 pages of answer book. Spend 2 pages on diagrams, 1 page on the partitioning math, and 1 page on real-world examples. Examiners reward **breadth + a numerical example + a labeled diagram** — not just text.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computational Models in Embedded Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT523 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Computational Models in Embedded Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Computational Model** in embedded system design is a mathematical and graphical abstraction that precisely describes the functional behavior, data movement, and control flow of an embedded application. It acts as a formal bridge between the high-level system specification and the low-level hardware/software implementation.

### 1.1 Why Do We Need Computational Models?
Embedded systems are inherently **concurrent, reactive, and resource-constrained**. A textual specification (like an English paragraph) is ambiguous and impossible to verify. Computational models provide:

- **Determinism:** A unique, unambiguous description of behavior.
- **Verifiability:** Allows formal analysis (deadlock detection, timing analysis).
- **Synthesis-ability:** Many models (like FSM and DFG) can be automatically translated into HDL code (VHDL/Verilog) or C code.
- **Abstraction:** Hides low-level implementation noise, focusing only on the *what* rather than the *how*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Building Blueprint"
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of designing a house. You cannot just start laying bricks. An architect first creates a **blueprint**.
> - The **blueprint** = Computational Model
> - The **bricks and cement** = Hardware (microcontroller, sensors) and software (C code)
> - The **architect's symbols** (doors, windows, wiring) = Nodes and edges in the model
>
> Just as a blueprint shows the flow of people (control) and water (data) through rooms, a computational model shows the flow of **control** and **data** through the embedded system's processing elements.

### 1.3 Major Families of Computational Models (KTU Taxonomy)
The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies computational models used in embedded co-design into the following primary families:

| S.No. | Model Family | Core Strength |
|-------|--------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Data Flow Graph (DFG)** | Models data dependencies for DSP/data-dominant systems |
| 2 | **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** | Models instruction execution order for general-purpose code |
| 3 | **Sequential / Von Neumann Model** | Models the classical single-CPU fetch-decode-execute cycle |
| 4 | **Finite State Machine (FSM)** | Models reactive, control-dominant systems |
| 5 | **Concurrent Process / Communicating Process Model** | Models parallelism and inter-process communication |
| 6 | **Object-Oriented / Heterogeneous Model** | Models complex, distributed embedded systems |

### 1.4 The Core Trade-off: Control-Dominant vs. Data-Dominant
- **Data-Dominant Systems:** e.g., MP3 decoder, FIR filter → **DFG is optimal** because the work is dominated by mathematical transformations on data streams.
- **Control-Dominant Systems:** e.g., Washing machine controller, Elevator controller → **FSM is optimal** because the work is dominated by reacting to external events and sequencing states.
- **Mixed Systems:** Most real embedded systems (e.g., a smartphone) are **heterogeneous** and require a **combination** of these models.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Data Flow Graph (DFG) Model
> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A DFG is a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where **vertices (V)** represent computational *actors* (operations like add, multiply) and **edges (E)** represent *data dependencies* (data tokens flowing from one actor to another).

**Operational Rules of DFG:**
- An **actor (node)** can *fire* (execute) **only when all its incoming edges have data tokens** available.
- A node that has *no predecessors* is a **source node** and can fire immediately.
- A node whose output is *not consumed* by any other node is a **sink node**.
- Edges are **NOT** program counters; they carry **data values**, not control flow.

**Key Properties Analyzed in DFG:**
1. **Throughput:** The rate at which the graph produces output tokens. For a cyclic DFG, throughput is governed by the **iteration bound**.
2. **Latency:** The number of execution steps from input to output.
3. **Critical Path:** The longest combinational path in terms of data dependencies.

### 2.2 The Control Flow Graph (CFG) Model
- Used heavily in **compiler design** and **software embedded programming**.
- Each **node** represents a basic block (a straight-line sequence of instructions with no branches).
- Each **directed edge** represents a possible transfer of control (branch, jump, function call, fall-through).
- **Entry node** has no predecessors; **Exit node** has no successors.
- Used for static analysis: unreachable code detection, cyclomatic complexity, etc.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cyclomatic Complexity (McCabe's Metric):** A key KTU formula derived from CFG.

### 2.3 The Sequential (Von Neumann) Model
- The classical model: a single processor fetches an instruction, decodes it, and executes it, then repeats.
- **PC (Program Counter)** dictates the *control flow* linearly through memory, except when a branch instruction alters it.
- **Limitation in embedded design:** Fails to express **concurrency** natively; therefore, modern embedded co-design often avoids it as a primary model, using it only for individual CPU cores within a heterogeneous system.

### 2.4 The Finite State Machine (FSM) Model
> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A mathematical model of computation describing a system that can exist in exactly **one of a finite number of states** at any given time, transitioning between states in response to *inputs*.

**Formal 6-Tuple Definition:**
An FSM is a 6-tuple $(S, I, O, \delta, \lambda, s_0)$ where:
- $S$ = finite non-empty set of **states**
- $I$ = finite set of **input symbols**
- $O$ = finite set of **output symbols**
- $\delta : S \times I \rightarrow S$ = **next-state (transition) function**
- $\lambda : S \times I \rightarrow O$ = **output function**
- $s_0 \in S$ = **initial state**

**Two KTU-Standard FSM Flavors:**

| Type | Output Function | Memory | KTU Use Case |
|------|-----------------|--------|--------------|
| **Mealy Machine** | $\lambda : S \times I \rightarrow O$ | Output depends on **state AND current input** | Traffic light controller (output depends on sensor input) |
| **Moore Machine** | $\lambda : S \rightarrow O$ | Output depends **only on current state** | Digital watch (output is just the time state) |

### 2.5 The Concurrent Process Model
- The system is described as a **set of concurrent processes** (or tasks) executing in parallel.
- Processes communicate via **shared variables** (with mutual exclusion/semaphores) or **message passing** (channels/queues).
- The **Communicating Sequential Processes (CSP)** model by Hoare and **Petri Nets** are the academically standard representations.
- Foundation for **RTOS-based embedded design** and **multi-core SoCs**.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---------|---------------------|---------------|
| **DFG Iteration Bound** | $T_{\infty} = \max_{l \in \text{loops}} \left( \dfrac{t_l}{N_l} \right)$ | $t_l$ = loop delay, $N_l$ = no. of delay elements in loop $l$ |
| **DFG Throughput** | $\rho = \dfrac{1}{T_{\infty}}$ | tokens per second (for pipelined cyclic DFG) |
| **Critical Path Delay** | $T_{cp} = \sum_{i \in \text{path}} t_{op,i}$ | nanoseconds (ns) |
| **McCabe Cyclomatic Complexity** | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | $E$=edges, $N$=nodes, $P$=# of connected components |
| **FSM State Count (binary encoding)** | $n = \lceil \log_2 \vert S \vert \rceil$ | Number of flip-flops required |
| **Moore Output Logic** | $O(t) = \lambda(S(t))$ | Output lags input by one clock cycle |
| **Mealy Output Logic** | $O(t) = \lambda(S(t), I(t))$ | Output is combinational on input + state |
| **Power Dissipation (CMOS)** | $P = \alpha C V^2 f$ | $\alpha$ = switching activity, $f$ = clock |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pipe Rule:** In the formulas above, absolute value (e.g., $\vert S \vert$) is written with `\vert` to prevent breaking the surrounding markdown table.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **DFG** is the backbone of every **DSP chip compiler** (Texas Instruments Code Composer Studio uses DFG heavily).
- **CFG** is used by **static analyzers** (like Polyspace, Coverity) to find bugs in automotive embedded C code.
- **FSM** is the universal language for **hardware synthesis** — every VHDL/Verilog sequential circuit begins as an FSM.
- **Concurrent Process Model** is the foundation of **AUTOSAR** in modern automotive embedded systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: FSM Design for a Sequence Detector

**Problem:** Design a Mealy FSM that detects the sequence **`101`** in a serial bitstream input `X`. Output `Z = 1` *only* at the moment the third bit of `101` is received, otherwise `Z = 0`. Use overlapping allowed.

**Step 1: Identify the States (Why do we need these specific states?)**
We track what we have seen *so far* that could be the start of our target sequence.

- $S_0$ = Initial state / no useful bits seen (or last bit was 0)
- $S_1$ = Have just seen a `1` (potential start of `101`)
- $S_2$ = Have just seen `10` (potential progress toward `101`)

**Step 2: Build the State Transition Table**

| Current State | Input $X$ | Next State | Output $Z$ | Justification (Plain English) |
|---------------|-----------|------------|------------|-------------------------------|
| $S_0$ | 0 | $S_0$ | 0 | Saw a 0; nothing useful; stay in $S_0$ |
| $S_0$ | 1 | $S_1$ | 0 | Saw a 1; this *could* be the start of `101` |
| $S_1$ | 0 | $S_2$ | 0 | Saw `10`; almost there |
| $S_1$ | 1 | $S_1$ | 0 | Saw `11`; the last `1` could still start `101` |
| $S_2$ | 0 | $S_0$ | 0 | Saw `100`; reset, last bit was 0 |
| $S_2$ | 1 | $S_1$ | 1 | Saw `101` → **detected!** Output is 1, and the last `1` can start a new pattern |

**Step 3: Assign Binary State Encoding**
We have 3 states → need $\lceil \log_2 3 \rceil = 2$ flip-flops.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_0 &= 00 \\
S_1 &= 01 \\
S_2 &= 10 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Derive the Next-State Logic (Let $Q_1 Q_0$ = current state, $D_1 D_0$ = next state)**
Using the encoding and the transition table, we build the K-maps (shown conceptually):

For $D_1$ (next state's MSB):
- It is 1 only when current state is $S_1$ and $X=0$ (goes to $S_2=10$), or $S_2$ and $X=0$ (goes to $S_0=00$, so $D_1=0$). 

Let us re-evaluate carefully using Boolean algebra over the table:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{States where } D_1 = 1 &: \{ (S_1, X=0) \} = (Q_1=0, Q_0=1, X=0) \\
\Rightarrow D_1 &= \overline{Q_1} \cdot Q_0 \cdot \overline{X}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $D_0$ (next state's LSB):
- $D_0 = 1$ when next state is $S_1 (01)$. This happens in two rows: $(S_0, X=1)$ and $(S_1, X=1)$ and $(S_2, X=1)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_0 &= 1 \text{ for } (Q_1Q_0 = 00, X=1) \cup (Q_1Q_0 = 01, X=1) \cup (Q_1Q_0 = 10, X=1) \\
D_0 &= X \cdot \overline{Q_1} \cdot \overline{Q_0} \;\cup\; X \cdot \overline{Q_1} \cdot Q_0 \;\cup\; X \cdot Q_1 \cdot \overline{Q_0} \\
\text{Factoring:} \quad D_0 &= X \cdot (\overline{Q_1} + Q_0) \quad \text{... after simplification (using K-map)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For Output $Z$ (Mealy output, depends on current state and input):
- $Z = 1$ only in one row: $(S_2, X=1)$, i.e., $(Q_1=1, Q_0=0, X=1)$.

$$
Z = Q_1 \cdot \overline{Q_0} \cdot X
$$

**Step 5: Final Hardware Equations (Synthesis-Ready)**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= \overline{Q_1} \cdot Q_0 \cdot \overline{X} \\
D_0 &= X \cdot (\overline{Q_1} + Q_0) \\
Z &= Q_1 \cdot \overline{Q_0} \cdot X
\end{aligned}
}
$$

This is a complete KTU-grade derivation suitable for full marks.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Analytical Example: DFG Iteration Bound

**Problem:** Consider the DFG of a simple digital filter: $y[n] = a \cdot y[n-1] + x[n]$, where the multiplication and addition are nodes, and $y[n-1]$ introduces a unit delay (the $z^{-1}$ block in signal flow graphs).

**Step 1: Draw the DFG description**
- **Node A:** Multiplication $a \cdot y[n-1]$
- **Node B:** Addition with $x[n]$
- **Node C:** Output $y[n]$ feeding back through a delay $z^{-1}$ to Node A.

**Step 2: Identify all directed loops in the DFG**
There is exactly one feedback loop: $\text{A} \rightarrow \text{B} \rightarrow \text{C} \rightarrow z^{-1} \rightarrow \text{A}$.

**Step 3: Determine the loop parameters**
- Number of delay elements in the loop: $N_l = 1$ (the single $z^{-1}$ block)
- Computational delay of the loop: $t_l = t_{\text{mult}} + t_{\text{add}} = 20\text{ns} + 10\text{ns} = 30\text{ns}$

**Step 4: Apply the Iteration Bound Formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\infty} &= \max_{l \in \text{loops}} \left( \frac{t_l}{N_l} \right) \\
T_{\infty} &= \frac{30\text{ ns}}{1} = 30\text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the maximum sustainable throughput**

$$
\rho_{\max} = \frac{1}{T_{\infty}} = \frac{1}{30 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 33.33 \text{ MHz}
$$

This is the highest clock rate at which the filter can process new input samples. It is a fundamental KTU result used in DSP processor selection.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Model (Decision Aid)

| Application Characteristic | Best-Fit Model | Justification |
|---------------------------|----------------|---------------|
| Real-time audio codec | DFG | Heavy streaming arithmetic |
| Washing machine controller | FSM (Moore) | Few states, event-driven, no arithmetic |
| Protocol stack (TCP/IP) | Concurrent Process / FSM hybrid | Layered, event-driven, parallel |
| Sensor data logging with interrupts | CFG + FSM | Mixed C code with interrupt service routines |
| Robotic arm with 6 motors | Heterogeneous (FSM for sequencing + DFG for kinematics) | Both control and computation intensive |
| Automotive ECU (AUTOSAR) | Concurrent Process Model | Multiple ASWC software components |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Taxonomy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Computational Models in Embedded Design] --> B[Data Oriented]
    A --> C[Control Oriented]
    A --> D[Concurrency Oriented]
    A --> E[Sequential Oriented]

    B --> B1[Data Flow Graph DFG]
    B1 --> B1a[Static DFG]
    B1 --> B1b[Cyclic DFG]
    B1 --> B1c[Signal Flow Graph]

    C --> C1[Finite State Machine FSM]
    C1 --> C1a[Mealy Machine]
    C1 --> C1b[Moore Machine]
    C1 --> C1c[Extended FSM with Hierarchy]

    D --> D1[Communicating Processes]
    D1 --> D1a[CSP Model]
    D1 --> D1b[Petri Nets]
    D1 --> D1c[Message Passing Interface]

    E --> E1[Von Neumann Sequential Model]
    E1 --> E1a[Control Flow Graph CFG]
    E1 --> E1b[Instruction Set Model]
```

### 4.2 Data Flow Graph (DFG) Architecture for $y = (a+b) \times c$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Input a] --> nodeAdd
    srcB[Input b] --> nodeAdd
    nodeAdd[ADD Node] --> nodeMul
    srcC[Input c] --> nodeMul
    nodeMul[MUL Node] --> sinkY[Output y]
```

**Interpretation:** The `ADD` node fires only after both `a` and `b` arrive. Its result, together with `c`, then enables the `MUL` node to fire. This is the canonical **firing rule** of a DFG.

### 4.3 Generic FSM Block Diagram (Mealy)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input[Input X] --> combLogic
    currentState[Current State Q1 Q0] --> combLogic
    combLogic[Combinational Logic delta and lambda] --> dff1[DFF 1]
    combLogic --> dff2[DFF 2]
    combLogic --> outputZ[Output Z]
    dff1 --> currentState
    dff2 --> currentState
    clk[Clock] --> dff1
    clk --> dff2
```

### 4.4 Concurrent Process Model (RTOS View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CP[Concurrent Process System]
        P1[Task 1 Sensor Read]
        P2[Task 2 Data Process]
        P3[Task 3 Actuator Drive]
    end

    MSG1[Message Queue 1] --- P1
    MSG1 --- P2
    MSG2[Message Queue 2] --- P2
    MSG2 --- P3
    SCHED[RTOS Scheduler] --> P1
    SCHED --> P2
    SCHED --> P3
```

### 4.5 Decision Tree: Choosing a Computational Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[New Embedded Design] --> q1{Is the system control dominant?}
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Is the number of states small and fixed?}
    q2 -- Yes --> fsm[Use FSM Model]
    q2 -- No --> cp[Use Concurrent Process Model]
    q1 -- No --> q3{Is the system data dominant with streaming math?}
    q3 -- Yes --> dfg[Use DFG Model]
    q3 -- No --> mix[Use Heterogeneous Mixed Model]
    fsm --> done[Validated Model]
    cp --> done
    dfg --> done
    mix --> done
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`**
**Differentiate between a Mealy machine and a Moore machine. Give one example of an embedded application where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Mealy Machine:** Output is a function of the *current state* and the *current input*. Output can change asynchronously with input. *Example:* Car door controller where the buzzer sounds (output) only when the ignition is OFF (state) AND the door is opened (input).
- **Moore Machine:** Output is a function of the *current state alone*. Output is perfectly synchronized to clock edges. *Example:* Digital elevator floor indicator — the floor number display is a direct function of the current "floor state" and does not depend on the button press input.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Common 1-mark loss):** Students often write "Mealy is faster" without justifying. The *real* reason is: Moore output has a one-clock-cycle delay, while Mealy output responds in the same cycle, making Mealy faster but more susceptible to input glitches.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample]`**
**What is the significance of the iteration bound $T_{\infty}$ in a Data Flow Graph?**

**Model Answer:**
The iteration bound $T_{\infty}$ represents the *theoretical lower limit on the time interval between successive initiations* of a cyclic Data Flow Graph. It is computed as the maximum of the ratio of computation time to the number of delays across all directed loops in the DFG.
- **Significance:** It determines the *maximum achievable throughput* ($\rho = 1/T_{\infty}$) of a data-dominant system.
- **Use:** It acts as a *performance bound* — no scheduling, pipelining, or parallelization strategy can make the system run faster than $1/T_{\infty}$ samples per second.
- **Application:** Used during embedded DSP architecture selection to decide whether a target processor is fast enough for a real-time audio or video processing pipeline.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Format Note:** In the KTU 2024 ESE, Module 2 questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. The standard split is **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**. Below are two complete alternative questions.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Modeled on `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`*

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Data Flow Graph (DFG) computational model** in detail. What are its advantages over the Von Neumann model for embedded DSP design? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** A Data Flow Graph is a directed graph $G = (V,E)$ in which vertices represent computational *actors* (operations such as $+$, $\times$, memory read) and the directed edges represent *data dependencies* (data tokens) flowing between actors. An actor becomes *enabled* and *executes* as soon as all its input tokens are available.

**Firing Rules (2 marks):**
1. Each edge can hold zero or more tokens initially.
2. An actor with no predecessors fires immediately.
3. On firing, the actor *consumes* one token from each incoming edge and *produces* one token on each outgoing edge.
4. Firing is asynchronous; multiple ready actors may fire in parallel.

**Diagram of a simple DFG for $y = (a+b) \cdot c$:**
*(Draw the same flowchart as in Section 4.2 above.)*

**Advantages over Von Neumann Model for Embedded DSP (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Von Neumann Model | DFG Model |
|--------|-------------------|-----------|
| Inherent parallelism | Sequential by design; one PC | Parallel; multiple actors fire concurrently |
| Data dependency | Implicit in instruction sequence | Explicitly visible in the graph |
| Pipelining | Manual, error-prone | Natural, graph exposes pipeline stages |
| Verification of throughput | Requires simulation | Analytical via iteration bound $T_\infty$ |
| Synthesis to parallel HW | Difficult | Direct mapping to ASIC/FPGA pipelines |

---

**(b)** Design a **Moore FSM** for a 2-bit up-down counter that counts through the sequence: $00 \rightarrow 01 \rightarrow 10 \rightarrow 11 \rightarrow 10 \rightarrow 01 \rightarrow 00 \rightarrow \dots$ when the input $U=1$ (up) or $D=1$ (down). Output $Z$ should be 1 only when the state is $00$ or $11$. Draw the state diagram, state table, and derive the flip-flop input equations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: State Identification (1 mark)**
We need 4 states matching the 2-bit count: $S_0 = 00, S_1 = 01, S_2 = 10, S_3 = 11$.

**Step 2: State Table (2 marks)**

| Present State $Q_1 Q_0$ | Input $U$ | Input $D$ | Next State $D_1 D_0$ | Output $Z$ |
|--------------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------------------|------------|
| 00 | 0 | 0 | 00 | 1 |
| 00 | 1 | 0 | 01 | 1 |
| 00 | 0 | 1 | 11 | 1 |
| 00 | 1 | 1 | 00 | 1 |
| 01 | 0 | 0 | 01 | 0 |
| 01 | 1 | 0 | 10 | 0 |
| 01 | 0 | 1 | 00 | 0 |
| 01 | 1 | 1 | 00 | 0 |
| 10 | 0 | 0 | 10 | 0 |
| 10 | 1 | 0 | 11 | 0 |
| 10 | 0 | 1 | 01 | 0 |
| 10 | 1 | 1 | 01 | 0 |
| 11 | 0 | 0 | 11 | 1 |
| 11 | 1 | 0 | 00 | 1 |
| 11 | 0 | 1 | 10 | 1 |
| 11 | 1 | 1 | 11 | 1 |

**Step 3: Moore Output (1 mark)**
Since this is a Moore machine, $Z$ depends only on the present state:

$$
Z = \overline{Q_1} \cdot \overline{Q_0} + Q_1 \cdot Q_0
$$

**Step 4: Next-State Equations (3 marks)**
Using K-map simplification (treating $U, D$ as control inputs):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= (Q_1 \oplus Q_0 \oplus U) \cdot \overline{D} + (Q_1 \odot Q_0 \odot D) \cdot \overline{U} \\
&\text{(Simplified:)} \\
D_1 &= \overline{Q_1} \cdot Q_0 \cdot U + Q_1 \cdot \overline{Q_0} \cdot U + Q_1 \cdot Q_0 \cdot \overline{U} + Q_1 \cdot \overline{Q_0} \cdot D \\
D_0 &= U \oplus Q_0 \oplus D
\end{aligned}
$$

*(A complete K-map derivation is expected for full 7 marks; the symbolic form above is acceptable for the answer key.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently forget that in a **Moore** machine, the output column depends **only** on the *Present State* — not on inputs. Writing $Z$ as a function of $U$ or $D$ is an instant 2-mark deduction.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice for Same Module*

**(a)** Explain the **Finite State Machine model** with its formal 6-tuple definition. Compare Mealy and Moore machines in terms of timing, output stability, and implementation complexity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Formal 6-tuple Definition (3 marks):**
An FSM is a sextuple $(S, I, O, \delta, \lambda, s_0)$:
- $S = \{s_0, s_1, \dots, s_n\}$ — finite set of states
- $I = \{i_1, i_2, \dots, i_m\}$ — finite set of input alphabet
- $O = \{o_1, o_2, \dots, o_k\}$ — finite set of output alphabet
- $\delta: S \times I \rightarrow S$ — next-state transition function
- $\lambda: S \times I \rightarrow O$ (Mealy) or $\lambda: S \rightarrow O$ (Moore) — output function
- $s_0 \in S$ — designated start state

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Mealy Machine | Moore Machine |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|
| **Output Equation** | $O(t) = \lambda(S(t), I(t))$ | $O(t) = \lambda(S(t))$ |
| **Timing** | Output appears *same cycle* as input change | Output appears *one cycle later* (delayed) |
| **Output Stability** | Sensitive to input glitches; may change asynchronously | Stable for full clock cycle; glitch-free |
| **State Count for same function** | Usually fewer states | Usually more states (need to encode outputs in states) |
| **Hardware** | Less state memory, more output combinational logic | More flip-flops, simpler output logic |
| **KTU Use Case** | Reactive controllers with fast response | Display drivers, sequence generators |

---

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, describe the **Concurrent Process Model** for embedded system design. How does it differ from the sequential Von Neumann model in representing parallelism? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** The Concurrent Process Model represents an embedded system as a *collection of sequential processes* (tasks) that execute *logically in parallel* and synchronize/communicate via shared variables, message queues, semaphores, or channels.

**Diagram (2 marks):** *(Reproduce the Mermaid diagram from Section 4.4 above, but as a clean block diagram showing 3 processes connected by 2 message queues and an RTOS scheduler.)*

**Differences from Von Neumann Model (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Von Neumann Model | Concurrent Process Model |
|--------|-------------------|---------------------------|
| **Program Counter** | Single, central | Multiple, one per process |
| **Parallelism** | Inherent sequentiality; pseudo-parallelism via interrupts | True logical parallelism (real on multi-core) |
| **Communication** | Shared memory (bus) | Message passing OR shared memory with synchronization primitives |
| **Synchronization** | Not natively supported | Semaphores, mutexes, monitors, rendezvous |
| **Designer Concern** | Instruction scheduling, cache | Process scheduling, deadlock avoidance, race conditions |
| **Real-Time Suitability** | Poor for hard real-time (jitter from interrupts) | Excellent (RTOS provides bounded latency) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **concurrency** with **parallelism**. *Concurrency* is a *program structure* property (logically simultaneous tasks). *Parallelism* is a *hardware execution* property (physically simultaneous on multiple cores). The Concurrent Process Model *describes* concurrency, which *can be mapped* onto parallel hardware.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Save this for the night before the exam!)**

- [ ] A **computational model** is a formal abstraction; it is *not* code or hardware — it is the in-between specification.
- [ ] **DFG** is for **data-dominant** systems; **FSM** is for **control-dominant** systems; **Concurrent Process** is for **parallel/real-time** systems.
- [ ] DFG firing rule: actor fires *only* when ALL inputs have tokens. (Memoryless, asynchronous, no PC.)
- [ ] FSM formal 6-tuple: $(S, I, O, \delta, \lambda, s_0)$ — memorize the order of Greek symbols.
- [ ] **Mealy** = output = f(state, input) — faster, glitch-prone.
- [ ] **Moore** = output = f(state) — slower by 1 cycle, glitch-free, more states.
- [ ] Number of flip-flops needed for FSM with $N$ states: $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil$.
- [ ] **Iteration bound** $T_\infty = \max(t_l / N_l)$ is the tightest achievable *lower bound* on DFG iteration time.
- [ ] **McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity** $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ is used in CFG-based software quality analysis for embedded C code.
- [ ] **Concurrent Process Model** requires explicit **synchronization primitives** (semaphores, mutexes) to avoid race conditions and deadlocks.
- [ ] In KTU 2024 scheme, a 14-mark Module 2 question will almost always ask for *one model in deep detail* (7 marks) *plus a comparison or design problem* (7 marks) — practice both flavors.
- [ ] Always draw the **state diagram** *before* the state table *before* the logic equations in FSM design questions — partial marking depends on the completeness of this sequence.
- [ ] Use `\vert` (not `|`) for absolute value in LaTeX inside markdown tables to avoid parser crashes.
- [ ] Real-time embedded systems like **AUTOSAR ECUs**, **RTOS-based IoT nodes**, and **DSP codecs** are the canonical industrial examples examiners love to cite.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Unified Modelling Language (UML)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT523 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
