# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**"Define Hardware-Software Trade-off in embedded system design. List any four criteria used to make this trade-off."**

**Model Answer (verbatim-quality, 3 marks):**

> Hardware-Software Trade-off is the engineering decision process of partitioning the functional specification of an embedded system between dedicated hardware (ASIC, FPGA, hardware accelerator) and programmable software (firmware running on a general-purpose processor) in order to meet design constraints optimally.
>
> Four standard criteria used for the trade-off are:
> 1. **Performance / Execution speed** — measured in latency, throughput, or deadline miss rate.
> 2. **Cost** — both NRE (one-time) and per-unit cost at the expected production volume.
> 3. **Power consumption** — critical for battery-operated IoT and wearable systems.
> 4. **Flexibility / Upgradability** — ability to modify functionality post-deployment (e.g., OTA firmware updates).
>
> *(Other acceptable criteria: reliability/determinism, time-to-market, design reuse, area, weight.)*

*(Mark split: 1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for the four criteria with brief justification.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]

**"Explain why pure hardware and pure software implementations are not optimal for modern embedded systems. Give an example."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Pure hardware (custom ASIC) implementations are not optimal because they carry extremely high **Non-Recurring Engineering (NRE) cost**, long design cycles (often 12–18 months for fabrication), zero post-fabrication flexibility, and high risk of design errors that cannot be patched.
>
> Pure software implementations on a general-purpose processor are not optimal because they are inherently **sequential**, with fetch-decode-execute overhead, and cannot meet sub-microsecond hard real-time deadlines for kernels like FFT, encryption, or motor PWM generation.
>
> **Example:** In a smartphone, the LTE baseband signal processing is done in dedicated hardware (DSP + accelerators) for sub-ms latency, while the user-interface rendering and app logic run in software on the application processor. Neither pure approach would satisfy the combined speed, cost, and flexibility demands of the phone.
>
> Hence, a **hybrid HW/SW co-design** is the de-facto standard for modern embedded systems.

*(Mark split: 1 mark each for the two reasons + 1 mark for the example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B Module-internal choice means *two full 14-mark questions, attempt any one*. Both are provided below with complete sub-parts and step-by-step model answers.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Numerical / Analytical Flavour*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model, Module 2]**

**(a)** A function in an embedded system can be implemented either in **software** on a microcontroller with $C_{NRE,sw} = \text{Rs. } 50{,}000$ and $C_{sw} = \text{Rs. } 30$ per unit, or as a **custom hardware block** with $C_{NRE,hw} = \text{Rs. } 30{,}00{,}000$ and $C_{hw} = \text{Rs. } 5$ per unit. Compute the **crossover volume** $N_{cross}$ and recommend the implementation for production volumes of (i) 10,000 units and (ii) 1,00,000 units. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the same system, an engineer proposes adding a hardware accelerator to a function $f$ that currently consumes 80% of the firmware execution time. The accelerator offers a **10× speedup** for that portion. Using **Amdahl's Law**, compute the overall system speedup. If the deadline budget for the system is **5 ms** and the original (pure software) execution time was **8 ms**, does the hardware accelerator make the system meet the deadline? Show all steps. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a)

**Step 1 — Write total cost equations.**

$$TC_{sw}(N) = 30N + 50{,}000$$

$$TC_{hw}(N) = 5N + 30{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Equate and solve for $N_{cross}$.**

$$30N + 50{,}000 = 5N + 30{,}00{,}000$$

$$25N = 30{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000 = 29{,}50{,}000$$

$$N_{cross} = \dfrac{29{,}50{,}000}{25} = 1{,}18{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3 — Compare the two production volumes.**

- At $N = 10{,}000$: software cost $= 30 \times 10{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 3{,}50{,}000$; hardware cost $= 5 \times 10{,}000 + 30{,}00{,}000 = 35{,}00{,}000$. Software is **10× cheaper**. **Recommendation: SOFTWARE.**

- At $N = 1{,}00{,}000$: software cost $= 30 \times 1{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 30{,}50{,}000$; hardware cost $= 5 \times 1{,}00{,}000 + 30{,}00{,}000 = 35{,}00{,}000$. Software is still cheaper, but only by ~14%. **Recommendation: SOFTWARE** (we are below $N_{cross} = 1{,}18{,}000$).

**Step 4 — Final boxed answer.**

$$\boxed{N_{cross} = 1{,}18{,}000 \text{ units. Use software for both given volumes.}}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b)

**Step 1 — Identify Amdahl's Law parameters.**
- Accelerated fraction: $f = 0.80$
- Speedup of accelerated portion: $S = 10$

**Step 2 — Apply Amdahl's Law.**

$$Speedup = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.80) + \dfrac{0.80}{10}} = \dfrac{1}{0.20 + 0.08} = \dfrac{1}{0.28} \approx 3.57$$

**Step 3 — Compute new (post-acceleration) execution time.**

$$T_{new} = \dfrac{T_{old}}{Speedup} = \dfrac{8 \text{ ms}}{3.57} \approx 2.24 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with deadline.**

$$T_{new} = 2.24 \text{ ms} \;<\; \text{Deadline} = 5 \text{ ms} \Rightarrow \text{Meets deadline with } 2.76 \text{ ms slack.}$$

**Step 5 — Final boxed answer.**

$$\boxed{Speedup = 3.57\times; \; T_{new} \approx 2.24 \text{ ms} < 5 \text{ ms deadline. Hardware accelerator JUSTIFIES itself.}}$$

**Insight to write:** Even though the accelerator speeds up 80% of the code by 10×, the unaccelerated 20% bottlenecks the system — a 3.57× total speedup, not 10×. This is the classic Amdahl lesson.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Conceptual / Design Flavour*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **Hardware-Software Co-design methodology** with a neat flow diagram. Compare it with the **traditional design flow**, highlighting at least three advantages of co-design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an IoT wearable heart-rate monitor, the system architect must decide whether the **PPG-signal peak-detection algorithm** should run on the Cortex-M0 firmware or on a dedicated hardware accelerator. Frame this as a **Hardware Gain vs Hardware Penalty** decision. List the **four penalty components** and the **three gain components** that should be considered, and recommend the implementation given that the device ships in volumes of **2,00,000 units/yr** with a **strict 10 ms latency budget** and **6-month battery life** requirement. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a)

**Co-design Flow (described in text for marks):**

1. Start from a **unified system specification** capturing both functional and non-functional (cost, power, latency) requirements.
2. Build a **unified HW/SW model** of the system, where the boundary between hardware and software is initially undefined.
3. Run a **partitioning algorithm** (manual, Kernighan-Lin, GCLP, or heuristic) to assign each function to either the HW or SW partition.
4. Synthesize the hardware partition (HLS to RTL, then to gate-level netlist).
5. Cross-compile the software partition for the target processor.
6. **Integrate** both partitions onto the target platform (FPGA prototype or virtual platform).
7. **Verify** against the original constraints.
8. If any constraint fails, **iterate** back to step 3 (this feedback loop is the defining feature of co-design).

**Three advantages over traditional flow:**

1. **Concurrent development** shortens time-to-market because HW and SW teams work in parallel from the specification stage, not sequentially.
2. **Optimal partitioning** is possible because the partition is searched, not forced. The traditional flow fixes HW first, leaving SW to "make do," which often produces suboptimal performance and bloated firmware.
3. **Early verification** through virtual platforms and FPGA prototypes catches HW/SW integration bugs (memory maps, bus protocols, timing) before tape-out, saving expensive re-spins.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b)

**Framing the decision:**

This is a textbook **Hardware Gain vs Hardware Penalty** trade-off.

**The three Hardware Gain components to evaluate:**
1. $G_{speed}$ — Speedup of the peak-detection loop. Software on Cortex-M0 (no FPU) might take ~3–5 ms per beat; hardware can finish in <0.5 ms.
2. $G_{power}$ — Power reduction: a custom detector at 1 MHz CMOS draws ~10 µW vs software polling on MCU drawing ~500 µW. Critical for 6-month battery life.
3. $G_{reliability}$ — Determinism: hardware detector gives a fixed 0.5 ms latency, satisfying the **10 ms hard real-time deadline**; software on a busy RTOS scheduler can jitter beyond 10 ms.

**The four Hardware Penalty components to evaluate:**
1. $C_{NRE}$ — One-time mask / verification cost (~₹20–40 lakh for the custom block).
2. $C_{area} \cdot \alpha$ — Silicon area cost in the SoC.
3. $C_{flexibility}$ — Once taped, the algorithm cannot be improved via OTA update; this is a *significant* penalty because peak-detection algorithms benefit from clinical tuning post-launch.
4. $C_{time\_to\_market}$ — Adding an accelerator block adds ~3–6 months to the schedule, delaying the wearable launch.

**Quantitative volume check:**

- Expected volume over 3 years: $N = 6{,}00{,}000$ units.
- Amortized NRE per unit: ₹20,00,000 / 6,00,000 ≈ **₹33/unit**, very small at this scale.
- Per-unit HW area + test cost ≈ **₹15/unit**.
- Total per-unit HW penalty ≈ **₹48**.
- Power saving at scale ≈ 490 µW × battery lifetime = significant user-experience benefit.

**Decision:**

The gain side has **all three factors strongly positive** (10× speed, 50× lower power, hard-real-time guarantee), and the penalty side is **amortized to <₹50/unit** at the 2,00,000/yr volume. The flexibility penalty is real but the algorithm is clinically mature.

$$\boxed{\text{Recommendation: Implement the PPG peak-detector in a CUSTOM HARDWARE ACCELERATOR, with the Cortex-M0 firmware handling configuration, BLE transmission, and battery management.}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in HW/SW Trade-off Questions:**
>
> 1. **Skipping the "interpretation" step in crossover-volume problems.** Writing only "$N_{cross} = 1{,}18{,}000$" without stating *"software is chosen for $N < N_{cross}$ and hardware for $N > N_{cross}$"* costs **1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing NRE with per-unit cost.** NRE is a **one-time** cost (mask, layout, verification); per-unit cost is what you pay for **each chip**. Mixing them in the total-cost equation is a **fatal error** worth losing 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the deadline / schedulability check** in Amdahl-style problems. A 3.57× speedup means nothing unless you check whether the *new* execution time satisfies the *deadline*. Examiners explicitly test this.
> 4. **Listing only benefits or only penalties** in a Gain-vs-Penalty question. The question asks for **both sides** — students often list three gains and zero penalties, losing 2 marks.
> 5. **Using "hardware is always faster" without quantifying.** Always pair a claim with a number (latency in ms/µs, power in mW, cost in ₹/$). Qualitative-only answers cap at ~60% marks in 14-mark questions.
> 6. **Forgetting the feedback loop in the co-design flow diagram.** A linear "Spec → HW → SW" diagram is the **traditional** flow, not co-design. The co-design diagram *must* have a feedback arrow from verification back to partitioning.
> 7. **Ignoring volume sensitivity.** The *same* function can flip from software to hardware as volume changes. Examiners love asking "if the volume doubles / halves, does your recommendation change?" — always do a sensitivity check.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this and revise 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **HW/SW trade-off** = partitioning system functions between dedicated hardware and programmable firmware to *optimally* meet cost, performance, power, flexibility, and time-to-market constraints.
- ✅ There are **three implementation styles**: pure HW (ASIC), pure SW (firmware), and **hybrid** (HW accelerators + SW on MCU) — the hybrid model dominates modern embedded SoCs.
- ✅ The **Hardware Gain** $G_i = w_s \Delta S + w_p \Delta P + w_r \Delta R$ must exceed the **Hardware Penalty** $P_i = C_{NRE} + C_{area}\alpha + C_{flex} + C_{TTM}$ for hardware to win.
- ✅ **Crossover volume formula:** $\;N_{cross} = \dfrac{C_{NRE,hw} - C_{NRE,sw}}{C_{unit,sw} - C_{unit,hw}}$ — derive it from equating total costs, never memorize the *formula* alone, memorize the *derivation*.
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law:** $\;Speedup = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + f/S}\;$ — the unaccelerated fraction $(1 - f)$ is the *bottleneck ceiling* on speedup, no matter how fast the accelerator is.
- ✅ **Partitioning granularities:** system / function / instruction / bit — most KTU problems use **function-level**.
- ✅ **Low volume + high flexibility** → **software wins**. **High volume + hard real-time** → **hardware wins**. Grey zone → use Gain vs Penalty analysis.
- ✅ **Co-design** has a *feedback loop* from verification back to partitioning — traditional flow does not.
- ✅ Standard quantitative metrics to always state: latency in **ms or µs**, power in **mW or µW**, cost in **$ or ₹**, NRE separately from per-unit, volume in **units/yr**, speedup as a **dimensionless ratio**.
- ✅ **Real-world examples to memorize:** AES in radio SoCs (HW); LTE baseband (HW); UI rendering (SW); flight control (SW with FPU); pacemaker logic (HW ASIC); IoT prototypes (SW on ESP32).
- ✅ **KTU favourite 14-mark structure:** 7 marks for one numerical (crossover or Amdahl) + 7 marks for one design question (Gain vs Penalty framing with real example).
- ✅ Always perform a **sensitivity check** on volume when recommending HW vs SW — the answer can flip.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Development: Hardware Design and Development - VLSI and Integrated Circuit Design, Recap of Electronic Design Automation (EDA) Tools, PCB Layout Design, Printed Circuit Board (PCB) Fabrication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT523 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design and Development: Hardware Design and Development - VLSI and Integrated Circuit Design, Recap of Electronic Design Automation (EDA) Tools, PCB Layout Design, Printed Circuit Board (PCB) Fabrication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 VLSI and Integrated Circuit (IC) Design

### Formal Academic Definition
**Very Large-Scale Integration (VLSI)** is the process of creating an **Integrated Circuit (IC)** by combining thousands to billions of **Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistors (MOSFETs)** onto a single semiconductor die (substrate), typically silicon. The discipline spans from architectural specification, logic design, circuit design, physical layout, and final tape-out for fabrication in foundries (e.g., TSMC, Intel, Samsung).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** VLSI design is the **front-end of hardware realization** for any embedded system. The "System-on-Chip (SoC)" concept in modern embedded design is a direct descendant of VLSI methodology.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of an IC as a **megacity** built on a single plot of land:
- The **silicon die** is the plot of land.
- **Transistors** are the buildings/houses.
- **Metal layers (interconnects)** are the roads and highways connecting them.
- **EDA tools** are the urban planners, architects, and civil engineers.
- The **fabrication plant (fab)** is the construction company that physically builds the city.

Without VLSI, your smartphone would be the size of a room. VLSI packs computational power that previously filled a building onto a fingernail-sized chip.

### Standard Metrics in VLSI
- **Node size / Process node:** **3 nm, 5 nm, 7 nm, 14 nm, 28 nm, 65 nm, 180 nm, 350 nm** (the smaller, the denser, faster, and lower power).
- **Transistor count on a chip:** Billions (Apple M3 ≈ 25 billion, NVIDIA H100 ≈ 80 billion).
- **Die size:** Typically **50 mm² to 800 mm²** for high-end processors.
- **Clock frequency:** **MHz to GHz** range (5 GHz max for commercial CPUs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Moore's Law (1965):** The number of transistors on an integrated circuit doubles approximately every **two years**, driving exponential growth in computing power while cost per transistor falls.

---

## 1.2 Recap of Electronic Design Automation (EDA) Tools

### Formal Academic Definition
**Electronic Design Automation (EDA)** refers to the category of **software tools** used to design, simulate, verify, and manufacture electronic systems — ranging from discrete PCBs to full-custom ASICs. EDA tools automate the tedious, error-prone manual steps and enable **correctness-by-construction** design.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
If VLSI is building a megacity, EDA tools are:
- **AutoCAD** for drawing the schematics (schematic capture).
- **ANSYS/SolidWorks simulation** for verifying that the building won't collapse (circuit simulation, SPICE).
- **Google Maps route optimizer** for routing connections between blocks (place & route).
- **Building code inspector** to verify rules (Design Rule Check — DRC).
- **City zoning office** to check the legal layout (Layout Versus Schematic — LVS).

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of EDA Tools in the VLSI Flow:**
> 1. **Front-End (Logical/Behavioral Design):** HDL editors, logic synthesis, simulation (ModelSim, Vivado, Synopsys VCS).
> 2. **Back-End (Physical Design):** Place & route, DRC, LVS (Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC, Synopsys Design Compiler).
> 3. **Verification & Sign-off:** Static timing analysis, power analysis, parasitic extraction (Synopsys PrimeTime, Ansys RedHawk).

### Major EDA Vendors
- **Cadence Design Systems** — Virtuoso, Innovus, Spectre, Allegro.
- **Synopsys** — Design Compiler, IC Compiler II, PrimeTime, VCS.
- **Siemens EDA (Mentor Graphics)** — Calibre (industry gold standard for DRC/LVS), HyperLynx.
- **Open-source:** **Yosys** (synthesis), **Icarus Verilog**, **Magic** (layout), **KiCad** (PCB).

---

## 1.3 PCB Layout Design

### Formal Academic Definition
**Printed Circuit Board (PCB) Layout Design** is the process of converting an **electronic schematic diagram** (logical netlist) into a **physical artwork** that defines the placement of components and the copper traces, vias, and planes on a laminated, non-conductive substrate (typically FR-4). It is the **bridge between schematic engineering and physical manufacturing**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
The PCB is like a **road map of a city**:
- **Components (ICs, resistors, capacitors)** are landmarks / buildings.
- **Copper traces** are the roads connecting them.
- **Vias** are the interchanges/underpasses between different layers.
- **Ground/power planes** are the city-wide utilities (water, electricity).
- **Silkscreen** is the road signage.
- **Solder mask** is the asphalt covering the copper roads (green paint).

Without a well-designed PCB, electrical signals degrade, the board overheats, or the circuit simply doesn't work.

---

## 1.4 Printed Circuit Board (PCB) Fabrication

### Formal Academic Definition
**PCB Fabrication** is the **manufacturing process** of physically producing the PCB from the design files (Gerber files). It involves laminating copper onto a substrate, patterning the copper via photolithography, drilling/plating vias, applying solder mask and silkscreen, and finally profiling the board to its final shape.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Industry Term:** The output of the PCB layout stage is a set of **Gerber files (RS-274X)**, which is the **universal standard** for PCB manufacturing — analogous to a "blueprint" sent to a fab house.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
If PCB design is the **architect's blueprint**, then PCB fabrication is the **construction company pouring concrete, laying bricks, painting walls, and handing over the finished house**. Just as architects never pour concrete, designers don't fabricate — they hand off **Gerber + drill files** to a **PCB fab house** (e.g., JLCPCB, PCBWay, AT&S, Unimicron).

### Standard PCB Metrics
- **Standard substrate:** **FR-4** (Flame Retardant grade 4) — glass-reinforced epoxy laminate, dielectric constant **Dk ≈ 4.2 to 4.6**, loss tangent **tan δ ≈ 0.02**.
- **Copper weight:** **0.5 oz/ft² (17 μm)** to **2 oz/ft² (70 μm)** standard; up to **6 oz/ft² (210 μm)** for high-current power PCBs.
- **Standard layer counts:** **1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20+** layers.
- **Min trace width / spacing (commercial):** **6 mil (0.15 mm) / 6 mil**.
- **Min via drill:** **0.2 mm (8 mil)** mechanical; **0.1 mm laser-drilled microvias**.
- **Standard board thickness:** **0.8 mm, 1.0 mm, 1.6 mm, 2.0 mm, 3.2 mm**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always remember the **signal flow chain** of embedded hardware development:
> **Specification → Schematic → VLSI/IC Selection → PCB Layout → Gerber → Fabrication → Assembly (SMT) → Bring-up/Test.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 VLSI Design Flow — The Y-Chart

The **Gajski-Kuhn Y-chart** is the canonical VLSI abstraction model used universally in industry and academia. It shows three concentric domains of design representation:

- **Behavioral domain (top arc):** What the system does (HDL, algorithmic description).
- **Structural domain (left arc):** How it is composed (gates, registers, MUXes).
- **Geometric/Physical domain (right arc):** How it is laid out physically (mask layouts, GDSII).

As you move from the **outer rings → center (silicon)**, the abstraction level **decreases** (more detail), and design cost of an error **increases exponentially**.

### Operational Logic — Steps in VLSI Design
1. **System Specification:** Define functionality, performance, power, area, cost (the "design constraints").
2. **Architectural Design:** Partition into hardware/software; choose processor, memory hierarchy, bus (AMBA, AXI, Wishbone).
3. **Behavioral/Logic Design:** Write **HDL (Verilog/VHDL/SystemVerilog)** to describe registers, FSMs, datapaths.
4. **Functional Simulation:** Verify logic correctness using testbenches (no timing).
5. **Logic Synthesis:** Convert HDL to a **gate-level netlist** (mapped to a standard cell library) using tools like Synopsys Design Compiler or Yosys.
6. **Pre-layout Static Timing Analysis (STA):** Check setup/hold violations.
7. **Floorplanning & Placement:** Decide macro positions, optimize for timing, congestion, and power.
8. **Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS):** Distribute the clock with balanced skew.
9. **Routing:** Connect cells using metal layers; resolve congestion.
10. **Post-layout STA + Parasitic Extraction (PEX):** Verify with real wire delays.
11. **Physical Verification:** **DRC (Design Rule Check)** + **LVS (Layout vs Schematic)** + **Antenna checks** + **ERC**.
12. **Tape-out:** Generate **GDSII** (Graphic Database System II) file — sent to foundry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** A bug caught at the **specification stage** costs **$1** to fix; the same bug at **tape-out** can cost **$10 million+**. This is why EDA verification is non-negotiable.

---

## 2.2 Power Dissipation in CMOS VLSI

A CMOS gate consumes power in two modes:

### 2.2.1 Static Power (Leakage Power)

$$P_{\text{static}} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{\text{leak}}$$

Where $I_{\text{leak}}$ includes **subthreshold leakage** and **gate-oxide tunneling leakage**. This was negligible in older nodes but dominates in **sub-7 nm** designs.

### 2.2.2 Dynamic Power (Switching Power)

When a CMOS gate switches, it charges and discharges the load capacitance $C_L$:

$$P_{\text{dynamic}} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{\text{clk}}$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = **switching activity factor** (probability the gate switches in a clock cycle, $0 \le \alpha \le 1$).
- $C_L$ = **load capacitance** (in Farads).
- $V_{DD}$ = **supply voltage** (in Volts).
- $f_{\text{clk}}$ = **clock frequency** (in Hz).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **$V_{DD}^{2}$ dependence** is why **voltage scaling** is the single most powerful lever to reduce power. Cutting $V_{DD}$ from 1.2 V → 0.8 V cuts dynamic power to **~44%** of the original.

### 2.2.3 Short-Circuit Power

During the brief transition interval, both NMOS and PMOS conduct simultaneously:

$$P_{\text{sc}} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{\text{sc,avg}}$$

### 2.2.4 Total Chip Power

$$P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{dynamic}} + P_{\text{static}} + P_{\text{short-circuit}}$$

---

## 2.3 CMOS Inverter Propagation Delay

The **intrinsic delay** of a CMOS inverter driving load $C_L$ is:

$$\tau_{p} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2 \cdot I_{D,\text{sat}}}$$

A more practical delay model uses the **logical effort** framework:

$$D = f + g \cdot h$$

Where:
- $D$ = **total delay** (in $\tau$, the FO4 inverter delay unit).
- $f$ = **parasitic delay** (technology constant, $\approx 1$ for inverters).
- $g$ = **logical effort** of the gate (1 for inverter, 4/3 for NAND, 5/3 for NOR).
- $h$ = **electrical effort** $= C_{\text{out}} / C_{\text{in}}$.

---

## 2.4 PCB Trace Resistance

For a copper trace of length $L$, width $w$, and thickness $t$:

$$R_{\text{trace}} = \frac{\rho_{\text{Cu}} \cdot L}{w \cdot t}$$

Where:
- $\rho_{\text{Cu}}$ = **resistivity of copper** $= 1.724 \times 10^{-8} \, \Omega \cdot \text{m}$ at **25 °C**.
- $L$, $w$, $t$ in meters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Temperature derating:** $\rho$ increases ~0.4% per °C. For a 70 °C board:
> $$\rho_{70} \approx \rho_{25} \cdot \bigl(1 + 0.004 \cdot 45\bigr) \approx 2.03 \times 10^{-8} \, \Omega \cdot \text{m}$$

---

## 2.5 PCB Trace Current Carrying Capacity (IPC-2152)

A widely used empirical relationship for **external** traces:

$$I = k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725}$$

Where:
- $I$ = current in **Amps**.
- $\Delta T$ = **temperature rise** above ambient (in °C).
- $A$ = **cross-sectional area** in **mils²** ($= w_{\text{mil}} \cdot t_{\text{mil}}$).
- $k$ = **0.024** for internal traces, **0.048** for external traces (standard IPC-2152 constants).

---

## 2.6 Characteristic Impedance of a Microstrip Line

For a top-layer trace over a ground plane (microstrip):

$$Z_{0} = \frac{87}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,\text{eff}} + 1.41}} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{5.98 \cdot h}{0.8 \cdot w + t}\right) \, \Omega$$

Where:
- $h$ = **dielectric thickness** (height of trace above ground plane).
- $w$ = **trace width**.
- $t$ = **trace thickness**.
- $\varepsilon_{r,\text{eff}}$ = **effective dielectric constant** $\approx \dfrac{\varepsilon_{r}+1}{2} + \dfrac{\varepsilon_{r}-1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1+12h/w}}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Speed Design Rule:** A trace must be terminated such that its **characteristic impedance matches the source/destination impedance** (typically **50 Ω single-ended, 90 Ω / 100 Ω differential**). Mismatch causes reflections, ringing, and EMI.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Equation** | **Parameters / Units** | **Engineering Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| CMOS Dynamic Power | $P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^{2} f_{\text{clk}}$ | $\alpha$: activity, $C_L$: F, $V_{DD}$: V, $f$: Hz | Power budgeting in SoCs |
| CMOS Static Power | $P_{\text{stat}} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{\text{leak}}$ | $I_{\text{leak}}$: A | Leakage in nanometer nodes |
| CMOS Inverter Delay | $\tau_{p} = C_L V_{DD} / (2 I_{D,\text{sat}})$ | seconds | Timing closure |
| Logic Delay (Logical Effort) | $D = f + g \cdot h$ | dimensionless $\tau$ units | Path delay optimization |
| PCB Trace Resistance | $R = \rho L / (w t)$ | $\rho$ in $\Omega \cdot$m, lengths in m | Voltage drop, power loss |
| Trace Current Capacity | $I = k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725}$ | $A$ in mils², $I$ in A | Fuse / power trace sizing |
| Microstrip Impedance | $Z_{0} = \frac{87}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,\text{eff}} + 1.41}} \cdot \ln(5.98 h / (0.8 w + t))$ | $\Omega$ | High-speed signal integrity |
| Via Inductance (approx) | $L_{\text{via}} \approx 1 \, \text{nH per mm of length}$ | nH | Power integrity, decoupling |
| Moore's Law (count) | $N(t) = N_{0} \cdot 2^{(t - t_{0})/2}$ | transistors | Long-term roadmap |
| Switching Energy | $E_{\text{sw}} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ | Joules | Energy per transition |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Never use the `\` character in tables** — for absolute value or magnitude, use $\vert x \vert$ or write "magnitude of x" to keep the markdown table parsable.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mobile/IoT chips:** Use VLSI dynamic power formula to budget battery life (a smartwatch SoC targets < **5 mW** average).
- **Automotive ECU PCBs:** Use IPC-2152 trace current formula to size **12 V power rails** that can carry **10–30 A** without overheating.
- **5G / DDR4 / PCIe Gen4 PCBs:** Use microstrip impedance equation to maintain **85 Ω differential / 50 Ω single-ended** controlled-impedance routing, otherwise the link fails eye-diagram compliance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Dynamic Power of a CMOS Gate

### Goal
Show why $P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{\text{clk}}$ emerges from first-principles capacitor charging.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Energy stored in the load capacitor when charged to $V_{DD}$:**

The energy stored on a capacitor charged from 0 to $V_{DD}$ is:

$$E_{\text{stored}} = \frac{1}{2} C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$$

This energy is supplied by the power supply.

**Step 2 — Energy dissipated in the PMOS during charging:**

During charging, the PMOS transistor acts as a resistor. By the time the capacitor charges to $V_{DD}$, exactly **half** the energy supplied by $V_{DD}$ is dissipated as heat in the PMOS channel, and the other half is stored in $C_L$. So the total energy drawn from the supply per charge cycle is:

$$E_{\text{drawn}} = C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Energy dissipated per charge AND discharge cycle:**

On the **discharge** cycle, the stored $\tfrac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ is dissipated as heat in the NMOS transistor. So **one full switching cycle (0 → 1 → 0)** dissipates:

$$E_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{1}{2} C_{L} V_{DD}^{2} \; (\text{in PMOS}) + \frac{1}{2} C_{L} V_{DD}^{2} \; (\text{in NMOS}) = C_{L} V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by switching activity and clock frequency:**

If the gate switches only a fraction $\alpha$ of the clock cycles, and the clock runs at $f_{\text{clk}}$, then the average power is:

$$P_{\text{dyn}} = E_{\text{cycle}} \cdot \alpha \cdot f_{\text{clk}} = C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot \alpha \cdot f_{\text{clk}}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{\text{clk}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic recap:** Energy per cycle comes from capacitor charge sharing. We multiply by the rate ($\alpha \cdot f_{\text{clk}}$) to convert energy per cycle into average power. The $\tfrac{1}{2}$ factors combine to give unity because charging and discharging each dissipate half.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: PCB Trace Resistance and Voltage Drop

### Problem Setup
A 50 mm long, 0.2 mm wide, 35 μm (1 oz) thick copper trace carries 2 A. What is the resistance and DC voltage drop?

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Convert all dimensions to meters:**

$$L = 50 \, \text{mm} = 0.050 \, \text{m}, \quad w = 0.2 \, \text{mm} = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}, \quad t = 35 \, \mu\text{m} = 3.5 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the trace resistance formula:**

$$R = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{w \cdot t} = \frac{1.724 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 0.050}{(2.0 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (3.5 \times 10^{-5})}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the cross-sectional area:**

$$A = w \cdot t = (2.0 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (3.5 \times 10^{-5}) = 7.0 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator and divide:**

$$\rho L = 1.724 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 0.050 = 8.62 \times 10^{-10} \, \Omega \cdot \text{m}^{2}$$

$$R = \frac{8.62 \times 10^{-10}}{7.0 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.1231 \, \Omega$$

**Step 5 — Voltage drop using Ohm's law:**

$$V_{\text{drop}} = I \cdot R = 2 \cdot 0.1231 = 0.2462 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 6 — Power dissipated in the trace:**

$$P_{\text{trace}} = I^{2} \cdot R = 4 \cdot 0.1231 = 0.4924 \, \text{W}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering interpretation:** A **0.25 V drop** on a 3.3 V rail means the load sees only **3.05 V** — possibly below its operating threshold. The solution: **widen the trace**, **increase copper weight**, or **shorten the path**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Trace Width for a Given Current and Temperature Budget

### Problem Setup
A power trace must carry **5 A** with max **10 °C** temperature rise on an **external** layer. Copper weight is **2 oz/ft²** (≈ **70 μm thick**). Find the **minimum trace width** $w$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Use the IPC-2152 formula and solve for $A$:**

$$I = 0.048 \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725}$$

$$5 = 0.048 \cdot 10^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $10^{0.44}$:**

$$10^{0.44} \approx 2.754$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and isolate $A^{0.725}$:**

$$5 = 0.048 \cdot 2.754 \cdot A^{0.725} = 0.1322 \cdot A^{0.725}$$

$$A^{0.725} = \frac{5}{0.1322} = 37.82$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $A$ by raising both sides to the power $1/0.725 \approx 1.3793$:**

$$A = 37.82^{1.3793}$$

**Step 5 — Compute:**

$$A \approx e^{1.3793 \cdot \ln(37.82)} = e^{1.3793 \cdot 3.633} = e^{5.010} \approx 150.4 \, \text{mils}^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Compute width:**

$$w_{\text{mil}} = \frac{A}{t_{\text{mil}}} = \frac{150.4}{2.76} \approx 54.5 \, \text{mil}$$

$$\boxed{w \approx 54.5 \, \text{mil} \approx 1.38 \, \text{mm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic recap:** We treat the IPC equation as a power-law and invert it via exponentiation. The result tells the designer the **physical minimum copper geometry** to avoid thermal failure.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: VLSI Design Levels & Cost of Error

| **Abstraction Level** | **Representation** | **Typical EDA Tool** | **Cost to Fix a Bug** | **Time to Fix** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| System Specification | Text/algorithm spec | Document, UML | **1 unit** | 1 day |
| Architectural | Block diagram, ISA | SystemC, MATLAB | **10 units** | 1 week |
| Register Transfer (RTL) | Verilog/VHDL code | ModelSim, Vivado | **100 units** | 2 weeks |
| Logic Synthesis | Gate netlist | Design Compiler, Yosys | **1,000 units** | 1 month |
| Place & Route | Physical layout, GDSII | Innovus, ICC2 | **10,000 units** | 3 months |
| Fabrication | Silicon die | Foundry (TSMC, Intel) | **1,000,000 units** | 12+ months |
| In-Field | Customer's hand | Recall / RMA | **10,000,000+ units** | Catastrophic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Mantra:** "**Shift Left.**" The earlier a bug is caught, the cheaper the fix. This is the philosophical foundation of **Verification-Driven Design (VDD)** in modern SoC flows.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Power Estimation of an Embedded SoC

**Given:**
- Clock frequency: $f_{\text{clk}} = 200 \, \text{MHz}$
- Average switched capacitance per cycle: $C_{\text{avg}} = 20 \, \text{nF}$
- Supply voltage: $V_{DD} = 1.0 \, \text{V}$
- Average switching activity: $\alpha = 0.15$
- Leakage current: $I_{\text{leak}} = 5 \, \text{mA}$

**Find:** Total power consumption.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Dynamic power:**

$$P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \cdot C_{\text{avg}} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{\text{clk}}$$

$$P_{\text{dyn}} = 0.15 \cdot (20 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot (1.0)^{2} \cdot (200 \times 10^{6})$$

$$P_{\text{dyn}} = 0.15 \cdot 20 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 2 \times 10^{8} = 0.15 \cdot 4 \times 10^{0} = 0.6 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 2 — Static power:**

$$P_{\text{stat}} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{\text{leak}} = 1.0 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-3} = 0.005 \, \text{W} = 5 \, \text{mW}$$

**Step 3 — Total power (ignoring short-circuit):**

$$P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{dyn}} + P_{\text{stat}} = 0.600 + 0.005 = 0.605 \, \text{W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{total}} \approx 0.605 \, \text{W} \approx 605 \, \text{mW}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Dynamic power dominates by **120×** — characteristic of an actively switching SoC. In idle/sleep modes, leakage can become 30–50% of total, which is why **clock gating** and **power gating** are essential.

## 4.1 VLSI / IC Design Flow (Top-Down RTL-to-GDSII)

This Mermaid diagram captures the canonical **RTL-to-GDSII** physical design flow used in any ASIC / SoC tape-out.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[SPECIFICATION] --> A2[ARCHITECTURAL DESIGN]
    A2 --> A3[RTL DESIGN VERILOG OR VHDL]

    A6 --> A7[PLACEMENT]
    A7 --> A8[CLOCK TREE SYNTHESIS CTS]
    A8 --> A9[ROUTING]
    A9 --> A10[PARASITIC EXTRACTION PEX]
    A10 --> A11[STATIC TIMING ANALYSIS STA]
    A11 --> A12[DRC LVS ERC PHYSICAL VERIFICATION]
    A12 --> A13[GDSII TAPEOUT TO FOUNDRY]
    A13 --> A14[PACKAGING AND TEST]

    A4 -.BUG FOUND.-> A3
    A11 -.TIMING FAIL.-> A7
    A12 -.DRC VIOLATION.-> A9

    classDef stepOk fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the dotted arrows:** They represent **iterative feedback loops** — if a bug is found at any step, the design is reworked upstream. This is the "**shift-left**" verification philosophy.

## 4.5 PCB Stackup Schematic (4-Layer, FR-4)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["LAYER 1 TOP SIGNAL 1 OZ COPPER"] --> S2["PREPREG 0.21 MM FR4 DK 4.3"]
    S2 --> S3["LAYER 2 INNER GROUND PLANE"]
    S3 --> S4["CORE 1.20 MM FR4 DK 4.3"]
    S4 --> S5["LAYER 3 INNER POWER PLANE 3V3"]
    S5 --> S6["PREPREG 0.21 MM FR4 DK 4.3"]
    S6 --> S7["LAYER 4 BOTTOM SIGNAL 1 OZ COPPER"]

    classDef copperLayer fill:#b45309,stroke:#fcd34d,color:#ffffff
    classDef dielectricLayer fill:#374151,stroke:#9ca3af,color:#ffffff
    class S1,S3,S5,S7 copperLayer
    class S2,S4,S6 dielectricLayer
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the stackup:** The **4-layer PCB** alternates **signal / dielectric / plane / core / plane / dielectric / signal**. The dedicated **ground and power planes** provide:
> - A low-impedance return path for high-speed signals (EMI control).
> - Decoupling capacitance between $V_{DD}$ and GND planes.
> - Mechanical rigidity and thermal mass.
