# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**"What is VLSI? List any four advantages of using CMOS technology for VLSI design."**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
**VLSI (Very Large Scale Integration)** is the technology of integrating **hundreds of thousands to billions of MOSFETs** onto a single silicon die to realize complex digital/analog/mixed-signal functions. The discipline covers everything from architectural design, RTL coding, synthesis, physical design, to fabrication.

**Four advantages of CMOS (1.5 marks):**

1. **Near-zero static power consumption:** CMOS gates draw negligible current in steady state since one of PMOS/NMOS is always OFF.
2. **High noise immunity:** The full-swing 0 → $V_{DD}$ transition yields wide noise margins (typically **40% of $V_{DD}$**).
3. **Scalable to deep submicron nodes:** CMOS has scaled from 10 μm (1970s) to **3 nm (2024)**.
4. **High packing density & low cost per gate:** Billions of transistors can be fabricated at minimal marginal cost per unit.
5. *(Bonus, optional)* **Wide commercial ecosystem:** Foundries, EDA tools, and standard cell libraries are mature.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse **MOSFET** with **BJT** in CMOS. Always explicitly state **CMOS = Complementary MOS = PMOS + NMOS**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**"Explain the term 'EDA tool'. With examples, classify EDA tools into front-end, back-end, and verification categories."**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1 mark):**
**Electronic Design Automation (EDA) tools** are software suites that automate the design, simulation, verification, and physical realization of electronic systems. Without EDA, designing a modern SoC with **billions of transistors** would be humanly impossible.

**Classification (2 marks):**

| **Category** | **Function** | **Examples** |
|---|---|---|
| **Front-end (Logical)** | HDL editing, simulation, logic synthesis | ModelSim, Vivado Simulator, **Synopsys VCS**, **Cadence Genus**, **Yosys** (open-source) |
| **Back-end (Physical)** | Floorplanning, placement, routing, PEX | **Cadence Innovus**, **Synopsys ICC2**, **Cadence Virtuoso** (full-custom) |
| **Verification & Sign-off** | DRC, LVS, STA, power, IR-drop, EM | **Siemens Calibre** (DRC/LVS), **Synopsys PrimeTime** (STA), **Ansys RedHawk** (power/EM) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Marks are deducted when students cite **SolidWorks** or **AutoCAD** as EDA tools — those are **mechanical CAD**, not **Electronic** Design Automation.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A [14 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **complete VLSI design flow** from specification to GDSII tape-out. Highlight the role of EDA tools at each stage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for **dynamic power dissipation in a CMOS inverter**. A CMOS chip operates at $V_{DD} = 1.2 \, \text{V}$, clock frequency $f = 500 \, \text{MHz}$, with average load capacitance $C_L = 50 \, \text{pF}$ and switching activity $\alpha = 0.1$. Calculate the dynamic power dissipation. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — VLSI Design Flow (7 Marks):**

1. **Specification (0.5 mark):** Define functionality, performance, power, area, cost.
2. **Architecture (0.5 mark):** Partition HW/SW; select processor, bus (AMBA AXI/AHB).
3. **RTL Design (1 mark):** Describe the design in **Verilog/VHDL/SystemVerilog** at the register-transfer level. Use FSMs, datapaths, and standard interfaces.
4. **Functional Simulation (0.5 mark):** Verify logic correctness using testbenches (no timing). Tools: **ModelSim, Vivado Simulator, VCS**.
5. **Logic Synthesis (0.5 mark):** Translate HDL to a **gate-level netlist** mapped to a standard-cell library. Tools: **Synopsys Design Compiler, Yosys, Cadence Genus**.
6. **Floorplanning (0.5 mark):** Decide the die area, aspect ratio, macro/IP placement, I/O pad ring.
7. **Placement (0.5 mark):** Place standard cells to minimize wirelength, congestion, and delay. Tools: **Innovus, ICC2**.
8. **Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS) (0.5 mark):** Distribute the clock with **balanced skew** and minimal insertion delay.
9. **Routing (0.5 mark):** Connect all cells using metal layers; resolve congestion and signal integrity issues.
10. **Parasitic Extraction (PEX) + Post-layout STA (0.5 mark):** Extract RC from layout, re-verify timing. Tools: **StarRC, PrimeTime**.
11. **Physical Verification (0.5 mark):** Run **DRC** (geometric rules), **LVS** (layout vs schematic), **ERC** (electrical rules), antenna checks. Tools: **Calibre, Pegasus**.
12. **Tape-out (0.5 mark):** Export **GDSII** to the foundry for mask generation and fabrication.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** Draw a clean left-to-right or top-to-bottom flowchart as in **Section 4.1** of these notes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Note:** Examiners award 1 mark for the diagram itself, and distribute the remaining 6 marks across the explanations. Each stage does NOT need equal weight — focus on **synthesis, P&R, and verification** for higher marks.

---

**Part (b) — Dynamic Power Derivation + Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

When a CMOS inverter output switches from 0 → 1, the load capacitor $C_L$ charges to $V_{DD}$.

**Energy supplied by the power supply** during charging:

$$E_{\text{supplied}} = Q \cdot V_{DD} = (C_L V_{DD}) \cdot V_{DD} = C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Energy stored on $C_L$** at the end of charging:

$$E_{\text{stored}} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Energy dissipated in PMOS** (charging half):

$$E_{\text{PMOS}} = C_L V_{DD}^{2} - \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

On the **discharge** cycle (1 → 0), the $\tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ stored energy is dissipated as heat in the **NMOS**.

So **energy dissipated per full switching cycle** (0 → 1 → 0):

$$E_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} + \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} = C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Multiplying by the activity factor $\alpha$ and clock frequency $f$** to get average power:

$$P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: $V_{DD} = 1.2 \, \text{V}$, $f = 500 \, \text{MHz} = 5 \times 10^{8} \, \text{Hz}$, $C_L = 50 \, \text{pF} = 50 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{F}$, $\alpha = 0.1$.

$$P_{\text{dyn}} = 0.1 \cdot (50 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (1.2)^{2} \cdot (5 \times 10^{8})$$

$$P_{\text{dyn}} = 0.1 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 1.44 \cdot 5 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step-by-step grouping:**

$$= (0.1 \cdot 1.44) \cdot (50 \times 5) \cdot (10^{-12} \cdot 10^{8})$$

$$= 0.144 \cdot 250 \cdot 10^{-4}$$

$$= 36 \cdot 10^{-4} = 3.6 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{dyn}} = 3.6 \, \text{mW}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall #1:** Forgetting the **$V_{DD}^{2}$** (squared) — students frequently write $V_{DD}$ linearly and get wrong answer.
>
> **Examiner Pitfall #2:** Not **converting units** (MHz → Hz, pF → F). Always write the conversion explicitly.
>
> **Examiner Pitfall #3:** Confusing **switching energy** ($\tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$) with **full-cycle dissipated energy** ($C V^{2}$). The factor of 2 difference is a favorite trap.

---

### **Question B [14 Marks — Alternative]**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **PCB design flow** from schematic capture to Gerber file generation. List the standard EDA tools used at each stage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a **50 mm long, 0.25 mm wide, 35 μm thick copper trace** on a **2-layer FR-4 PCB** carrying **3 A**, calculate:
- (i) The DC resistance of the trace at **25 °C**. **(3 Marks)**
- (ii) The voltage drop across the trace. **(2 Marks)**
- (iii) The power dissipated in the trace. **(2 Marks)**

Take the resistivity of copper as $\rho_{Cu} = 1.724 \times 10^{-8} \, \Omega \cdot \text{m}$.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — PCB Design Flow (7 Marks):**

1. **Schematic Capture (1 mark):** Draw the circuit in an EDA schematic editor. Tools: **KiCad Eeschema, Altium Designer, OrCAD Capture, Cadence Allegro Design Entry HDL**.
2. **Netlist Generation (0.5 mark):** Export a **netlist** (SPICE, PADS, ODB++, IPC-D-356A) describing the connectivity.
3. **Component Footprint Assignment (0.5 mark):** Map every schematic symbol to a **PCB footprint** (land pattern).
4. **Stackup & Layer Planning (0.5 mark):** Define the **PCB layer stack** (signal, power, ground, dielectric thicknesses). Critical for controlled-impedance designs.
5. **Design Rules Setup (0.5 mark):** Set trace widths, clearances, via sizes per fab house capabilities.
6. **Component Placement (1 mark):** Place components for **optimal signal flow, thermal, and EMI** considerations.
7. **Power & Ground Plane Design (0.5 mark):** Pour copper planes for $V_{DD}$ and GND; star-ground or split planes as needed.
8. **Signal Routing (1 mark):** Route traces; honor **controlled-impedance** (50 Ω, 90 Ω differential) constraints for high-speed nets.
9. **Design Rule Check (DRC) (0.5 mark):** Run EDA's built-in DRC for trace width, clearance, via-to-trace, plane-to-trace.
10. **Signal Integrity Simulation (0.5 mark):** For high-speed (DDR, PCIe, USB, HDMI), run **SI/PI analysis** using HyperLynx, Ansys SIwave.
11. **Gerber + Drill File Export (1 mark):** Export **RS-274X Gerber files** (one per layer) + **Excellon drill files** + assembly files (**BOM**, **Pick-and-Place**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Tip:** Examiners appreciate explicit **tool names** alongside each step. Avoid generic statements like "use EDA tools" — name them.

---

**Part (b) — Trace Resistance, Voltage Drop, and Power (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $L = 50 \, \text{mm} = 0.050 \, \text{m}$
- $w = 0.25 \, \text{mm} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}$
- $t = 35 \, \mu\text{m} = 3.5 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}$
- $I = 3 \, \text{A}$
- $\rho_{Cu} = 1.724 \times 10^{-8} \, \Omega \cdot \text{m}$

**(i) DC Resistance (3 marks):**

$$R = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{w \cdot t}$$

**Cross-sectional area:**

$$A = w \cdot t = (2.5 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (3.5 \times 10^{-5}) = 8.75 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}^{2}$$

**Numerator:**

$$\rho L = 1.724 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 0.050 = 8.62 \times 10^{-10} \, \Omega \cdot \text{m}^{2}$$

**Resistance:**

$$R = \frac{8.62 \times 10^{-10}}{8.75 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.0985 \, \Omega \approx 98.5 \, \text{m}\Omega$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 0.0985 \, \Omega}$$

**(ii) Voltage Drop (2 marks):**

$$V_{\text{drop}} = I \cdot R = 3 \cdot 0.0985 = 0.2955 \, \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{drop}} \approx 0.296 \, \text{V}}$$

**(iii) Power Dissipated (2 marks):**

$$P_{\text{trace}} = I^{2} \cdot R = 9 \cdot 0.0985 = 0.8865 \, \text{W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{trace}} \approx 0.887 \, \text{W}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall #1:** Forgetting to convert **mm → m**. The factor of **$10^{-3}$** mistake is the most common — failing this unit conversion can cascade to wrong answers in all three sub-parts.
>
> **Examiner Pitfall #2:** Mixing up **m (milli, $10^{-3}$)** with **M (Mega, $10^{6}$)** in unit prefixes. Always write them out in full: "millimeter" and "milliohm".
>
> **Examiner Pitfall #3:** Using the wrong power formula ($V \cdot I$ vs $I^2 R$ vs $V^2 / R$). For trace power loss, $I^{2} R$ is the most direct and correct.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Universal Pitfalls for This Module (Lose 1–3 Marks Each):**
>
> 1. **Vague definitions:** Writing "VLSI is a chip with many components" is **worth 0 marks**. Use the textbook term "**hundreds of thousands to billions of MOSFETs on a single silicon die**."
> 2. **Skipping the GDSII / Gerber mention:** Every VLSI/PCB question that touches "design output" expects you to name the **GDSII** (VLSI) or **Gerber RS-274X** (PCB) file format. Examiners consider this a **must-mention keyword**.
> 3. **Forgetting the $V_{DD}^{2}$ in dynamic power:** This is the #1 numerical error in KTU papers. Re-derive every time.
> 4. **Confusing internal vs external trace current capacity:** IPC-2152 uses **$k = 0.024$ for internal** and **$k = 0.048$ for external** layers. Wrong $k$ = wrong answer.
> 5. **Missing units in the final answer:** Always append **mW, Ω, V, m** to every numerical result. A correct number without units is **marked partially wrong**.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **VLSI = Very Large Scale Integration** — integrating millions to billions of transistors on a single die; modern SoCs use **3 nm / 5 nm** process nodes.
- **Moore's Law** (1965): Transistor count on an IC doubles approximately every **2 years** — still a useful industry roadmap.
- **EDA Tools** automate the VLSI/PCB design flow; the three pillars are **front-end (HDL/synthesis)**, **back-end (P&R)**, and **sign-off (DRC/LVS/STA)**.
- **Standard EDA vendors:** **Cadence**, **Synopsys**, **Siemens EDA (Mentor)** dominate; **Yosys, KiCad, Magic** are popular open-source tools.
- **VLSI Design Flow:** Spec → Architecture → RTL → Simulation → Synthesis → Floorplan → Placement → CTS → Routing → PEX → STA → Verification → **GDSII** → Tape-out.
- **CMOS dynamic power formula:** $P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{\text{clk}}$ — the **$V_{DD}^{2}$** dependence makes voltage scaling the most powerful power-reduction knob.
- **Static (leakage) power:** $P_{\text{stat}} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{\text{leak}}$ — dominates in sub-7 nm designs; mitigated by **power gating**, **multi-$V_{TH}$** libraries, and **clock gating**.
- **PCB substrate** is almost always **FR-4** with $\varepsilon_r \approx 4.2$–$4.6$; copper resistivity is $\rho_{Cu} = 1.724 \times 10^{-8} \, \Omega \cdot \text{m}$.
- **PCB trace resistance:** $R = \rho L / (w t)$ — to minimize, **widen the trace**, use **thicker copper** (2 oz/ft² ≈ 70 μm), or **shorten the path**.
- **IPC-2152 trace current formula:** $I = k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725}$, with $k = 0.048$ external, $k = 0.024$ internal.
- **Characteristic impedance** of a microstrip is set by trace geometry and substrate — high-speed signals (DDR, USB, HDMI, PCIe) require **controlled impedance (50 Ω SE / 90–100 Ω diff)**.
- **PCB Design Flow:** Schematic → Netlist → Footprint → Stackup → Placement → Routing → DRC → SI Check → **Gerber (RS-274X)** + Excellon drill → **Fab house** → Assembly.
- **Gerber RS-274X** is the **universal PCB fabrication file format**; **Excellon** is the drill format; **ODB++** and **IPC-2581** are modern alternatives.
- **4-layer PCB stackup:** Top signal → Prepreg → Inner GND plane → Core → Inner Power plane → Prepreg → Bottom signal — planes provide low-impedance returns and decoupling.
- **Verification is king:** **DRC** (geometry rules), **LVS** (layout vs schematic), **STA** (timing), **ERC** (electrical rules), and **SI/PI** analysis are non-negotiable in any professional flow.
- **Cost of error grows ~10× per design stage** — caught at spec = $1; caught at tape-out = $1 M; caught in field = $10 M+ (think Intel Pentium FDIV bug, 1994).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Firmware Design and Development - Embedded Firmware Design, Embedded Firmware Development Languages, Programming in Embedded C
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT523 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Firmware Design and Development - Embedded Firmware Design, Embedded Firmware Development Languages, Programming in Embedded C

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Embedded Firmware?

**Embedded Firmware** is the specialised software program permanently stored in the non-volatile memory (ROM, EPROM, EEPROM, Flash) of an embedded system. It is the "brain" that directs every hardware component, executes the intended application logic, and reacts to real-time events without any underlying operating system or with a lightweight Real-Time Operating System (RTOS).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (PECIT523, Module 3):**
> *Embedded firmware is a low-level software control program tightly coupled to specific hardware, residing in read-only or flash memory, that directly manipulates hardware registers to perform deterministic, real-time operations of an embedded device.*

### Intuitive Analogy: The Restaurant Kitchen 🍳

Think of an embedded system as a **fully automated vending machine**:

- **Hardware** = The metal cabinet, motors, sensors, and display screen.
- **Firmware** = The hidden, permanent instruction list (written on a chip) that says: *"If coin inserted → validate → select motor rotates → dispense product → display thanks."*

Just like a chef's secret recipe is locked in a notebook and executed deterministically for every customer, **firmware** is a fixed, deterministic, hardware-aware program that never gets swapped by the user.

| Term | Analogy Counterpart |
|------|---------------------|
| Microcontroller | Kitchen apparatus (oven, mixer) |
| Firmware | Recipe notebook bolted to the wall |
| User Application | Customer's order |
| Firmware Update | Re-writing the recipe in the notebook |

## 1.2 Embedded Firmware vs Application Software vs Device Driver

| Property | Application Software | Device Driver | **Embedded Firmware** |
|----------|---------------------|---------------|-----------------------|
| Runs on | PC / Server | OS Kernel | Bare metal / RTOS |
| Updatable by user | Yes (frequent) | Yes (with OS update) | Rarely (field updates only) |
| Hardware awareness | Indirect | High | **Highest (direct register access)** |
| Real-time constraints | Soft | Mixed | **Hard real-time** |
| Storage | HDD/SSD | RAM | **ROM/Flash** |

## 1.3 Embedded Firmware Development Languages — The Big Picture

Embedded firmware can be expressed in many languages, each with its own trade-off between **performance**, **ease of development**, **portability**, and **hardware control**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically lists the following firmware development languages:
> 1. **Assembly Language** (machine-level control)
> 2. **C Language** (the industry default)
> 3. **Embedded C++ / C++** (object-oriented embedded)
> 4. **Java (J2ME / Embedded Java)** (for high-level appliances)
> 5. **Python (MicroPython / CircuitPython)** (rapid prototyping)
> 6. **Rust** (modern safety-critical)
> 7. **Ada** (aerospace & defence)
> 8. **VHDL/Verilog** (FPGA firmware via HDLs)

## 1.4 Why C is the *De-facto* Language of Embedded Firmware

Among all candidates, **C** dominates because it provides the perfect equilibrium:

- **Low-level access** (via pointers & bitwise operators) → talks to hardware directly.
- **High-level portability** → source code can be recompiled for ARM, AVR, PIC, 8051.
- **Predictable performance** → no hidden garbage collector, no JIT overhead.
- **Tiny runtime footprint** → suits kilobyte-scale memory budgets.
- **Massive ecosystem** → Keil, IAR, GCC-AVR, ARM-GCC toolchains.

> [!TIP]
> If you remember only one sentence for the exam: *"Embedded firmware is hardware-aware, deterministic, real-time software written primarily in C (with assembly for critical inner loops)."*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Embedded Firmware Design Process (Step-by-Step)

A disciplined firmware designer follows this structured lifecycle. Each step has a specific deliverable and a measurable metric.

### Step 1 — Requirements Analysis
- Identify **functional** requirements (what the system must do).
- Identify **non-functional** requirements (latency, power, memory ceiling, reliability).
- Quantify the **worst-case execution time (WCET)** budget.

### Step 2 — Hardware Analysis
- Read the **datasheet** of the MCU (registers, memory map, peripherals).
- Identify **I/O pin assignments**, ADC channels, timers, communication buses.
- Note the **clock frequency** $f_{\text{clk}}$ (this controls every timing calculation).

### Step 3 — Architecture & Module Partitioning
- Break the firmware into **logical modules**: `main.c`, `gpio.c`, `uart.c`, `isr.c`, `scheduler.c`.
- Define a **layered architecture** (Application → Drivers → HAL → Registers).

### Step 4 — Algorithm Design
- Use **finite state machines (FSM)** for sequential control.
- Express timing constraints as **timer equations**.

### Step 5 — Coding in Embedded C
- Use **hardware abstraction headers** (e.g., `stm32f4xx.h`).
- Avoid dynamic memory (`malloc`/`free`) — causes fragmentation.
- Use `const` for ROM data, `volatile` for hardware registers and ISR-shared variables.

### Step 6 — Compilation, Linking & Locating
- Compile C → assembly → relocatable object.
- **Linker script** (.ld / .sct) places code into Flash, data into RAM.
- Output: **Intel HEX** or **ELF + BIN** for the programmer.

### Step 7 — Debug & Validation
- Use **JTAG / SWD** debuggers (OpenOCD, GDB).
- Employ **logic analysers** and **oscilloscopes** for signal integrity.
- Perform **static analysis** (MISRA-C) and **code coverage** testing.

### Step 8 — Deployment & Field Update Strategy
- Program the **Flash memory** via ISP / IAP / bootloader.
- Reserve a **bootloader region** in the linker map for OTA updates.

## 2.2 Comparison of Firmware Development Languages

| Language | Hardware Control | Performance | Memory Footprint | Learning Curve | Typical Use Case |
|----------|:---------------:|:-----------:|:----------------:|:--------------:|------------------|
| Assembly | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | Tiny | Steep | Boot loaders, ISR, DSP inner loops |
| **Embedded C** | ★★★★☆ | ★★★★☆ | Small | Moderate | **Universal MCU firmware** |
| Embedded C++ | ★★★☆☆ | ★★★★☆ | Medium | Steep | Complex stateful systems (AUTOSAR) |
| Java (Embedded) | ★★☆☆☆ | ★★★☆☆ | Large (JVM) | Moderate | Smart cards, set-top boxes |
| MicroPython | ★★☆☆☆ | ★★☆☆☆ | Medium | Easy | Education, rapid prototyping |
| Rust | ★★★★☆ | ★★★★★ | Small | Steep | Safety-critical (medical, automotive) |
| Ada | ★★★☆☆ | ★★★★★ | Small | Steep | Aerospace, defence (DO-178C) |
| HDL (VHDL/Verilog) | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | N/A (synthesised) | Steep | FPGA/CPLD firmware |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 examiner expects you to state **why C is preferred over assembly and over C++ for typical 8/16/32-bit MCUs**. Memorise the keyword: *C offers the best performance-to-portability ratio with deterministic, minimal runtime overhead.*

## 2.3 Key Concepts of Programming in Embedded C

### 2.3.1 Anatomy of an Embedded C Program

An embedded C program differs from a host-PC C program in three structural ways:
1. It **never returns from `main()`** (it runs an infinite loop).
2. It manipulates **memory-mapped peripheral registers** using pointers.
3. It must initialise the **interrupt vector table** and the **stack pointer**.

### 2.3.2 Memory-Mapped I/O (MMIO)

In an MCU, every peripheral (UART, ADC, GPIO, Timer) appears as a **32-bit (or 8/16-bit) register at a fixed address** in the physical memory map.

$$\text{Register Address} = \text{Peripheral Base Address} + \text{Offset}$$

For example, on the STM32F4 family:

$$\text{GPIOA\_ODR} = 0x40020000_{\text{hex}} + 0x14_{\text{hex}} = 0x40020014_{\text{hex}}$$

To toggle a pin, the firmware writes a `1` (for set) or `0` (for reset) to the bit position corresponding to that pin.

### 2.3.3 The `volatile` Qualifier — A Critical Keyword

The keyword **`volatile`** tells the compiler: *"Do not optimise this variable into a register; its value may change outside the program's flow."*

```c
volatile uint32_t *const GPIOA_ODR = (volatile uint32_t *)0x40020014;
```

This is **mandatory** for:
- Hardware registers (changed by hardware).
- Variables shared between an ISR and the main loop (changed by interrupt context).

### 2.3.4 Bitwise Operators — The Firmware Engineer's Toolbox

The six bitwise operators used constantly in embedded C are:

| Operator | Symbol | Purpose in Firmware |
|----------|:------:|---------------------|
| AND | `&` | Masking specific bits (clear others) |
| OR | `\|` | Setting specific bits |
| XOR | `^` | Toggling specific bits |
| NOT | `~` | Bit inversion |
| Left shift | `<<` | Multiplying by 2 or building masks |
| Right shift | `>>` | Dividing by 2 or extracting bits |

### 2.3.5 Bit Manipulation Macros

These are the **canonical bit-manipulation idioms** that appear in almost every KTU exam:

| Operation | Standard Macro / Expression | Effect on bit `n` |
|-----------|----------------------------|-------------------|
| Set bit n | `reg \|= (1U << n);` | Forces bit to 1 |
| Clear bit n | `reg &= ~(1U << n);` | Forces bit to 0 |
| Toggle bit n | `reg ^= (1U << n);` | Inverts the bit |
| Check bit n | `(reg >> n) & 1U;` | Returns 0 or 1 |
| Write bit n to value v | `reg = (reg & ~(1U << n)) \| (v << n);` | Assigns v to bit n |

### 2.3.6 Inline Assembly & Mixed C-Assembly

Some time-critical sections (e.g., enabling interrupts, context switch) require **inline assembly** inside C code. GCC syntax:

```c
asm volatile ("msr primask, %0" :: "r"(0x1) : "memory");
```

> [!WARNING]
> Inline assembly is **architecture-specific** — it breaks portability. Use it sparingly, only when C cannot meet a timing deadline.

### 2.3.7 Interrupt Service Routines (ISR)

An **ISR** is a function invoked automatically by hardware when an event occurs (e.g., timer overflow, UART byte received). Rules:
- Keep ISRs **short** (set a flag, do heavy work in main).
- Use the **`volatile`** keyword on shared variables.
- Clear the **interrupt flag** inside the ISR to prevent re-entry.
- Do not call blocking functions (e.g., `printf` with float) inside an ISR.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Embedded C & Firmware Timing

| Concept | Equation | Notes |
|---------|----------|-------|
| Timer overflow period | $T_{\text{overflow}} = \dfrac{(ARR + 1) \times (PSC + 1)}{f_{\text{clk}}}$ | ARR = auto-reload, PSC = prescaler |
| Machine cycle | $T_{\text{machine}} = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{clk}}}$ | For most ARM Cortex-M |
| Baud rate (UART) | $f_{\text{baud}} = \dfrac{f_{\text{clk}}}{(BRR + 1)}$ | BRR = baud rate register |
| ADC sampling time | $T_{\text{ADC}} = (n + 12) \times T_{\text{ADC\_CLK}}$ | n = sample cycles |
| Code-size estimate | $S_{\text{kB}} \approx 0.6 \times N_{\text{C\_lines}}$ | Empirical for -Os optimisation |
| Interrupt latency (Cortex-M) | $t_{\text{latency}} \le 12 \text{ cycles} + t_{\text{jitter}}$ | Tail-chaining saves cycles |
| Power consumption | $P = C \times V^{2} \times f$ | Dynamic power model |

> [!NOTE]
> The table follows the standard notation used in **ARM Cortex-M** reference manuals and the **8051 family datasheets** — both of which are valid for KTU 2024 exam questions.

## 2.5 Why Embedded C is the Industry Standard — Real-World Engineering Impact

- **Automotive ECUs** (Bosch, Continental) use C for 90 %+ of engine control firmware because of deterministic timing and MISRA-C compliance.
- **Medical implants** (pacemakers) use C with strict static analysers to satisfy IEC 62304.
- **IoT devices** (ESP32, Nordic nRF52) ship with SDKs in C because of the tiny RAM budget.
- **Aerospace flight software** (NASA CFS) uses a C framework despite Ada's safety reputation, due to the broad C talent pool.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section derives every timing, register, and program-structure example exhaustively. No step is skipped.

## 3.1 Derivation — Timer Delay Calculation for a GPIO Toggle

**Problem:** An STM32F407 (Cortex-M4) is clocked at $f_{\text{clk}} = 168 \text{ MHz}$. Configure TIM2 to generate an interrupt every **500 ms** to toggle an LED. Prescaler (PSC) = 8399, find the Auto-Reload Register (ARR).

### Step 1 — Write the fundamental timer equation

The timer counter increments on each tick of the timer's internal clock:

$$f_{\text{TIM\_CK}} = \frac{f_{\text{clk}}}{PSC + 1}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the timer's tick frequency

Substitute $f_{\text{clk}} = 168 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}$ and $PSC = 8399$:

$$f_{\text{TIM\_CK}} = \frac{168 \times 10^{6}}{8399 + 1} = \frac{168 \times 10^{6}}{8400}$$

$$f_{\text{TIM\_CK}} = 20\,000 \text{ Hz} = 20 \text{ kHz}$$

The corresponding tick period:

$$T_{\text{TIM\_CK}} = \frac{1}{20\,000} = 50 \,\mu\text{s}$$

### Step 3 — Derive the required count for a 500 ms delay

The counter must overflow after time $T_{\text{overflow}} = 500 \text{ ms} = 0.5 \text{ s}$. The number of ticks needed:

$$N_{\text{ticks}} = \frac{T_{\text{overflow}}}{T_{\text{TIM\_CK}}} = \frac{0.5}{50 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$N_{\text{ticks}} = \frac{0.5}{0.00005} = 10\,000$$

### Step 4 — Relate the count to the ARR register

For a "count up then overflow" timer, $N_{\text{ticks}} = ARR + 1$, so:

$$ARR = N_{\text{ticks}} - 1 = 10\,000 - 1 = 9999$$

### Step 5 — Express the full derived formula

$$\boxed{ARR = \left( \frac{T_{\text{overflow}} \cdot f_{\text{clk}}}{PSC + 1} \right) - 1}$$

### Step 6 — Verify the result dimensionally

$$ARR = \frac{0.5 \times 168 \times 10^{6}}{8400} - 1 = \frac{84 \times 10^{6}}{8400} - 1 = 10\,000 - 1 = 9999 \; \checkmark$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation — UART Baud Rate Register (BRR) Value

**Problem:** STM32 USART2 is on APB1 ($f_{\text{APB1}} = 42 \text{ MHz}$). Compute the BRR for 115 200 baud.

### Step 1 — State the baud rate formula

The USART baud rate is derived from the peripheral clock by an integer + fractional divider:

$$\text{Baud} = \frac{f_{\text{APB1}}}{8 \times (2 - \text{OVER8}) \times (\text{USARTDIV})}$$

For OVER8 = 0 (oversampling by 16), this simplifies to:

$$\text{Baud} = \frac{f_{\text{APB1}}}{16 \times \text{USARTDIV}}$$

### Step 2 — Solve for USARTDIV

$$\text{USARTDIV} = \frac{f_{\text{APB1}}}{16 \times \text{Baud}} = \frac{42 \times 10^{6}}{16 \times 115\,200}$$

$$\text{USARTDIV} = \frac{42 \times 10^{6}}{1\,843\,200} = 22.786458\overline{3}$$

### Step 3 — Split into integer and fractional parts

$$\text{USARTDIV} = 22 + 0.786458\overline{3}$$

The integer part goes into bits [15:4] of BRR, the fractional part into bits [3:0]:

$$\text{frac} = 0.786458\overline{3} \times 16 = 12.58333$$

Rounded to nearest integer: $\text{frac} = 13 = 0xD$.

### Step 4 — Reconstruct the BRR register

$$\text{BRR} = (22 \ll 4) \mid 13 = 0x016D$$

### Step 5 — Verify actual achievable baud rate

$$\text{Baud}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{42 \times 10^{6}}{16 \times 22.8125} = 115\,134 \text{ baud}$$

Error percentage:

$$\text{Error} = \frac{\vert 115\,200 - 115\,134 \vert}{115\,200} \times 100 = 0.057 \%$$

> Well within the **±2 %** UART tolerance limit. $\checkmark$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Memory-Mapped GPIO Access for an LED Blink

**Problem:** Toggle PA5 (LED) on a microcontroller with GPIOA base = `0x40020000`, using the ODR register (offset `0x14`).

### Step 1 — Identify the register address

$$\text{GPIOA\_ODR} = 0x40020000 + 0x14 = 0x40020014$$

### Step 2 — Create a `volatile` pointer constant

```c
#define GPIOA_ODR  (*(volatile uint32_t *)0x40020014)
```

The `volatile` qualifier **prevents the compiler from caching** the ODR value in a CPU register.

### Step 3 — Set bit 5 (turn LED on)

```c
GPIOA_ODR |= (1U << 5);   // Bitwise OR sets bit 5 to 1
```

**Bitwise expansion** for bit 5:
- `1U << 5` = `0b00000000000000000000000000100000`
- OR with ODR forces only bit 5 high; other bits untouched.

### Step 4 — Clear bit 5 (turn LED off)

```c
GPIOA_ODR &= ~(1U << 5);  // AND with inverted mask clears bit 5
```

Bitwise expansion:
- `~(1U << 5)` = `0b11111111111111111111111111011111`
- AND with ODR forces only bit 5 to 0.

### Step 5 — Toggle bit 5 (alternate on/off)

```c
GPIOA_ODR ^= (1U << 5);   // XOR with mask flips bit 5
```

If bit 5 was 0, it becomes 1; if it was 1, it becomes 0. Other bits unchanged.

### Step 6 — Compose the full Embedded C program

```c
#include <stdint.h>
#include "device_header.h"   /* vendor register definitions */

#define GPIOA_ODR  (*(volatile uint32_t *)0x40020014)
#define RCC_AHB1ENR (*(volatile uint32_t *)0x40023830)

void delay(volatile uint32_t t) {
    while (t--);   /* crude software delay */
}

int main(void) {
    /* 1. Enable GPIOA clock in RCC (bit 0 of RCC_AHB1ENR) */
    RCC_AHB1ENR |= (1U << 0);

    /* 2. Configure PA5 as output (MODER register, offset 0x00) */
    volatile uint32_t *GPIOA_MODER = (volatile uint32_t *)0x40020000;
    *GPIOA_MODER &= ~(0x3U << (5 * 2));   /* clear bits 11:10 */
    *GPIOA_MODER |=  (0x1U << (5 * 2));   /* set bits 11:10 = 01 (output) */

    /* 3. Infinite loop toggling the LED */
    while (1) {
        GPIOA_ODR |= (1U << 5);   /* LED ON  */
        delay(1000000);
        GPIOA_ODR &= ~(1U << 5);  /* LED OFF */
        delay(1000000);
    }
    /* main never returns in embedded C */
}
```

### Step 7 — Identify the structure for KTU marking

- **Initialization phase** (1 mark): enable clock + configure MODER.
- **Main loop** (1 mark): infinite `while(1)`.
- **Bitwise logic** (2 marks): OR to set, AND-NOT to clear.
- **`volatile` keyword** (1 mark): mandatory explanation.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each Firmware Language

| Decision Criterion | Assembly | C | C++ | MicroPython | Rust |
|--------------------|:--------:|:-:|:---:|:-----------:|:----:|
| Strict real-time deadline (< 1 µs) | ★ | ★ | ★ | ✗ | ★ |
| 8/16-bit MCU with < 16 KB Flash | ★ | ★ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| 32-bit MCU with RTOS | ✗ | ★ | ★ | ★ | ★ |
| Object-oriented state management | ✗ | ✗ | ★ | ★ | ★ |
| Memory safety required | ✗ | ✗ | △ | ✗ | ★ |
| Rapid prototyping / education | ✗ | ★ | ★ | ★ | ★ |
| Safety certification (ISO 26262) | ✗ | ★ | ★ | ✗ | ★ |

**Legend:** ★ Recommended · △ Possible with care · ✗ Not suitable

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Firmware Design & Development Lifecycle (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Requirements Analysis] --> node2[Hardware Datasheet Study]
    node2 --> node3[Architecture & Module Partition]
    node3 --> node4[Algorithm Design FSM Timing]
    node4 --> node5[Embedded C Coding]
    node5 --> node6[Compilation with Cross Toolchain]
    node6 --> node7[Linker Script Memory Map]
    node7 --> node8[Generate HEX or BIN File]
    node8 --> node9[Flash Programming via JTAG or ISP]
    node9 --> node10[On Target Debug with GDB]
    node10 --> node11{Validation Passed?}
    node11 -- Yes --> node12[Field Deployment]
    node11 -- No --> node5
    node12 --> node13[OTA or Bootloader Update]
    node13 --> node14[Return to Step node10 if New Bug]
```

## 4.2 Cross-Compilation Flow for Embedded C

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[main.c] --> preA[Preprocessor cpp]
    srcB[uart.c] --> preB[Preprocessor cpp]
    srcC[isr.c] --> preC[Preprocessor cpp]
    hdr1[device.h] --> preA
    hdr1 --> preB
    preA --> asmA[main.s Assembly]
    preB --> asmB[uart.s Assembly]
    preC --> asmC[isr.s Assembly]
    asmA --> objA[main.o]
    asmB --> objB[uart.o]
    asmC --> objC[isr.o]
    objA --> linker[Linker with .ld script]
    objB --> linker
    objC --> linker
    linker --> reloc[Relocatable ELF]
    reloc --> objcopy[objcopy tool]
    objcopy --> hex[Intel HEX file]
    objcopy --> bin[Raw BIN file]
    hex --> flash[Flash Programmer]
    bin --> flash
    flash --> mcu[Target MCU Flash Memory]
```

## 4.3 Memory Map of a Typical 32-bit MCU (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Flash_ROM
        F1[Bootloader 0x00000000]
        F2[Vector Table 0x00000040]
        F3[Application Firmware 0x00000100]
        F4[Const Data 0x00080000]
    end
    subgraph SRAM
        S1[Stack 0x20000000 to 0x20000FFF]
        S2[Heap 0x20001000 to 0x20004FFF]
        S3[Static Globals 0x20005000 to 0x2001FFFF]
    end
    subgraph Peripherals
        P1[GPIOA 0x40020000]
        P2[USART1 0x40011000]
        P3[TIM2 0x40000000]
        P4[ADC1 0x40012000]
    end
    cpu[ARM Cortex-M4 Core] --> Flash_ROM
    cpu --> SRAM
    cpu --> Peripherals
    F3 -.loads.-> S3
```

## 4.4 Layered Firmware Architecture (HAL / Driver / Application)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    app[Application Layer user.c main.c] --> hal[Hardware Abstraction Layer hal_gpio.h hal_uart.h]
    hal --> drv[Peripheral Drivers gpio.c uart.c adc.c]
    drv --> cmsis[CMSIS Core device.h core_cm4.h]
    cmsis --> reg[Memory Mapped Registers]
    reg --> hw[Physical Hardware]
    isr[Interrupt Service Routines isr.c] -.directly calls.-> drv
    rtos[RTOS Kernel optional] --> app
```

> [!NOTE]
> This four-layer pattern is what every KTU 2024 examiner loves to see in a 14-mark "design a firmware for X" answer. Always draw this layered view before writing code.

## 4.5 State Machine Diagram — Embedded Firmware Control Flow

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> INIT
    INIT --> IDLE : System Initialised
    IDLE --> READ_SENSOR : Timer Tick Every 10 ms
    READ_SENSOR --> PROCESS : ADC Conversion Complete
    PROCESS --> ACTUATE : PWM Duty Cycle Updated
    ACTUATE --> IDLE : Cycle Complete
    READ_SENSOR --> ERROR : Sensor Out of Range
    PROCESS --> ERROR : Compute Overflow
    ERROR --> SAFE_STOP : Log to EEPROM
    SAFE_STOP --> IDLE : Operator Reset
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECIT523]**
> *Differentiate between embedded firmware and application software. List two firmware development languages used in embedded systems.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Embedded Firmware | Application Software |
|--------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Platform** | Runs on bare metal / RTOS on an MCU | Runs on PC/Server OS (Windows/Linux) |
| **User updatability** | Rare; field update only | Frequent user-driven updates |
| **Hardware access** | Direct register-level | Indirect via OS APIs and drivers |
| **Real-time guarantees** | Hard real-time deadlines | Soft or no real-time requirements |
| **Memory budget** | Kilobytes to low megabytes | Megabytes to gigabytes |
| **Examples** | Washing machine controller, ECU | MS Word, Chrome browser |

**Two firmware development languages (3 marks — list any two):**
1. **Assembly Language** — direct control over the CPU.
2. **Embedded C** — industry default, portable, low overhead.
3. **Embedded C++** — object-oriented; used in AUTOSAR.
4. **MicroPython** — high-level rapid prototyping.
5. **Java (Embedded)** — set-top boxes, smart cards.

---

### Question A.2
> **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, PECIT523]**
> *Explain the role of the `volatile` keyword in embedded C programming. Why is it essential when accessing hardware registers?*

**Model Answer:**

The **`volatile`** qualifier instructs the C compiler **not to optimise away reads or writes** to the variable/object it qualifies. Each access is performed as a fresh load/store from the physical address.

**Why essential for hardware registers:**

1. **Hardware changes the register asynchronously.** Without `volatile`, the compiler may cache the value in a CPU register and skip subsequent reads, missing hardware-driven changes.
2. **ISR-shared variables.** A variable updated inside an Interrupt Service Routine and read in the main loop will be cached by the optimiser; `volatile` forces a real memory read every time.
3. **Memory-mapped I/O.** Peripheral registers (e.g., `GPIOA_ODR`, `UART_DR`) must be touched exactly when the program says, never optimised out.

**Example:**
```c
volatile uint32_t *const GPIOA_ODR = (volatile uint32_t *)0x40020014;
```
- The pointer is `const` (address never changes).
- The pointee is `volatile` (value can change at any time).

**Mark-split expected by examiner (3 marks):**
- Definition of volatile — 1 mark.
- Reason 1 (hardware change) — 1 mark.
- Reason 2 (ISR / shared variable) — 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 3, PECIT523]**
> *As per the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, you must answer either Question A OR Question B.*

---

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the embedded firmware design process in detail. List and briefly describe **five key steps** with one example of a deliverable from each step.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **Assembly, C, and MicroPython** as firmware development languages on the basis of (i) hardware control, (ii) performance, (iii) memory footprint, (iv) development time, (v) portability, and (vi) typical application. Give one example use case where C is preferred over the other two.

#### Model Answer — (a)

The embedded firmware design process is a structured, iterative workflow:

| # | Step | Description | Example Deliverable |
|---|------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Requirements Analysis** | Capture functional (e.g., "measure temperature every 100 ms") and non-functional (latency, power, cost) needs. | Software Requirements Specification (SRS) document. |
| 2 | **Hardware Analysis** | Study the MCU datasheet, identify registers, peripherals, clock tree, and pin map. | Register map and pin-assignment table. |
| 3 | **Architecture & Partitioning** | Decompose firmware into modules: drivers, HAL, application, ISRs. Decide whether an RTOS is needed. | Module dependency diagram + layer diagram. |
| 4 | **Algorithm & State Design** | Express control logic as finite state machines; compute timer and baud-rate equations. | FSM diagram + timing budget table. |
| 5 | **Coding in Embedded C** | Write source files using vendor headers, `volatile` for hardware, `const` for ROM data. | `main.c`, `gpio.c`, `uart.c` source files. |
| 6 | **Compilation & Linking** | Use cross-toolchain (e.g., `arm-none-eabi-gcc`) with linker script. | Intel HEX file + memory map. |
| 7 | **Debug & Validation** | JTAG/SWD debugging, logic-analyser tracing, MISRA-C static checks. | Validation report with coverage metrics. |

#### Model Answer — (b)

| Criterion | Assembly | C | MicroPython |
|-----------|:--------:|:-:|:-----------:|
| (i) Hardware Control | ★★★★★ (direct opcodes) | ★★★★☆ (via pointers) | ★★☆☆☆ (abstracted) |
| (ii) Performance | ★★★★★ | ★★★★☆ | ★★☆☆☆ (interpreted) |
| (iii) Memory Footprint | Smallest (bytes to KB) | Small (10s of KB) | Large (hundreds of KB) |
| (iv) Development Time | Slowest | Moderate | Fastest |
| (v) Portability | None (CPU-specific) | High (with HAL) | High (interpreter port) |
| (vi) Typical Application | Boot loaders, DSP inner loops | Universal MCU firmware | Education, hobby boards |

**Where C is preferred:** A 32-bit automotive ECU that must read 16 ADC channels at 10 kHz, run a control loop in 200 µs, and fit in 256 KB Flash, while remaining portable across ARM and PowerPC variants. C is the only language satisfying all three constraints (performance, footprint, portability) simultaneously.

---

### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is memory-mapped I/O? Explain with a suitable block diagram how a C program accesses a GPIO pin to control an LED. Show the relevant register-level code.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a complete Embedded C program to:
- Configure **Port B pin 7** as output.
- Blink the LED at **1 Hz** (500 ms ON, 500 ms OFF).
- Use a **timer interrupt** to toggle the LED without blocking the CPU.
- Assume peripheral base addresses: `GPIOB_ODR = 0x40020414`, `RCC_APB2ENR = 0x40023844`, `TIM2_ARR = 0x4000002C`, `TIM2_PSC = 0x40000028`.

#### Model Answer — (a)

**Memory-Mapped I/O (MMIO):** In MCUs, peripheral control and data registers are mapped to specific addresses within the processor's physical memory space. Reading/writing these addresses is equivalent to communicating with the peripheral.

**Block Diagram (textual, since it complements the Mermaid of §4.3):**

```
+----------------------------+
|  ARM Cortex-M4 Core (CPU)  |
|  Loads/STores from AHB/APB |
+-------------+--------------+
              |
              v
+----------------------------+
| Address Bus                |
| 0x40020000 → GPIOA        |
| 0x40020400 → GPIOB        |
| 0x40023800 → RCC          |
| 0x40000000 → TIM2         |
+----------------------------+
              |
              v
+----------------------------+
| Physical GPIO Pins         |
| PA5, PB7, ...             |
+----------------------------+
```

**C code to access GPIO pin:**
```c
#define RCC_APB2ENR  (*(volatile uint32_t *)0x40023844)
#define GPIOB_ODR    (*(volatile uint32_t *)0x40020414)

/* Enable GPIOB clock: set bit 3 in RCC_APB2ENR */
RCC_APB2ENR |= (1U << 3);

/* PB7 as output: bits 15:14 in GPIOB_MODER = 01 */
volatile uint32_t *GPIOB_MODER = (volatile uint32_t *)0x40020400;
*GPIOB_MODER &= ~(0x3U << (7 * 2));
*GPIOB_MODER |=  (0x1U << (7 * 2));

/* Turn LED on */
GPIOB_ODR |= (1U << 7);
```

#### Model Answer — (b)

```c
#include <stdint.h>

/* Register definitions */
#define RCC_APB1ENR     (*(volatile uint32_t *)0x40023840)
#define RCC_APB2ENR     (*(volatile uint32_t *)0x40023844)
#define GPIOB_ODR       (*(volatile uint32_t *)0x40020414)
#define GPIOB_MODER     (*(volatile uint32_t *)0x40020400)
#define TIM2_ARR        (*(volatile uint32_t *)0x4000002C)
#define TIM2_PSC        (*(volatile uint32_t *)0x40000028)
#define TIM2_CR1        (*(volatile uint32_t *)0x40000000)
#define TIM2_DIER       (*(volatile uint32_t *)0x4000000C)
#define TIM2_SR         (*(volatile uint32_t *)0x40000010)

/* Flag set in ISR, read in main */
volatile uint8_t g_tick = 0;

void TIM2_IRQHandler(void) {
    if (TIM2_SR & (1U << 0)) {       /* UIF flag set? */
        TIM2_SR &= ~(1U << 0);       /* clear UIF */
        g_tick = 1;                  /* notify main loop */
    }
}

void TIM2_Init_500ms(uint32_t f_clk) {
    /* 500 ms with PSC=8399, f_clk=8 MHz: ARR = 49999 */
    TIM2_PSC = 8399;                 /* 8 MHz / (8399+1) = 1 kHz */
    TIM2_ARR = 499;                  /* 1 kHz / (499+1)   = 2 Hz → 500 ms */
    TIM2_DIER |= (1U << 0);          /* enable update interrupt */
    TIM2_CR1  |= (1U << 0);          /* CEN: enable counter */
    /* NVIC enable for TIM2 IRQ number 28 in Cortex-M4 */
    volatile uint32_t *ISER0 = (volatile uint32_t *)0xE000E100;
    *ISER0 |= (1U << 28);
}

int main(void) {
    /* 1. Enable GPIOB clock */
    RCC_APB2ENR |= (1U << 3);
    /* 2. Configure PB7 as general-purpose output */
    GPIOB_MODER &= ~(0x3U << (7 * 2));
    GPIOB_MODER |=  (0x1U << (7 * 2));
    /* 3. Enable TIM2 clock (APB1 bit 0) */
    RCC_APB1ENR |= (1U << 0);
    /* 4. Initialise timer 2 for 500 ms tick at 8 MHz */
    TIM2_Init_500ms(8000000);

    /* 5. Main loop — non-blocking, ISR-driven */
    while (1) {
        if (g_tick) {
            g_tick = 0;
            GPIOB_ODR ^= (1U << 7);   /* toggle PB7 */
        }
        /* CPU can do other work here */
    }
}
```

**Explanation of design choices (for the 7 marks):**
- `volatile` on every peripheral register and the shared `g_tick` flag (1 mark).
- Timer pre-scaled to 1 kHz, ARR = 499 gives exactly 2 Hz → 500 ms tick (1 mark).
- ISR clears UIF, sets a flag — keeps the ISR short (1 mark).
- Main loop polls the flag, toggles ODR with XOR — non-blocking (1 mark).
- NVIC ISER0 bit 28 enables the IRQ vector for TIM2 on Cortex-M4 (1 mark).
- LED initialisation: enable clock + set MODER bits (1 mark).
- Code is fully self-contained, no dynamic memory (1 mark).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers**
> 1. **Forgetting `volatile` on hardware registers** → losing 1–2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Using `malloc` / `printf` in ISR** → considered fatal in firmware reviews.
> 3. **Configuring MODER incorrectly** (e.g., setting both bits to 1 → analog mode) — pin won't toggle.
> 4. **Not clearing the interrupt flag** inside the ISR → ISR re-fires infinitely (system hangs).
> 5. **Returning from `main()`** — non-embedded style; firmware never returns.
> 6. **Confusing bit-set and bit-clear idioms** — using `reg = 1 << n` instead of `reg |= 1 << n` overwrites all other pins (catastrophic in multi-LED systems).
> 7. **Skipping the clock-enable step** for a peripheral — register writes silently have no effect; examiners specifically test this.
> 8. **Not justifying the language choice** in 14-mark answers — merely listing languages without the *"why C is best"* reasoning loses the language-comparison marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

- **Embedded firmware** = hardware-aware, deterministic, real-time software permanently stored in non-volatile memory.
- **Firmware ≠ Application software**: firmware talks directly to registers, no OS, no user install.
- **Firmware design lifecycle** = Requirements → Hardware study → Architecture → Algorithm → C coding → Compile → Debug → Deploy → Update.
- **Languages**: Assembly, C, C++, MicroPython, Java, Rust, Ada, HDL. **C is the de-facto industry default.**
- **Why C?** Performance-to-portability ratio, deterministic, tiny footprint, massive ecosystem.
- **`volatile` is mandatory** for any variable representing a hardware register or shared with an ISR.
- **Bit manipulation toolbox**: `|= (1U<<n)` to set, `&= ~(1U<<n)` to clear, `^= (1U<<n)` to toggle.
- **Memory-mapped I/O** = peripheral registers live at fixed addresses in the CPU's address space; accessed via `volatile` pointers.
- **Timer delay formula**: $T = \dfrac{(ARR+1)(PSC+1)}{f_{\text{clk}}}$.
- **UART baud formula** (OVER8=0): $\text{USARTDIV} = \dfrac{f_{\text{APB1}}}{16 \times \text{Baud}}$.
- **ISR rules**: keep short, set a flag, clear the IRQ flag, never block.
- **`main()` in embedded C** never returns — runs an infinite `while(1)`.
- **Cross-compilation**: host PC compiles → linker places code per `.ld` script → HEX/BIN flashed to target.
- **Layered architecture** (App → HAL → Driver → Register) is the gold-standard answer in design questions.
- **Power formula**: $P = C V^2 f$ — lower the clock, lower the power.
- **MISRA-C** = the C coding standard for safety-critical embedded firmware (memorise the name, not the rules).
- **Static vs dynamic memory** — always prefer static; `malloc`/`free` cause fragmentation in long-running firmware.
- **End-of-exam mantra**: draw the layered diagram, justify C, mark `volatile` everywhere, show the timer math, and clear the IRQ flag.

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — Firmware Design and Development | KTU 2024 Scheme | PECIT523**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integration and Testing of Embedded Hardware and Firmware: Integration of Hardware and Firmware
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT523 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Integration and Testing of Embedded Hardware and Firmware: Integration of Hardware and Firmware

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hardware-Firmware Integration** is the critical phase in the embedded system development life cycle (EDLC) where the developed hardware platform (the physical PCB with processor, memory, peripherals, and I/O subsystems) is combined with the developed firmware (the low-level software, drivers, RTOS, and application code) to form a single, fully functional embedded product. This phase validates that the **hardware-software contract** — the agreed-upon interface, timing, electrical behavior, and protocol — holds true in the physical domain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECIT523 Module 4):** *Integration of Hardware and Firmware refers to the systematic procedure of loading the compiled firmware image into the target hardware's non-volatile memory, initializing the device drivers, executing the Board Support Package (BSP), and validating functional correctness through hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) testing.*

### 1.2 The Two Pillars Being Integrated

| Pillar | Constituents | Verification Tools |
|---|---|---|
| **Hardware** | Microcontroller/Processor, ROM, RAM, Sensors, Actuators, Power circuits, Communication interfaces | Oscilloscope, Logic Analyzer, Multimeter, JTAG probe |
| **Firmware** | Bootloader, BSP, Device Drivers, RTOS Kernel, Middleware, Application Logic | IDE Debugger, Trace tools, GDB, Semihosting |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Car Engine Calibration" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of building a car engine.
> - The **engine block, pistons, crankshaft, and cylinders** are the *hardware* — built precisely to drawings but not yet running.
> - The **engine control unit (ECU) software** — the *firmware* — is written on a computer but has never touched a real engine.
> - **Integration** is the moment the mechanic bolts the ECU onto the engine, uploads the software, turns the key, and checks: *Does the fuel injector pulse at the exact crank angle? Does the oxygen sensor feedback loop close? Does the engine run smoothly under load?*
>
> If the fuel map (firmware) is wrong for the cylinder volume (hardware), the engine misfires. This is exactly what hardware-firmware integration tests and corrects.

## 1.4 Why This Stage is the Most Failure-Prone

Studies in industrial embedded design show that **>60% of embedded product defects** are detected *only* after hardware and firmware are joined. The reason is that each component, when tested in isolation, hides its assumptions about the other:

- The firmware assumes the ADC completes in 12 µs.
- The hardware ADC, due to PCB layout parasitics, actually takes 14 µs.
- In isolation, both work perfectly. **Together, they fail.**

This phenomenon is called the **"Meeting of Assumptions"** defect, and integration testing exists to expose it.

## 1.5 Key Terminology (Board-Examiner Favorites)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **BSP (Board Support Package)** | Layer of firmware that abstracts hardware-specific details (clocks, memory map, peripherals) for the OS/application. |
| **HAL (Hardware Abstraction Layer)** | Software layer providing uniform APIs to upper layers, hiding register-level hardware differences. |
| **Bootloader** | Small primary program that initializes hardware and loads the main firmware image. |
| **JTAG / SWD** | Debug interfaces defined by IEEE 1149.1 (JTAG) and ARM (SWD) used to load and debug firmware on target. |
| **Cross-Compilation** | Compiling firmware on a host (x86 PC) for a target architecture (ARM Cortex-M, AVR, RISC-V). |
| **HIL (Hardware-In-the-Loop)** | Real-time testing where real hardware is stimulated by simulated environment signals. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 The Five Phases of Hardware-Firmware Integration

The integration of hardware and firmware is **not a single event** but a phased process. The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to enumerate and describe these phases:

### Phase 1 — Hardware Bring-Up (Bare-Metal Power-On)
Before any firmware is loaded, the hardware is powered and basic checks are performed.

- Verify **power rails** (3.3 V, 1.8 V, 5 V) using a multimeter.
- Confirm **clock oscillator** output frequency with an oscilloscope or frequency counter.
- Check **reset signal** behavior on power-up.
- Look for **short circuits** or excessive current draw.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why before firmware?** If hardware is faulty, loading firmware is wasted effort. The first 30 minutes of integration are spent proving the board is electrically alive.

### Phase 2 — Firmware Build & Cross-Compilation
The host PC compiles the firmware using a **cross-compiler toolchain**.

Common toolchains:

- **GCC ARM Embedded** (`arm-none-eabi-gcc`) for ARM Cortex targets
- **avr-gcc** for Atmel AVR microcontrollers
- **RISC-V GCC** for RISC-V cores
- **IAR / Keil** commercial toolchains

The build produces:
- **.elf** file (executable + symbols for debugger)
- **.hex** or **.bin** file (raw binary image for flashing)

### Phase 3 — Firmware Loading (Flashing/Programming)
The compiled binary is transferred to the target's non-volatile memory.

| Method | Interface | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **JTAG** | 4-wire (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO) | Production & development |
| **SWD (Serial Wire Debug)** | 2-wire (SWDIO, SWCLK) | ARM Cortex-M devices |
| **UART Bootloader** | TX, RX, GND | Field updates |
| **SPI/I2C Bootloader** | 2-3 wires | Factory programming |
| **USB DFU** | USB D+/D- | Consumer devices |
| **OTA (Over-The-Air)** | Wireless | IoT devices |

### Phase 4 — Driver & BSP Validation
Each peripheral driver is exercised to confirm the firmware correctly accesses hardware registers.

**Example validation flow for UART driver:**
1. Initialize UART at 115200 baud.
2. Transmit the string `"HELLO\r\n"`.
3. Verify on a terminal (PuTTY, Tera Term) that the string is received correctly.
4. Loop back RX to TX to test reception.
5. Stress-test with continuous streaming.

### Phase 5 — System-Level Integration Testing
Full system validation including RTOS task scheduling, inter-process communication, memory management, and end-to-end functionality.

## 2.2 Integration Approaches (Top-Down, Bottom-Up, Sandwich)

| Approach | Procedure | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Top-Down** | Start from main control module; use **stubs** to simulate lower-level hardware/firmware not yet ready. | Early detection of architectural flaws. | Stubs may mask real hardware issues. |
| **Bottom-Up** | Integrate lowest-level drivers first; progressively move up to application layer. | Real hardware exercised early. | High-level design flaws detected late. |
| **Sandwich / Hybrid** | Simultaneously integrate top and bottom, meeting at a target layer. | Balanced. Best for medium-complexity projects. | Requires careful planning of the "meeting layer." |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "compare integration approaches," use a table like above. Examiners award full marks when the *context* (firmware integration) is mentioned, not just generic software-engineering theory.

## 2.3 The Build Process — Engineering Detail

```
Source Files (.c, .h)
       │
       ▼
[Preprocessor] ──► Expanded .c with macros resolved
       │
       ▼
[Compiler] ──► Assembly (.s) ──► Object Files (.o)
       │
       ▼
[Assembler]
       │
       ▼
[Linker] ──► Combines .o + libraries + startup code ──► .elf
       │
       ▼
[Locator / objcopy] ──► Places sections in memory map ──► .hex / .bin
       │
       ▼
[Flash Programmer] ──► Loaded into target's ROM/Flash
```

**Key linker outputs:**
- **.text** — Program code (placed in Flash)
- **.data** — Initialized global/static variables (Flash → RAM at startup)
- **.bss** — Uninitialized variables (zeroed in RAM at startup)
- **.stack / .heap** — Dynamic memory regions in RAM

## 2.4 The Memory Map — Where Firmware Lives

A typical ARM Cortex-M4 microcontroller (e.g., STM32F407) has this memory layout:

| Address Range | Region | Contents |
|---|---|---|
| `0x0000_0000 – 0x0007_FFFF` | Flash / ROM | Firmware .text, .rodata, .data initializer |
| `0x2000_0000 – 0x2001_FFFF` | SRAM | .data, .bss, stack, heap |
| `0x4000_0000 – 0x5FFF_FFFF` | Peripheral registers | GPIO, UART, ADC, TIM registers |
| `0xE000_0000 – 0xE00F_FFFF` | System Control Space | NVIC, SysTick, Debug |

> [!NOTE]
> The **linker script** (`.ld` or `.scatter`) defines *exactly* where each section goes. A wrong memory map is a classic source of integration failure.

## 2.5 Hardware-Firmware Interface (HFI) Contract

The integration step is successful when the firmware's view of hardware (the **HFI contract**) matches reality:

| Aspect | What the Firmware Assumes | What the Hardware Must Provide |
|---|---|---|
| **Timing** | ADC conversion ≤ 12 µs | ADC completes within spec under all conditions |
| **Voltage** | GPIO high threshold = 2.0 V | Driver output ≥ 2.0 V at rated load |
| **Endianness** | Little-endian register reads | MCU designed in little-endian mode |
| **Pull-ups** | I2C SDA/SCL externally pulled high | External 4.7 kΩ resistors present on PCB |
| **Clock** | APB1 runs at 42 MHz | Crystal + PLL configured to deliver 42 MHz |

## 2.6 Common Integration Defects

| Defect Class | Example | Detection Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Timing violations** | I2C clock stretched beyond firmware timeout | Logic analyzer + GPIO toggle in ISR |
| **Pin mux mismatches** | Firmware configures PA2 as UART, PCB routes PA2 to SPI | Visual inspection + schematic diff |
| **Power sequencing** | Peripheral needs 3.3 V before 1.8 V; firmware inits both together | Oscilloscope on power rails during boot |
| **Memory map conflict** | Stack grows into `.bss` region | Linker map + stack-painting (filling RAM with `0xDEADBEEF`) |
| **Interrupt priority inversion** | Lower-priority ISR preempts higher one | NVIC configuration review + trace |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Walkthrough: Cross-Compilation and Loading of a "Blinky" Firmware

This is the canonical first integration exercise in any KTU embedded systems lab. We will exhaustively trace every step from `main.c` to a blinking LED on a STM32 board.

### Step 1 — Source Code (firmware developer writes)

```c
// main.c — Toggles PC13 LED every 500 ms
#define RCC_BASE   0x40023800UL
#define GPIOC_BASE 0x40020800UL
volatile unsigned int *RCC_AHB1ENR = (unsigned int *)(RCC_BASE + 0x30);
volatile unsigned int *GPIOC_MODER  = (unsigned int *)(GPIOC_BASE + 0x00);
volatile unsigned int *GPIOC_ODR    = (unsigned int *)(GPIOC_BASE + 0x14);

void delay(volatile unsigned int count) {
    while(count--);
}

int main(void) {
    *RCC_AHB1ENR |= (1U << 2);       // Enable GPIOC clock (bit 2)
    *GPIOC_MODER  |= (1U << 26);     // Set PC13 as output
    *GPIOC_MODER  &= ~(1U << 27);
    while(1) {
        *GPIOC_ODR ^= (1U << 13);    // Toggle PC13
        delay(1000000);
    }
}
```

### Step 2 — Preprocessing

The preprocessor expands:
- `#define RCC_BASE 0x40023800UL` → literal substitution
- Header files (`<stdint.h>`, etc.) inlined

Output: a single `.i` file with all macros resolved.

### Step 3 — Compilation to Assembly

`arm-none-eabi-gcc -c -mcpu=cortex-m4 -mthumb main.c -o main.o`

The compiler:
1. Lexical analysis → tokens
2. Parsing → Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)
3. Semantic analysis → type checking
4. Code generation → ARM Thumb-2 assembly

### Step 4 — Assembly to Object File

`arm-none-eabi-as -mcpu=cortex-m4 main.s -o main.o`

Object file contains machine code + symbol table + relocations (placeholders for unresolved addresses).

### Step 5 — Linking

`arm-none-eabi-ld -T stm32f4.ld main.o startup_stm32.o -o firmware.elf`

The linker:
- Resolves `*RCC_AHB1ENR` address by adding `RCC_BASE + 0x30` → concrete address `0x40023830`
- Merges sections (`.text`, `.data`, `.bss`)
- Assigns final memory addresses per linker script

### Step 6 — Locating / Object Copy

`arm-none-eabi-objcopy -O ihex firmware.elf firmware.hex`

Converts ELF (with debug info) to Intel HEX format (raw byte stream with addresses).

### Step 7 — Flashing via ST-Link (SWD)

Using `st-flash write firmware.hex 0x08000000`:
- The ST-Link probe, connected to the host via USB, drives the SWD lines (SWDIO, SWCLK).
- The probe sends a sequence: chip ID read → mass erase → page-by-page write of HEX records to Flash base `0x0800_0000`.

### Step 8 — Reset and Run

The probe issues an MCU reset. The Cortex-M4 begins execution at `0x0800_0000` (the reset vector in the Vector Table). The startup code (in `startup_stm32.o`) runs first:
1. Initialize stack pointer (`SP = *0x0800_0000`)
2. Copy `.data` from Flash to RAM
3. Zero `.bss` in RAM
4. Call `main()`

### Step 9 — Verification (Integration Test)

```
Test ID      : IT-001
Test Action  : Observe PC13 with oscilloscope
Expected     : Square wave, period 1.0 s ± 5%, duty 50%
Actual       : Square wave, 1.02 s, 49% duty
Result       : PASS
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The integration is successful** when the firmware's intent (toggle every 500 ms) produces the expected physical effect (square wave on the pin). This single test validates: power, clock, GPIO driver, linker map, Flash integrity, and reset vector.

## 3.2 Walkthrough: Debugging a Failed Integration

**Scenario:** The same firmware is loaded, but the LED does not blink. Voltage at PC13 is constant 0 V.

**Step-by-step debug procedure:**

| Step | Action | Tool | Finding |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Measure VDD (3.3 V) | Multimeter | OK |
| 2 | Measure oscillator at OSC_IN pin | Oscilloscope | 8 MHz present |
| 3 | Check reset pin during power-up | Oscilloscope | Stays low for 50 ms — OK |
| 4 | Halt MCU via debugger, inspect PC | GDB / IDE | PC = `0x0000_0000` (not `0x0800_0004`) |
| 5 | Inspect memory at `0x0800_0000` | Memory window in IDE | All `0xFF` — Flash empty |
| 6 | Re-flash with verbose output | `st-flash --verbose` | Reports "Write failed at 0x0801_0000" |
| 7 | Inspect PCB around `0x0801_0000` — corresponds to sector 4 | Schematic review | Sector 4 lock bit set in OPTCR register |
| 8 | Modify linker script to place `.text` in sector 0–3 only | Re-build + re-flash | PC13 blinks! |

> [!NOTE]
> **Lesson:** A perfectly written firmware, a perfectly manufactured PCB, and a successful flash operation can *still* fail integration due to a one-bit configuration register. This is why **integration is iterative, not a one-shot event**.

## 3.3 Walkthrough: Top-Down vs Bottom-Up in a Real Project

Consider a 3-layer embedded system: **App Layer → Driver Layer → Hardware**

| Phase | Top-Down | Bottom-Up |
|---|---|---|
| Week 1 | App + stubs that return hardcoded "sensor value" | Hardware powered, no firmware |
| Week 2 | Replace sensor stub with empty driver interface | UART, GPIO, ADC drivers tested individually |
| Week 3 | Replace driver stub with real driver | Integrate two drivers, test inter-driver data flow |
| Week 4 | System test of full stack | System test of full stack |

**Outcome in Top-Down:** Architectural defects in the App (e.g., "wrong data type from sensor") are caught in Week 1.
**Outcome in Bottom-Up:** Driver register errors are caught in Week 2, but App architecture issues are not found until Week 4.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU:** In exam answers, always state *which approach is preferred for firmware integration specifically*. Answer: **Bottom-Up** is preferred when hardware is ready; **Top-Down** is preferred when hardware is delayed (firmware team can work in parallel using stubs).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Integration Flow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Host PC Development Environment] --> B[Cross-Compiler Toolchain]
    B --> C[Source Code .c / .h]
    C --> D[Preprocessor]
    D --> E[Compiler]
    E --> F[Object Files .o]
    F --> G[Linker with Linker Script]
    G --> H[Executable ELF File]
    H --> I[OBJCOPY to HEX or BIN]
    I --> J[Flash Programmer ST-Link J-Link]
    J --> K[Target Hardware PCB]
    K --> L{Bootloader or Direct JTAG SWD}
    L --> M[Flash Memory Stores Firmware]
    M --> N[Reset Vector Execution]
    N --> O[Startup Code BSP Init]
    O --> P[Stack Pointer Setup]
    P --> Q[Copy .data from Flash to RAM]
    Q --> R[Zero .bss Section in RAM]
    R --> S[Call main]
    S --> T[Application Runs]
    T --> U[Integration Test Validation]
    U --> V{Tests Pass}
    V -- Yes --> W[System Validation Phase]
    V -- No --> X[Debug via JTAG SWD Breakpoints Trace]
    X --> S
```

## 4.2 Three-Layer Software Architecture Mapped to Hardware

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AppLayer [Application Layer]
        A1[main c]
        A2[State Machine]
        A3[Business Logic]
    end
    subgraph MidLayer [Middleware and RTOS]
        B1[FreeRTOS Scheduler]
        B2[Message Queues]
        B3[Memory Manager]
    end
    subgraph DriverLayer [Hardware Abstraction Layer HAL]
        C1[GPIO Driver]
        C2[UART Driver]
        C3[ADC Driver]
        C4[SPI Driver]
    end
    subgraph BoardLayer [Board Support Package BSP]
        D1[Clock Init PLL Setup]
        D2[Pin Mux Configuration]
        D3[Interrupt Vector Table]
        D4[Memory Map Definition]
    end
    subgraph HardwareLayer [Physical Hardware]
        E1[Processor Core]
        E2[Memory Flash and RAM]
        E3[Peripherals Sensors and Actuators]
    end
    AppLayer --> MidLayer
    MidLayer --> DriverLayer
    DriverLayer --> BoardLayer
    BoardLayer --> HardwareLayer
```

## 4.3 Integration Testing Methodology Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Integration Test Plan] --> B[Module A Standalone Test]
    A --> C[Module B Standalone Test]
    B --> D{A Pass}
    C --> E{B Pass}
    D -- Yes --> F[Integrate A and B]
    E -- Yes --> F
    D -- No --> G[Debug Module A]
    E -- No --> H[Debug Module B]
    G --> B
    H --> C
    F --> I[Interface Test]
    I --> J{Interface OK}
    J -- Yes --> K[Functional Test Combined]
    J -- No --> L[Debug Interface Contract]
    L --> F
    K --> M{Functional OK}
    M -- Yes --> N[Regression Test Full System]
    M -- No --> O[Debug Functional Logic]
    O --> F
    N --> P[Sign Off]
```

## 4.4 Build Process Detailed Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Src1[main c] --> Pre[Preprocess cpp]
    Src2[driver uart c] --> Pre
    Src3[startup s] --> Pre
    Hdr[header h] --> Pre
    Pre --> Comp[Compile gcc to assembly s]
    Comp --> Asm[Assemble as to object o]
    Asm --> Link[Link ld with linker script ld]
    Lib[libc a standard library] --> Link
    Link[Linker Script .ld] -.-> Link
    Link --> Elf[ELF Executable with debug symbols]
    Elf --> Strip[Optional strip debug info]
    Strip --> Copy[objcopy to ihex or bin]
    Copy --> Flash[Flash Programmer]
    Flash --> Mem[Target Flash Memory 0x08000000]
    Mem --> Boot[Bootloader reads and runs]
    Boot --> Startup[Startup Code Runs]
    Startup --> App[main Function Executes]
```

## 4.5 Hardware Bring-Up Power-On Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Apply Power to PCB] --> Check1{Voltage Rails OK}
    Check1 -- No --> FixP[Repair Power Circuit]
    Check1 -- Yes --> Check2{Clock Signal Present}
    Check2 -- No --> FixC[Check Crystal and Load Caps]
    Check2 -- Yes --> Check3{Reset Released}
    Check3 -- No --> FixR[Check Reset Supervisor]
    Check3 -- Yes --> Check4{Boot Pins Set Correct}
    Check4 -- No --> FixB[Reconfigure Boot Mode]
    Check4 -- Yes --> Connect[Connect Debugger SWD or JTAG]
    Connect --> ReadID[Read Device ID via SWD]
    ReadID --> Erase[Mass Erase Flash]
    Erase --> Flash[Write Firmware]
    Flash --> Verify[Verify Flash Content CRC]
    Verify --> Reset[Reset and Halt at main]
    Reset --> Run[Single Step and Verify Execution]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These diagrams serve as **board-exam-ready illustrations**. When answering a 14-mark question on integration, sketching even a simplified version of one of these flows earns you 2–3 evaluation marks for "diagrammatic representation."
