KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]` > Differentiate between an **Object File (`.o`)** and an **Executable File (`.elf`)**. List two key structural differences. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Attribute | Object File `.o` | Executable File `.elf` | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **ELF Type Field** | `ET_REL` (Relocatable) | `ET_EXEC` (Executable) | | **Symbol Resolution** | Contains unresolved external symbols (relocation entries) | All symbols resolved to absolute addresses | | **Address Space** | Base-relative, section-relative | Absolute addresses assigned by linker script | | **Runnability** | Cannot be loaded into memory and executed | Can be loaded into target flash/RAM and executed | | **Generation Stage** | Output of `arm-none-eabi-as` | Output of `arm-none-eabi-ld` | **Awarding marks:** 1 mark for ELF type, 1 mark for relocation, 1 mark for address nature. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` > Why is the `.bss` section **not** included in the Intel HEX file (`.hex`) even though it appears in the `.elf` file? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The `.bss` (Block Started by Symbol) section contains **uninitialized global and static variables** that, by the **C standard**, must be initialized to **zero** before program execution begins. The C runtime startup code (`_start` or `Reset_Handler` in the startup file) is responsible for zeroing the entire `.bss` region in RAM by executing a loop that writes zero to every byte in the `.bss` address range. Since storing a contiguous block of zeros in flash memory is wasteful (redundant), the toolchain **excludes** `.bss` from both `.bin` and `.hex` files. The `.elf` still records the `.bss` section's *virtual address and size* in its section header table, but no actual bytes are emitted in the flash image. The startup code uses this metadata to know *how much* RAM to zero out. **Awarding marks:** 1 mark for zero-init convention, 1 mark for startup code responsibility, 1 mark for flash-storage efficiency. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme, Module 4]` > **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **complete cross-compilation toolchain** for an ARM Cortex-M target. List **all file types** generated at each stage and state the GNU toolchain utility responsible for producing them. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** A bare-metal embedded project yields the following post-link section sizes: `.text` = 48 KB, `.rodata` = 6 KB, `.data` = 3 KB, `.bss` = 2 KB. Calculate the sizes of the **`.bin`** and **`.hex`** files produced. Justify why `.bss` is excluded. Mention the role of the **linker script** in this process. #### **Model Solution:** ##### **Part (a) — Toolchain Block Diagram & File Types [7 Marks]** The **ARM cross-compilation toolchain** consists of **five sequential stages**, each emitting a distinct file type: **Stage 1 — Preprocessing (utility: `arm-none-eabi-cpp` or `gcc -E`)** - **Input:** `.c` source files. - **Output:** `.i` file (preprocessed C). - **Operations:** Macro expansion, header inclusion, conditional compilation. **Stage 2 — Compilation (utility: `arm-none-eabi-gcc -S`)** - **Input:** `.i` file. - **Output:** `.s` file (assembly source). - **Operations:** Lexical → Syntax → Semantic analysis; code generation in target ISA (Thumb-2). **Stage 3 — Assembly (utility: `arm-none-eabi-as`)** - **Input:** `.s` file. - **Output:** `.o` file (relocatable object, ELF type `ET_REL`). - **Operations:** Mnemonic-to-opcode encoding, symbol table construction, relocation entry generation. **Stage 4 — Linking (utility: `arm-none-eabi-ld`)** - **Input:** Multiple `.o` files + linker script (`*.ld`). - **Output:** `.elf` file (executable, type `ET_EXEC`). - **Operations:** Section merging, symbol resolution, address relocation, section placement per memory map. - **Side-output:** `.map` file (memory map report) when `-Map=firmware.map` flag is used. **Stage 5 — Format Conversion (utility: `arm-none-eabi-objcopy`)** - **Input:** `.elf` file. - **Outputs:** `.bin` (raw binary) and `.hex` (Intel HEX). - **Operations:** Strips headers, debug info, and `.bss`; produces flash-ready image. **Block Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[.c files] -->|cpp| B[.i file] B -->|gcc -S| C[.s file] C -->|as| D[.o file] D -->|ld with linker.ld| E[.elf file plus .map file] E -->|objcopy -O binary| F[.bin file] E -->|objcopy -O ihex| G[.hex file] style A fill:#fef3c7 style E fill:#dcfce7 style F fill:#fee2e2 style G fill:#e0e7ff ``` *(Award 1 mark for each correctly described stage with file type; 1 mark for the diagram.)* ##### **Part (b) — Numerical Computation & Linker Script Role [7 Marks]** **Given:** - $S_{.text} = 48$ KB - $S_{.rodata} = 6$ KB - $S_{.data} = 3$ KB - $S_{.bss} = 2$ KB **Step 1: Compute `.bin` size [2 Marks]** $$ \begin{aligned} S_{.bin} & = S_{.text} + S_{.rodata} + S_{.data} \\ S_{.bin} & = 48 \text{ KB} + 6 \text{ KB} + 3 \text{ KB} \\ S_{.bin} & = 57 \text{ KB} = 58368 \text{ bytes} \end{aligned} $$ > The `.bss` section is **excluded** because it is **zero-initialized at runtime** by the startup code; storing zeros in flash would waste memory bandwidth and storage. **Step 2: Compute `.hex` size [2 Marks]** $$ \begin{aligned} S_{.hex} & \approx 2 \times S_{.bin} + \text{record overhead} \\ S_{.hex} & \approx 2 \times 58368 + \text{headers and EOF records} \\ S_{.hex} & \approx 116736 + 256 \\ S_{.hex} & \approx 117 \text{ KB} = 119808 \text{ bytes (approx.)} \end{aligned} $$ > The factor of **2.0× – 2.1×** arises because each byte is encoded as **2 ASCII hex characters**, plus `:`, address, type, and checksum overhead per record. **Step 3: Role of the Linker Script [3 Marks]** The **linker script** (e.g., `STM32F407VG.ld`) is a declarative text file that instructs the linker on: 1. **Memory region definitions** — `MEMORY { FLASH (rx) : ORIGIN = 0x08000000, LENGTH = 1M; RAM (rwx) : ORIGIN = 0x20000000, LENGTH = 128K; }` 2. **Section-to-region mapping** — assigning `.text`, `.rodata` → FLASH; `.data`, `.bss` → RAM. 3. **Symbol provision** — exporting `__StackTop`, `_sidata`, `_sdata`, `_edata`, `_sbss`, `_ebss` for the startup code to perform the **`.data` copy from Flash to RAM** and **`.bss` zeroing**. Without the linker script, the linker cannot determine *where* in physical memory each section must reside for the target microcontroller. --- ### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme, Module 4]` > **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Intel HEX file format** in detail. Draw the structure of a single record and compute the checksum for a record carrying the data bytes `0x10, 0x20, 0x30, 0x40` to be loaded at address `0x0800`. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast the **ELF**, **BIN**, and **HEX** file formats with respect to: (i) Symbol table presence, (ii) Debug info, (iii) Storage overhead, (iv) Typical use case. #### **Model Solution:** ##### **Part (a) — Intel HEX Format & Checksum Calculation [7 Marks]** **Intel HEX Record Structure [3 Marks]:** Each record is a single ASCII text line of the form: $$ \texttt{:LLAAAATT[DD...]CC} $$ | Field | Bytes | Description | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `:` | 1 | Start code (colon) | | `LL` | 1 | Byte count (number of data bytes) | | `AAAA` | 2 | Load address (big-endian, 16-bit) | | `TT` | 1 | Record type (00=data, 01=EOF, 02=ESA, 03=SSA, 04=ELA, 05=ELA) | | `DD` | N | Data bytes (N = LL) | | `CC` | 1 | Checksum (two's complement of sum of all preceding bytes) | **Record Type Table [1 Mark]:** - `00` → Data record - `01` → End-of-File record - `02` → Extended Segment Address - `04` → Extended Linear Address (used for >64 KB addressing) **Checksum Computation [3 Marks]:** For the record with: - Data bytes: `0x10, 0x20, 0x30, 0x40` - Load address: `0x0800` (split into high `0x08` and low `0x00`) - Byte count: `0x04` **Step 1:** Sum all bytes (excluding colon and checksum): $$ \begin{aligned} \Sigma & = LL + A_{high} + A_{low} + TT + D_1 + D_2 + D_3 + D_4 \\ \Sigma & = 0x04 + 0x08 + 0x00 + 0x00 + 0x10 + 0x20 + 0x30 + 0x40 \\ \Sigma & = 0x04 + 0x08 + 0x00 + 0x00 + 0x10 + 0x20 + 0x30 + 0x40 \end{aligned} $$ Adding byte-by-byte: $$ 0x04 + 0x08 = 0x0C \\ 0x0C + 0x00 = 0x0C \\ 0x0C + 0x00 = 0x0C \\ 0x0C + 0x10 = 0x1C \\ 0x1C + 0x20 = 0x3C \\ 0x3C + 0x30 = 0x6C \\ 0x6C + 0x40 = 0xAC $$ Therefore, $\Sigma = \text{0xAC} = 172$ in decimal. **Step 2:** Compute the two's complement (negation modulo 256): $$ \begin{aligned} CC & = (0x100 - \Sigma) \mod 0x100 \\ CC & = (0x100 - 0xAC) \mod 0x100 \\ CC & = 0x54 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Verify the checksum (sum of all bytes including CC must be 0x00, ignoring carry): $$ 0xAC + 0x54 = 0x100 \equiv 0x00 \pmod{0x100} \quad \checkmark $$ **Final HEX record (ASCII):** $$ \texttt{:04\,0800\,00\,10\,20\,30\,40\,54} $$ **Validation:** 4 data bytes, address 0x0800, type 00, checksum 0x54. ✔ --- ##### **Part (b) — ELF vs BIN vs HEX Comparison [7 Marks]** | Criterion | ELF `.elf` | BIN `.bin` | HEX `.hex` | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **(i) Symbol Table** | **Full `.symtab`** with function/variable names and addresses | **None** — purely raw bytes | **None** — only addresses and data | | **(ii) Debug Info** | **Full DWARF-3/5** sections (`.debug_info`, `.debug_line`, etc.) | **Stripped** during `objcopy` | **Stripped** during `objcopy` | | **(iii) Storage Overhead** | **Largest** (headers + symbols + debug + data) | **Smallest** (data only, no overhead) | **~2.1×** the `.bin` size (ASCII encoding) | | **(iv) Typical Use Case** | Source-level debugging via **GDB + OpenOCD**; symbolic backtraces in field crashes | Direct **flash programming** via `st-flash`, `J-Flash`, or `OpenOCD` | **UART/serial bootloader** programming (STM32 System Memory bootloader, AVR `avrdude`) | | **(v) Generation Tool** | `arm-none-eabi-ld` | `arm-none-eabi-objcopy -O binary` | `arm-none-eabi-objcopy -O ihex` | | **(vi) Human Readability** | Binary, but parseable by `readelf`, `objdump` | Pure binary (opaque) | ASCII text, parseable by `cat`/`less` | | **(vii) Contains `.bss`?** | Yes (in section header, no bytes) | **No** | **No** | **Awarding marks:** 1 mark per row × 7 criteria (capped at 7). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:** > 1. **Confusing `.o` and `.elf`**: Students often claim `.o` files are "executable" — they are NOT. The ELF type field must be checked (`ET_REL` vs `ET_EXEC`). > 2. **Forgetting that `.bss` has zero size in `.bin`**: Many students wrongly include `.bss` in their `.bin` size calculation, leading to off-by-2 KB or more errors. > 3. **Ignoring linker script role**: Examiners frequently award 2-3 marks for explaining *why* a linker script is mandatory; vague answers like "it links files" lose marks. > 4. **Writing Intel HEX records with lowercase hex or wrong checksum arithmetic**: The checksum must use **two's complement** (not one's complement, not bitwise NOT). Show all intermediate sums. > 5. **Mixing up `.lst` and `.map`**: A `.lst` is a per-source-file assembly listing; a `.map` is a whole-program memory map. Do not interchange the terms. > 6. **Stating that `.hex` files are "encrypted" or "compressed"**: They are NOT — they are merely **ASCII-encoded binary** for serial transport safety. > 7. **Omitting the role of startup code** in `.data` initialization (Flash-to-RAM copy) and `.bss` zeroing — this is a recurring 2-mark sub-question in KTU Module 4. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:** - **Cross-compilation** = build on **host** (x86), execute on **target** (ARM/AVR/PIC). - **Toolchain utilities (mnemonic: "CC-A-L-O")**: - **C**pp → `.i` - **C**ompiler (`-S`) → `.s` - **A**ssembler → `.o` - **L**inker → `.elf` (+ `.map`) - **O**bjcopy → `.bin` / `.hex` - **`.elf`** is the **master artifact** (with symbols + debug info). - **`.bin`** is the **raw flash image** (no headers, no `.bss`, no symbols). - **`.hex`** is the **ASCII-encoded version of `.bin`** with addresses (for serial bootloaders). - **`.bss` is NEVER in `.bin`/`.hex`** — it is zero-initialized at startup. - **`.data` exists in BOTH Flash (initial values) AND RAM (run-time copy)**. - **`.text` + `.rodata` → Flash** (read-only at runtime). - **Linker script** is **mandatory** for bare-metal embedded; it maps sections to physical memory regions. - **`.map`** = memory map report (audited for stack overflow and section placement). - **`.lst`** = source-to-assembly cross-reference (used for code optimization). - **Intel HEX checksum** = **two's complement** of byte sum; final record sum must equal `0x00` mod 256. - **STM32 memory map**: Flash at `0x08000000`, RAM at `0x20000000` (Cortex-M4 with FPU). - **ARM Cortex-M uses Thumb-2 instructions** (16-bit and 32-bit mixed), NOT classic 32-bit ARM mode. - **DWARF** is the debug info standard (DWARF-3 for ARM GCC 10.x, DWARF-5 for ARM GCC 12.x+). - **JTAG/SWD debuggers** use the `.elf` directly — they do NOT need `.bin` or `.hex` to start a debug session.

topics.title: Disassembler/Decompiler
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT523 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Disassembler/Decompiler

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — Disassembler & Decompiler in Embedded Systems

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition — Disassembler**
> A **disassembler** is a software utility that translates **machine code (binary instructions)** stored in an object file, firmware image, or memory dump back into its equivalent **human-readable assembly language mnemonics** (e.g., `MOV R0, #1`, `ADD R1, R2`, `B label`). It is precisely the **inverse operation of an assembler**, and it is a foundational tool in **reverse engineering (RE)** of embedded firmware.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition — Decompiler**
> A **decompiler** is a more advanced software tool that reconstructs **high-level source code** (typically C or C++) from compiled machine code or binary executables. It goes beyond a disassembler by attempting to recover **control flow, data types, function prototypes, variable names (where symbols exist), and higher-level constructs** such as loops, conditionals, and switch statements.

### 🔍 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you baked a cake (the compiled binary) from a recipe (the original C source). The cake contains all the ingredients, but you can no longer see the original recipe card.

- **Disassembler** = Looking at the cake and writing down a list of "1 cup flour added, 2 eggs mixed, baked at 180°C for 30 minutes" — i.e., low-level assembly steps. Very accurate, but hard to read.
- **Decompiler** = A skilled pastry chef tasting the cake and reconstructing the *original recipe* in plain English: "This is a sponge cake with buttercream frosting." It loses exact wording and some details, but the **logic and structure** are recovered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters in Embedded Systems (KTU High-Yield Context)**
> - Firmware stored in **ROM, Flash, or EEPROM** of microcontrollers (e.g., ARM Cortex-M, AVR, PIC) is **not human-readable** — only a disassembler can make it readable.
> - Used for **security auditing, malware analysis, vulnerability discovery, bootloader reverse engineering, and IP theft detection** in IoT/embedded devices.
> - Tools used in industry: **IDA Pro, Ghidra, Binary Ninja, objdump, radare2, RetDec**.

---

### 🔑 Disassembler vs Decompiler — Quick Glance

| Aspect | Disassembler | Decompiler |
|---|---|---|
| Output Language | Assembly (low-level) | High-level (C/C++/Java) |
| Reversal Level | Machine code → ASM | Machine code → Source |
| Information Loss | Very low | Moderate to high |
| Computation Complexity | Low–Medium | Very High (uses compilers techniques) |
| Industry Examples | `objdump`, `IDA`, `Ghidra` | `Hex-Rays`, `Ghidra Decompiler`, `RetDec` |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept of a Disassembler — Step-by-Step Logic

A disassembler performs these structured logical steps:

1. **Input Acquisition** — Reads the binary file (ELF, HEX, BIN, COFF, Mach-O, or raw memory dump).
2. **Format Parsing** — Identifies the file format and extracts the **.text section** (code) along with entry points.
3. **Instruction Decoding** — Reads **opcode bytes** one-by-one, looks them up in the **ISA (Instruction Set Architecture) reference table**, and resolves operands.
4. **Symbol Resolution** — If a symbol table (e.g., from `gcc -g`) is present, it replaces addresses with function/variable names.
5. **Control-Flow Graph (CFG) Construction** *(optional, recursive disassemblers)* — Identifies branch targets to correctly split basic blocks.
6. **Mnemonic Emission** — Outputs the assembly listing in a standardized format (Intel or AT&T syntax).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Major Disassembly Algorithms — Must Know for KTU**
> - **Linear Sweep Disassembler:** Starts at the first byte and decodes sequentially. Fast but fails when **data is interleaved with code** (e.g., jump tables, alignment bytes in ARM Thumb).
> - **Recursive Traversal Disassembler:** Starts from known entry points and follows branch/jump instructions. More accurate; handles mixed code-data correctly. Used by **IDA Pro** and **Ghidra**.

## 2.2 Operational Concept of a Decompiler — Multi-Phase Pipeline

A modern decompiler works in **five distinct phases** (this is a classic KTU 14-mark question topic):

1. **Loading & Front-End Parsing** — Binary is parsed, sections identified.
2. **Disassembly Stage** — Uses a recursive disassembler to obtain assembly.
3. **Intermediate Representation (IR) Translation** — Assembly is converted to a **Three-Address Code (TAC)** or SSA-based IR.
4. **Analysis & Transformation** — Performs **data-flow analysis**, **type inference**, **variable propagation**, **expression simplification**, and **control-flow structuring** (if/while/for recovery).
5. **Code Generation** — Emits pseudo-C code.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Disassembler & Decompiler

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarises all critical formulas, sizes, and metrics for this topic. **Memorize this for KTU exams.**

| # | Concept / Metric | Formula / Definition | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Instruction Length (fixed ISA) | $L_{inst} = \text{constant}$ | bits (e.g., ARM = **32 bits**) |
| 2 | Instruction Length (variable ISA) | $L_{inst} \in \{L_{min}, L_{max}\}$ | x86: 1–15 bytes |
| 3 | Disassembly Speed (Linear Sweep) | $T_{LS} = \dfrac{N \cdot \bar{L}}{R_{decode}}$ | N = number of bytes |
| 4 | CFG Basic Block Count | $B = V - E + C$ | V = vertices, E = edges, C = connected components |
| 5 | Cyclomatic Complexity | $M = E - V + 2P$ | P = number of connected components (= 1 for single function) |
| 6 | Code Coverage Metric | $\text{Cov} = \dfrac{\text{Decoded Bytes}}{\text{Total .text Bytes}} \times 100$ | Percent (%) |
| 7 | Decompilation Recovery Ratio | $R_r = \dfrac{\text{Recovered Constructs}}{\text{Original Constructs}}$ | $0 \le R_r \le 1$ |
| 8 | Memory Address Calculation | $A_{target} = A_{branch} \pm \text{offset}$ | Bytes offset |
| 9 | Branch Target Resolution | $T = PC + 4 + (\text{Signed Offset} \times 4)$ | ARM/Thumb offset, in bytes |
| 10 | Total Binary Size | $S_{bin} = S_{text} + S_{data} + S_{bss} + S_{rodata}$ | Bytes |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **IoT Security** | Auditing firmware of smart devices for backdoors |
| **Forensics** | Analyzing malware embedded in industrial control systems (ICS/SCADA) |
| **Automotive** | Reverse engineering ECU firmware for vulnerability research |
| **Aerospace** | Verifying compiled binaries against certified source code |
| **Academia** | Teaching compiler design by examining the *reverse* process |
| **IP Protection** | Detecting unauthorized reuse of proprietary embedded code |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Step-by-Step Content** — No step skipping. Every transition explicitly explained.

## 3.1 Worked Example — Disassembling a Simple C Function (ARM Cortex-M)

### Original C Source Code

```c
int add_twice(int a, int b) {
    int sum = a + b;
    return sum * 2;
}
```

### Step 1 — Compilation Output (Genuine Assembly from `arm-none-eabi-gcc -O0`)

```asm
add_twice:
    push    {r7}
    sub     sp, #4
    add     r7, sp, #0
    str     r0, [r7, #4]     ; store a
    str     r1, [r7, #0]     ; store b
    ldr     r3, [r7, #4]     ; r3 = a
    ldr     r2, [r7, #0]     ; r2 = b
    add     r3, r3, r2       ; r3 = a + b
    str     r3, [r7, #0]     ; sum = a + b
    ldr     r3, [r7, #0]
    lsl     r3, r3, #1       ; r3 = sum * 2
    mov     r0, r3           ; return r3
    mov     sp, r7
    pop     {r7}
    bx      lr
```

### Step 2 — Raw Machine Code (Hex Dump)

```
0x08000000: 80 b5       push {r7}
0x08000002: 4b 4b 00 20 add r7, sp, #0
...
```

### Step 3 — Disassembler Reading the Binary

| Step | Action | Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Read 2 bytes `0x80B5` | Look up in ARM ISA → `PUSH {r7}` |
| 2 | Read 4 bytes `0x4B4B0020` | Decode → `ADD R7, SP, #0` |
| 3 | Continue linearly (Linear Sweep) | Decodes each instruction |
| 4 | Resolve register names | `R0` → first argument `a` |
| 5 | Emit listing | Assembly text printed to screen |

### Step 4 — Decompiler Reconstruction (Output of Ghidra/Hex-Rays)

```c
int add_twice(int a, int b)
{
  int local_c;
  local_c = a + b;
  return local_c * 2;
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the decompiler has **lost the original variable names** `sum` and replaced it with `local_c`. This is because the original ELF had no debug symbols (compiled with `gcc` instead of `gcc -g`).

## 3.2 Detailed Decompilation Pipeline — Step-by-Step Walkthrough

Let us derive each stage for the above `add_twice` function.

### Stage 1 — Loading
The binary file (ELF format) is loaded. The tool identifies:
- **Entry Point:** `0x08000000`
- **.text section size:** `64 bytes`
- **Architecture:** `ARM Cortex-M3`

### Stage 2 — Disassembly
Using **Recursive Traversal**, starting from `add_twice`:
- Follows `BX LR` exit branch
- Does not decode data regions
- Produces clean assembly listing

### Stage 3 — Intermediate Representation (IR) Generation

Each assembly instruction is translated to **Three-Address Code (TAC)**:

$$\text{IR}_1: \ t_1 = \text{add}(a, b)$$

$$\text{IR}_2: \ t_2 = \text{lshift}(t_1, 1)$$

$$\text{IR}_3: \ \text{RETURN} \ t_2$$

### Stage 4 — Analysis & Transformation

**4.1 Type Inference:**
- `t_1` is assigned type `int` (from operand sizes, 32-bit registers).
- `t_2` is also `int` (shift result of int is int).

**4.2 Expression Simplification:**

The compiler backend may express `*2` as a left-shift, but the decompiler recovers the original intent:

$$t_2 = t_1 \ll 1 \quad \longrightarrow \quad t_2 = t_1 \times 2$$

**4.3 Control-Flow Structuring:**
- Detects single-entry, single-exit region.
- No loops, no conditionals → linear function.

### Stage 5 — Code Generation

Pseudo-C is emitted using a printer module. Final output (as shown in Step 4 above).

## 3.3 Cyclomatic Complexity Derivation (Worked)

Given a function with **V = 8** basic blocks, **E = 10** edges, single connected component **P = 1**:

$$M = E - V + 2P$$

$$M = 10 - 8 + 2(1)$$

$$M = 4$$

This indicates **4 independent paths** in the function — useful for **reverse engineering** and **test case generation** in security audits.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Linear Sweep vs Recursive Disassembler

| Parameter | Linear Sweep | Recursive Traversal |
|---|---|---|
| **Decoding Strategy** | Sequential byte stream | Follows control flow |
| **Handles Data in Code** | ❌ Fails | ✅ Correctly skips |
| **Speed** | Very Fast | Slower |
| **Accuracy on ARM Thumb** | Poor | Excellent |
| **Industry Tools** | `objdump -d` (default) | `IDA Pro`, `Ghidra`, `Binary Ninja` |
| **Use Case** | Quick inspection | Deep RE & security |

## 3.5 Lossy Recovery — Quantitative Analysis

Original variable `sum` is lost; let:

$$L_{recover} = 1 - R_r$$

Where $R_r$ is the recovery ratio. If only **1 of 3** original symbols (`a`, `b`, `sum`) is recovered (function arguments), then:

$$R_r = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$

$$L_{recover} = 1 - 0.333 = 0.667$$

This means **66.7% of naming information is lost** without symbol tables. **KTU exam tip:** always compile with `-g` to preserve symbols.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Disassembly Workflow — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Binary Firmware File] --> B[File Format Parser ELF HEX BIN]
    B --> C[Section Identification text data rodata]
    C --> D[Opcode Decoder ISA Lookup Table]
    D --> E[Disassembly Algorithm Linear or Recursive]
    E --> F[Symbol Table Resolver]
    F --> G[Assembly Mnemonic Emitter]
    G --> H[Final Assembly Listing text file]
    F --> I[CFG Builder for Recursive]
    I --> J[Basic Block Identification]
    J --> K[Function Boundary Detection]
```

## 4.2 Decompilation Pipeline — Five-Stage Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Stage1 Load Binary] --> A2[Stage2 Disassemble]
    A2 --> A3[Stage3 IR Generation TAC SSA]
    A3 --> A4[Stage4 Analysis Type CFG Dataflow]
    A4 --> A5[Stage5 Code Generation Pseudo C]
    A5 --> A6[Recovered High Level Source]
    A3 -.feedback.-> A4
    A4 -.feedback.-> A5
```

## 4.3 Reverse Engineering Workflow for Embedded Firmware

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Acquire Firmware Dump from Flash] --> S2[Identify Architecture ARM AVR PIC]
    S2 --> S3[Load into Disassembler IDA Ghidra]
    S3 --> S4[Manual RE of Entry Point and ISR]
    S4 --> S5[Apply Decompiler for High Level View]
    S5 --> S6[Annotate and Rename Variables]
    S6 --> S7[Document Findings Security Report]
    S7 --> S8{Vulnerability Found}
    S8 -- Yes --> S9[Patch and Re flash]
    S8 -- No --> S10[Archive RE Report]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture of a Typical Decompiler (Ghidra-style)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FE[Front End]
        FE1[ELF MachO Parser] --> FE2[Section Extractor]
    end
    subgraph CORE[Decompiler Core]
        C1[Disassembler Engine] --> C2[Pcode IR Translator]
        C2 --> C3[Data Flow Analyzer]
        C3 --> C4[Type Inference Engine]
        C4 --> C5[Control Flow Structurer]
    end
    subgraph BE[Back End]
        C5 --> BE1[C Code Printer]
        BE1 --> BE2[Syntax Highlighting UI]
    end
    FE --> C1
```

## 4.5 Disassembler Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is debug symbol table present?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2[Use Recursive Disassembler with symbols]
    Q1 -- No --> Q3[Use Recursive Disassembler with manual entry points]
    Q3 --> Q4{Is data interleaved with code?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5[Apply heuristic byte classification]
    Q4 -- No --> Q6[Use Linear Sweep for speed]
    Q2 --> OUT[Generate Assembly]
    Q5 --> OUT
    Q6 --> OUT
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between a disassembler and a decompiler. List two tools for each.**

**Model Answer:**
A disassembler converts machine code into **assembly language mnemonics** (low-level), whereas a decompiler converts machine code into **high-level source code** (typically C/C++). A disassembler preserves all machine-level details but produces hard-to-read output; a decompiler produces readable pseudo-code but loses some details like exact variable names.

**Tools:**
- Disassemblers: **IDA Pro**, `objdump`, Ghidra
- Decompilers: **Hex-Rays (IDA plugin)**, Ghidra Decompiler, RetDec

> [!NOTE]
> *Key differentiator examiners look for:* "Assembly" vs "High-level language" output.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**What is the difference between linear sweep and recursive traversal disassembly? Which is more accurate and why?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Linear Sweep:** Decodes instructions sequentially from the first byte to the last, without considering control flow. It is **fast** but **fails on embedded data within code** (e.g., jump tables, alignment padding).
- **Recursive Traversal:** Begins at known entry points and follows branch/jump instructions to identify valid code paths. It is **slower** but **far more accurate**, since it correctly skips data sections.

Recursive traversal is more accurate, especially for **variable-length ISAs** like x86 and **mixed-mode ARM/Thumb** code in embedded systems.

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the working of a **disassembler** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the **linear sweep** and **recursive traversal** algorithms in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A disassembler is a reverse-engineering tool that converts machine-readable binary code into human-readable assembly language. It is the **inverse of an assembler**.

**Working Steps:**
1. **Input:** Accepts a binary file (ELF, BIN, HEX, COFF, etc.).
2. **Parsing:** Identifies the file format and extracts the **.text** section containing code.
3. **Decoding:** Reads bytes sequentially or based on control flow and maps each opcode to its assembly mnemonic using the target **ISA reference table**.
4. **Symbol Resolution:** If debug symbols are present, replaces addresses with function/variable names.
5. **Output:** Generates an assembly listing in Intel or AT&T syntax.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Binary Input] --> B[Parser]
    B --> C[Opcode Decoder]
    C --> D[Algorithm Linear or Recursive]
    D --> E[Symbol Resolver]
    E --> F[Assembly Output]
```

**Linear Sweep Algorithm:**
- Begins at the lowest address of the .text section.
- Decodes each instruction in sequence, advancing by the instruction's length.
- **Advantage:** Extremely fast, simple to implement.
- **Disadvantage:** Fails on data embedded in code (e.g., jump tables, function pointers, alignment bytes in ARM Thumb).

**Recursive Traversal Algorithm:**
- Begins at a known entry point (e.g., reset vector, exported function).
- Decodes the instruction; if it is a **branch (B, BL, BX)**, recursively follows the target address.
- Maintains a **visited instruction set** to prevent infinite loops.
- **Advantage:** Highly accurate; properly separates code from data.
- **Disadvantage:** Slower; cannot recover unreachable code (dead code).

**Comparison:**

| Feature | Linear Sweep | Recursive Traversal |
|---|---|---|
| Approach | Sequential | Branch-following |
| Data Handling | Poor | Excellent |
| Speed | Fast | Slower |
| Tools | `objdump` | IDA, Ghidra |

---

**Q. (b)** Describe the **five phases of a decompiler** with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A decompiler reconstructs high-level source code from machine code by performing five well-defined phases:

**Phase 1 — Loading / Front-End Parsing:**
The binary file is loaded; the format (ELF, PE, Mach-O) is identified, and relevant sections (.text, .data, .rodata) are extracted. The **ISA** (e.g., ARM, x86) is determined.

**Phase 2 — Disassembly:**
A recursive disassembler is invoked to obtain an accurate assembly listing.

**Phase 3 — Intermediate Representation (IR) Generation:**
The assembly instructions are translated into a **platform-independent IR**, such as **Three-Address Code (TAC)** or **Static Single Assignment (SSA)** form. This decouples analysis from the source ISA.

Example: `ADD r3, r1, r2` becomes `t1 = r1 + r2`.

**Phase 4 — Analysis & Transformation:**
Multiple analyses run on the IR:
- **Data-Flow Analysis:** Tracks how values propagate.
- **Type Inference:** Recovers the original data types of variables.
- **Control-Flow Structuring:** Converts low-level CFGs into high-level constructs (`if`, `while`, `for`, `switch`).
- **Expression Simplification:** Rewrites patterns like `x << 1` back to `x * 2`.

**Phase 5 — Code Generation:**
The structured IR is printed as pseudo-C code (or C++, Java, etc.), with syntax highlighting and comments.

**Pipeline Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Load] --> A2[Disassemble] --> A3[IR Gen] --> A4[Analysis] --> A5[Code Gen]
    A3 -.feedback.-> A4
    A4 -.feedback.-> A5
```

**Real-world Example:** The **Ghidra** decompiler (developed by the NSA) uses a custom IR called **P-code**, which then undergoes the above phases to produce C output.

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]**

**Q. (a)** With a suitable example, explain how a **C function is reverse engineered** using a disassembler and decompiler. Show the original C code, generated assembly, and recovered pseudo-C output. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Original C Source:**

```c
int square_diff(int x, int y) {
    int diff = x - y;
    return diff * diff;
}
```

**Generated ARM Assembly:**

```asm
square_diff:
    push    {r7}
    sub     sp, #8
    add     r7, sp, #0
    str     r0, [r7, #4]      ; store x
    str     r1, [r7, #0]      ; store y
    ldr     r3, [r7, #4]
    ldr     r2, [r7, #0]
    sub     r3, r3, r2        ; r3 = x - y
    str     r3, [r7, #0]
    ldr     r3, [r7, #0]
    mul     r3, r3, r3        ; r3 = (x-y) * (x-y)
    mov     r0, r3
    mov     sp, r7
    pop     {r7}
    bx      lr
```

**Hex Dump (Disassembler Input):**

```
0x08000100: 80 B5
0x08000102: 4F 4B 00 AF
0x08000106: 4B 4B 01 90
...
```

**Disassembler Output (`objdump -d`):**

```asm
08000100 <square_diff>:
 8000100: 80b5            push  {r7}
 8000102: af00            add   r7, sp, #0
 8000106: 9001            str   r1, [r0, #4]
 ...
```

**Decompiler Output (Ghidra):**

```c
int square_diff(int x, int y)
{
  int diff = x - y;
  return diff * diff;
}
```

The decompiler successfully recovers the **logic** but variable name `diff` is preserved only if **debug symbols** are included. Otherwise it becomes `local_8` or similar.

---

**Q. (b)** Compare the use of disassemblers/decompilers in **IoT security auditing** vs **academic compiler education**. List three challenges faced in each domain. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Domain 1: IoT Security Auditing**

Use cases:
1. Auditing firmware of smart home devices, wearables, and industrial IoT sensors.
2. Detecting hard-coded credentials, cryptographic keys, or backdoors in firmware.
3. Analyzing malware targeting embedded Linux or RTOS systems.

**Challenges:**
1. **Obfuscation:** Vendors often strip symbols and use packers/custom encryption.
2. **Hardware Dependency:** Some code interacts with custom peripherals, making decompilation ambiguous.
3. **Legal/Ethical Constraints:** Reverse engineering may violate DMCA or local laws without proper authorization.

**Domain 2: Academic Compiler Education**

Use cases:
1. Demonstrating the **inverse** of the compilation process in compiler design courses.
2. Teaching optimization analysis (e.g., observing how `-O2` transforms loops).
3. Understanding **intermediate representations** (LLVM IR, P-code, TAC).

**Challenges:**
1. **Abstraction Loss:** Decompiled code is not identical to the original, confusing beginners.
2. **Platform Specificity:** Students must learn the target ISA (ARM, x86, RISC-V).
3. **Tool Learning Curve:** Tools like IDA Pro have steep learning curves; open-source alternatives (Ghidra) require setup effort.

| Aspect | IoT Security | Compiler Education |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Goal | Find vulnerabilities | Understand compilation |
| Output Priority | Accuracy | Clarity |
| Common Tools | IDA Pro, Ghidra | Ghidra, RetDec |
| Audience | Security professionals | B.Tech/M.Tech students |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing "assembler" with "disassembler":** Assembler = source → binary; Disassembler = binary → source. Marks deducted if reversed.
> 2. **Skipping the algorithm names:** Always explicitly mention **Linear Sweep** and **Recursive Traversal** when discussing disassembly — this is a recurring 7-mark sub-question.
> 3. **Forgetting the 5 phases of decompilation:** Examiners expect all five: *Load → Disassemble → IR Gen → Analysis → Code Gen*. Missing even one loses a mark.
> 4. **No diagrams = lost marks:** Always include a block diagram or pipeline diagram for 7-mark questions.
> 5. **Not giving tools/examples:** Mentioning real tools (Ghidra, IDA Pro, objdump) shows depth and earns full marks.
> 6. **Mixing up decompiler output with original source:** Always clarify that decompiled code is *approximate*, not identical.

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize These Bullet Points**

- ✅ **Disassembler** = Binary → **Assembly language** (low-level, accurate)
- ✅ **Decompiler** = Binary → **High-level source code** (C/C++, approximate)
- ✅ Disassembler is the **inverse of an assembler**
- ✅ **Linear Sweep** = fast, sequential, fails on data-in-code
- ✅ **Recursive Traversal** = accurate, follows branches, used in IDA/Ghidra
- ✅ **5 Phases of Decompilation:** Load → Disassemble → IR Generation → Analysis/Transformation → Code Generation
- ✅ **Intermediate Representation (IR):** Three-Address Code (TAC) or SSA — used to decouple analysis from the source ISA
- ✅ **Ghidra (NSA)** uses a custom IR called **P-code**
- ✅ **Cyclomatic Complexity:** $M = E - V + 2P$
- ✅ **Recovery Ratio:** $R_r = \dfrac{\text{Recovered}}{\text{Original}}$; without `-g` debug symbols, ~60–70% naming info is lost
- ✅ Compiling with `gcc -g` preserves symbol tables for better disassembly
- ✅ Real-world tools: **IDA Pro, Ghidra, Binary Ninja, objdump, radare2, RetDec, Hex-Rays**
- ✅ Embedded use cases: Firmware RE, IoT security, automotive ECU auditing, malware analysis, IP protection
- ✅ Decompilers **cannot** recover comments, original variable names, or preprocessor macros
- ✅ Always produce a **block diagram or pipeline diagram** in 14-mark answers — examiners reward visual aids
- ✅ Mention **ARM Cortex-M** or **AVR** examples for embedded-specific context to earn bonus marks

---

### 📚 End of Module 4 Notes — Disassembler/Decompiler

*Prepared under KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 aligned, OBE-compliant.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT523 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Simulators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

A **Simulator** in the context of embedded systems is a software-based emulation environment that models the behavioral characteristics of a target microcontroller/processor (and optionally its peripheral set) on a host development workstation. It executes the compiled object code (or a high-level representation) of the embedded firmware instruction-by-instruction, while maintaining a software replica of the CPU registers, memory map, program counter, stack pointer, status flags, and interrupt vectors.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, simulators belong to the broader category of **Host-Target Development Tools** and serve as a critical pre-silicon and pre-hardware validation layer in the embedded software development life-cycle (SDLC).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 4):**
> Simulators are classified as **software-only debugging tools**, distinct from Emulators (which use actual hardware bond-out chips) and Debuggers (e.g., JTAG-based, which require physical hardware access).

### Types of Simulators (KTU Classification)

| Simulator Type | Granularity | Typical Use-Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Instruction Set Simulator (ISS)** | Instruction-level | Functional verification of algorithms |
| **Cycle-Accurate Simulator** | Clock-cycle level | Performance analysis & timing studies |
| **Full-System Simulator** | System-level (CPU + Peripherals + Buses) | Hardware-Software co-validation |
| **RTL / Hardware Simulator** | Logic-gate level | Pre-silicon firmware testing (e.g., on FPGA prototypes) |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Flight Simulator" for Chips:**
> Think of an embedded simulator exactly like a **flight simulator used to train pilots**. A pilot practices take-off, navigation, and emergency landings inside a computer-generated cockpit *before* ever flying a real aircraft. Similarly, an embedded developer "flies" their firmware inside a software-generated virtual microcontroller *before* burning the code onto real silicon.
> 
> Just as a flight simulator models altitude, wind, and engine RPM, the embedded simulator models **registers, memory, timers, I/O pins, and interrupts** — letting the developer catch bugs *without risking damage to expensive hardware*.

### Why Simulators Are Used in Industry

- **Zero hardware risk:** Buggy code cannot physically damage the target board.
- **Cost-effective:** No need to procure prototype boards for every developer.
- **Deterministic replay:** Bugs can be reproduced exactly the same way, every time.
- **Early development:** Firmware can be written before silicon is fabricated (FPGA-based prototypes + ISS).
- **Instrumentation:** Provides visibility into internal CPU state (registers, flags) that physical oscilloscopes cannot show.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Internal Architecture of a Typical Simulator

A simulator internally comprises **five tightly coupled modules**:

1. **Instruction Decoder / Fetcher**
   - Reads binary opcodes from the simulated program memory.
   - Decodes the opcode into an internal micro-operation representation.

2. **Register File Model**
   - A software array mirroring the physical CPU registers (R0–R7, PC, SP, PSW, etc.).
   - Updated atomically on every instruction execution.

3. **Memory Model**
   - A linear or segmented addressable byte/word array simulating RAM, ROM, SFRs (Special Function Registers), and external memory.
   - Emulates endianness and alignment rules of the target CPU.

4. **Execution Engine**
   - Implements the **fetch-decode-execute-store** pipeline in software.
   - May operate in *functional mode* (fast) or *cycle-accurate mode* (slow but timing-true).

5. **Peripheral / I/O Model**
   - Software stubs that mimic hardware peripherals (UART, ADC, Timers, GPIO).
   - Accepts stimuli from a virtual environment and produces virtual responses.

### Operational Flow (Fetch–Decode–Execute Cycle)

The simulator loops through these steps for every virtual clock tick or instruction:

- **Step 1 — FETCH:** Read the opcode at the address held in the simulated **Program Counter (PC)**.
- **Step 2 — DECODE:** Identify the instruction class (arithmetic, logical, branch, memory, control).
- **Step 3 — OPERAND FETCH:** Read source operands from the simulated register file or memory.
- **Step 4 — EXECUTE:** Perform the operation in software (using host CPU arithmetic).
- **Step 5 — WRITE-BACK:** Store results back into destination registers/memory.
- **Step 6 — PC UPDATE:** Increment PC (or load branch target) and loop back to Step 1.
- **Step 7 — INTERRUPT CHECK:** Service any pending virtual interrupts or hardware events.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** Always draw the **fetch–decode–execute cycle** as a closed loop when asked to explain how a simulator works. Examiners award full marks for showing the cyclic nature of the loop.

### KTU Formula / Metric Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Expression | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Simulated Cycles | $N_c$ | Number of virtual clock cycles executed | cycles |
| Simulated Time | $T_{sim} = \dfrac{N_c}{f_{clk}}$ | Time represented in the virtual world | seconds |
| Host Execution Time | $T_{host}$ | Wall-clock time taken on the host PC | seconds |
| Simulation Speed Ratio | $R = \dfrac{T_{sim}}{T_{host}}$ | How much virtual time is covered per real second | dimensionless |
| Slowdown Factor | $S = \dfrac{1}{R}$ | Inverse of speed ratio (typical ISS: $S \approx 10^3$–$10^6$) | dimensionless |
| MIPS Equivalence | $M = \dfrac{N_{inst}}{T_{host} \times 10^6}$ | Million simulated instructions per host second | MIPS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A typical cycle-accurate simulator runs **1,000 to 1,000,000 times slower** than real hardware. This is why it is used for **functional correctness** and small code paths, not for full benchmark runs.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECU development (ISO 26262):** Used in Model-in-the-Loop (MIL) and Software-in-the-Loop (SIL) testing before Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL).
- **IoT firmware prototyping:** Tools like **QEMU** emulate ARM Cortex-M cores on Linux hosts.
- **Avionics & Medical devices:** DO-178C and IEC 62304 certified toolchains rely heavily on ISS for unit testing.
- **Academic instruction:** Tools like **Keil µVision Simulator**, **MPLAB X SIM**, and **Renesas e² Studio Simulator** are mandated in KTU lab curricula.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Simulated Time per Instruction

Let us derive the simulated time consumed by **one instruction** running on a target microcontroller clocked at $f_{clk}$ Hz.

Assume the target CPU executes $CPI$ (Cycles Per Instruction) clock cycles per machine instruction on average.

$$
\text{Cycles consumed} = CPI
$$

The clock period of the target is:

$$
T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}}
$$

Therefore, the simulated (virtual) time consumed per instruction is:

$$
T_{inst} = CPI \times T_{clk} = \frac{CPI}{f_{clk}}
$$

For an entire program of $N$ instructions:

$$
T_{sim} = N \times T_{inst} = \frac{N \times CPI}{f_{clk}}
$$

This is the canonical relationship used in every embedded timing analysis.

### Derivation 2: Simulation Speed Ratio

Let the host workstation execute the simulator's $C$-instruction interpreter loop in $T_{host}$ seconds of wall-clock time. The number of host cycles consumed is:

$$
N_{host} = f_{host} \times T_{host}
$$

The number of *target* cycles simulated in that interval is $N_c$. The simulation speed ratio is therefore:

$$
R = \frac{N_c / f_{clk}}{T_{host}} = \frac{N_c}{f_{clk} \times T_{host}}
$$

Equivalently:

$$
R = \frac{T_{sim}}{T_{host}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a cycle-accurate ARM ISS running on a 3 GHz host simulating a 168 MHz STM32 target, typical $R \approx 10^{-4}$, meaning 1 second of host time ≈ 100 µs of simulated time. This is the **slowdown factor** that limits simulator-based testing of long-duration real-time code.

### Derivation 3: Memory Footprint of the Register File Model

A simulator for an n-bit CPU with $r$ general-purpose registers plus $s$ special registers (PC, SP, PSW, etc.) requires:

$$
M_{reg} = (r + s) \times \frac{n}{8} \text{ bytes}
$$

For a typical 8051 simulator ($r=8$, $s=10$, $n=8$):

$$
M_{reg} = (8 + 10) \times \frac{8}{8} = 18 \text{ bytes}
$$

For an ARM Cortex-M simulator ($r=13$, $s=4$, $n=32$):

$$
M_{reg} = (13 + 4) \times \frac{32}{8} = 68 \text{ bytes}
$$

### Analytical Matrix: Simulator vs. Emulator vs. Debugger

| Parameter | Simulator | Emulator | Hardware Debugger (JTAG/BDM) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hardware Required** | None (pure software) | Bond-out chip + special hardware | Target board + probe |
| **Real-time Execution** | No (offline / slowed) | Yes (full-speed) | Yes (full-speed) |
| **Visibility into CPU State** | Total (every cycle) | Total (via bond-out) | Limited (via breakpoints) |
| **Peripheral Realism** | Low–Medium (modeled) | High (real silicon) | Real (actual silicon) |
| **Cost** | **Low** (free/cheap) | **High** (₹₹₹) | **Medium** (probe cost) |
| **Speed** | Slow (10³–10⁶× slower) | Real-time | Real-time |
| **Best For** | Early code dev, unit tests | Hardware validation | Production debugging |
| **KTU Lab Tool Example** | Keil µVision Simulator | None in typical curriculum | JTAG via ULINK |

### Derivation 4: Slowdown for a Realistic KTU Lab Example

**Problem (KTU-style):** An 8051 firmware consists of $N = 10{,}000$ instructions with an average $CPI = 2$, running on a target clocked at $f_{clk} = 12$ MHz. The host simulator executes at $f_{host} = 3$ GHz and consumes $C = 500$ host cycles per simulated instruction.

**Step 1 — Simulated target time:**

$$
T_{sim} = \frac{N \times CPI}{f_{clk}} = \frac{10{,}000 \times 2}{12 \times 10^{6}} = 1.667 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1.667 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Host execution time:**

$$
T_{host} = \frac{N \times C}{f_{host}} = \frac{10{,}000 \times 500}{3 \times 10^{9}} = 1.667 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1.667 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Speed ratio:**

$$
R = \frac{T_{sim}}{T_{host}} = \frac{1.667 \times 10^{-3}}{1.667 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.0
$$

**Step 4 — Slowdown factor:**

$$
S = \frac{1}{R} = 1.0
$$

> [!TIP]
> This counter-intuitive result (no slowdown!) occurs only because the host is *250× faster* than the target and the interpreter overhead is low. In practice, peripheral modeling adds 10×–1000× more overhead, restoring the expected slowdown.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Embedded Simulator

```mermaid
graph TD
    HostApp[Host Application Program]
    Loader[Program Loader - HEX/BIN]
    DecUI[Decoded Instruction Buffer]
    RegFile[Register File Model - R0..R7, PC, SP, PSW]
    MemMod[Memory Model - RAM, ROM, SFR Map]
    PeriphMod[Peripheral Model - UART, Timer, ADC, GPIO]
    IntCtrl[Virtual Interrupt Controller]
    ExecEng[Execution Engine - Fetch Decode Execute]
    StimGen[Stimulus Generator - Virtual Inputs]
    Viewer[Register / Memory / Waveform Viewer]
    Breakpoint[Breakpoint Manager]

    HostApp --> Loader
    Loader --> DecUI
    DecUI --> ExecEng
    ExecEng --> RegFile
    ExecEng --> MemMod
    ExecEng --> PeriphMod
    PeriphMod --> IntCtrl
    StimGen --> PeriphMod
    ExecEng --> Viewer
    Breakpoint --> ExecEng
    IntCtrl --> ExecEng
```

### Diagram 2: Fetch–Decode–Execute Loop (Closed Cycle)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start([Simulator Start])
    Fetch{Fetch Opcode at PC}
    Decode{Decode Instruction}
    Operand[Fetch Operands from Register File / Memory]
    Execute[Execute Operation in Software]
    Writeback[Write-back Result to Register / Memory]
    PCUpdate[Update PC - Increment or Branch Target]
    IntCheck{Pending Interrupt?}
    IntServe[Service Interrupt - Push PC, Vector Jump]
    LoopBack[Loop back to Fetch]
    Halt([Simulation End])

    Start --> Fetch
    Fetch --> Decode
    Decode --> Operand
    Operand --> Execute
    Execute --> Writeback
    Writeback --> PCUpdate
    PCUpdate --> IntCheck
    IntCheck -- Yes --> IntServe
    IntServe --> LoopBack
    IntCheck -- No --> LoopBack
    LoopBack --> Fetch
    PCUpdate -. End Opcode .-> Halt
```

### Diagram 3: Simulator's Place in the Embedded SDLC

```mermaid
graph LR
    Spec[Requirements Specification]
    Sim[Simulator - Pure Software - Early Validation]
    Compile[Cross-Compiler - Keil / GCC]
    Emul[In-Circuit Emulator - Bond-out Chip]
    HWDbg[JTAG / SWD Hardware Debugger]
    Target[Target Hardware Board]
    Field[Field Deployment]

    Spec --> Sim
    Spec --> Compile
    Compile --> Emul
    Emul --> HWDbg
    HWDbg --> Target
    Target --> Field
    Sim -.Optional Validation.-> Compile
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The simulator sits at the *leftmost* (earliest) stage of the embedded development pipeline. It allows code to be validated *before* the cross-compiler even produces the final HEX file, which is why it is a mandatory tool in KTU 2024 Scheme laboratory courses.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define an embedded system simulator. List any two advantages and two limitations.**

**Model Answer:**

A **simulator** is a software tool that emulates the functional behavior of a target microcontroller (CPU + memory + peripherals) on a host computer, allowing embedded firmware to be executed and debugged without the actual hardware.

**Advantages:**
1. Enables early-stage code development and debugging before hardware availability.
2. Provides complete visibility into internal CPU state (registers, flags, memory), which is not possible on physical hardware.
3. Zero risk of damaging expensive prototype hardware.

**Limitations:**
1. Cannot model real-world analog noise, signal integrity, or true real-time peripheral timing accurately.
2. Runs significantly slower (10³–10⁶×) than real hardware, making long-duration tests impractical.
3. Peripheral models are approximations and may diverge from actual silicon behavior.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Differentiate between a simulator and an emulator in the context of embedded systems.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Simulator | Emulator |
|---|---|---|
| Hardware | Pure software; runs on host PC | Uses special bond-out chip + hardware pod |
| Real-time | No; executes slower than real-time | Yes; runs at full target speed |
| Cost | Low (often free) | High (₹50,000 – ₹5,00,000+) |
| Peripheral accuracy | Modeled in software | Uses real silicon peripherals |
| Visibility | Total (every internal signal) | Total (via bond-out pins) |
| Typical KTU tool | Keil µVision Simulator | None (rare in curriculum) |

---

### Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE full question. Each carries 14 marks, split into 7 + 7.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **internal architecture of a typical embedded simulator** with a neat block diagram. Describe the function of each block.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable example, derive the relationship between **simulated target time** ($T_{sim}$) and the **number of instructions executed**.

---

##### Model Solution for Q.A (a):

The internal architecture of a typical embedded simulator consists of **five major functional blocks**:

1. **Program Loader:** Accepts the HEX/BIN file produced by the cross-compiler. Parses Intel HEX or Motorola S-record format and loads the binary image into the simulated program memory.

2. **Instruction Decoder:** Maintains the simulated **Program Counter (PC)**. Fetches the opcode at PC, decodes it into micro-operations, and dispatches to the execution engine.

3. **Register File Model:** A software array that mirrors the CPU's general-purpose registers (R0–R7 for 8051; R0–R12 for ARM), special registers (PC, SP, LR, PSR), and banked registers if any.

4. **Memory Model:** A linear addressable byte/word array simulating internal RAM, external RAM, code ROM, and the **Special Function Register (SFR)** map. Honors endianness and alignment rules.

5. **Peripheral / I/O Model:** Software stubs that mimic hardware peripherals (Timers, UART, ADC, GPIO, Interrupts). Accept virtual stimuli (e.g., a button press) and produce virtual responses (e.g., toggling a simulated port pin).

**Auxiliary blocks:**
- **Execution Engine:** Orchestrates the fetch–decode–execute–write-back cycle.
- **Breakpoint Manager:** Pauses simulation when PC matches a user-set address.
- **Waveform Viewer:** Plots virtual logic-analyzer traces of simulated pins over simulated time.

The **fetch–decode–execute loop** is then described cyclically:

$$
\text{PC} \rightarrow \text{Fetch} \rightarrow \text{Decode} \rightarrow \text{Execute} \rightarrow \text{Update PC, Regs, Flags} \rightarrow \text{Loop}
$$

##### Model Solution for Q.A (b):

Let a target microcontroller be clocked at $f_{clk}$ Hz. The firmware program contains $N$ machine instructions, each consuming an average of $CPI$ (Cycles Per Instruction) clock cycles.

The clock period is:

$$
T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}}
$$

The simulated time consumed by **one instruction** is:

$$
T_{inst} = CPI \times T_{clk} = \frac{CPI}{f_{clk}}
$$

For the entire program of $N$ instructions, the total simulated time is:

$$
T_{sim} = N \times T_{inst} = \frac{N \times CPI}{f_{clk}}
$$

**Numerical Example:** For $N = 50{,}000$, $CPI = 1.5$, $f_{clk} = 16$ MHz:

$$
T_{sim} = \frac{50{,}000 \times 1.5}{16 \times 10^{6}} = 4.6875 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \approx 4.69 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to state the **assumptions** ($CPI$ is average, $f_{clk}$ is constant, no pipeline stalls). Always declare assumptions explicitly. Marks are deducted for silent assumptions.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **simulators, emulators, and JTAG debuggers**. Tabulate the comparison on at least **six** parameters.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A firmware of $N = 2 \times 10^6$ instructions with $CPI = 1.2$ runs on an ARM Cortex-M0 target clocked at $f_{clk} = 50$ MHz. Calculate the **simulated target time** and the **slowdown factor** if the host simulator consumes $C = 800$ host cycles per simulated instruction on a $f_{host} = 2.5$ GHz workstation.

---

##### Model Solution for Q.B (a):

| Sl. No. | Parameter | Simulator | Emulator | JTAG/SWD Debugger |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hardware Required | None | Bond-out chip + pod | Target board + probe |
| 2 | Real-time Execution | No | Yes | Yes |
| 3 | Cost | Low (free) | Very high | Medium |
| 4 | Peripheral Realism | Approximate | Real silicon | Real silicon |
| 5 | Internal Visibility | Complete | Complete | Limited (breakpoint-based) |
| 6 | Typical Use | Early dev, unit tests | HW validation | Production debugging |
| 7 | Speed vs. Target | 10³–10⁶× slower | Equal | Equal |
| 8 | Code Modification | Easy (software) | Difficult | Difficult |

**Conclusion:** Simulators are best for **early functional validation**; emulators for **hardware bring-up**; JTAG debuggers for **final production-grade debugging**.

##### Model Solution for Q.B (b):

**Step 1 — Simulated target time:**

$$
T_{sim} = \frac{N \times CPI}{f_{clk}} = \frac{2 \times 10^6 \times 1.2}{50 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
T_{sim} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^6}{5 \times 10^7} = 4.8 \times 10^{-2} \text{ s} = 48 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Host execution time:**

$$
T_{host} = \frac{N \times C}{f_{host}} = \frac{2 \times 10^6 \times 800}{2.5 \times 10^9}
$$

$$
T_{host} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^9}{2.5 \times 10^9} = 0.64 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Speed ratio:**

$$
R = \frac{T_{sim}}{T_{host}} = \frac{0.048}{0.64} = 0.075
$$

**Step 4 — Slowdown factor:**

$$
S = \frac{1}{R} = \frac{1}{0.075} \approx 13.33
$$

**Interpretation:** The host PC must spend **~13.33 seconds of real time** to simulate **1 second of target time**. This is a realistic figure for a moderately instrumented cycle-accurate ARM ISS.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):**
> 1. **Unit mix-ups:** Forgetting to convert MHz → Hz and GHz → Hz in the same expression is the #1 reason for losing 2–3 marks. Always show units in every line.
> 2. **Confusing $R$ and $S$:** Students frequently invert the ratio. Memorize: $R = T_{sim}/T_{host}$ and $S = 1/R$.
> 3. **Skipping the interpretation line:** Examiners award 1 mark for explicitly stating "this means 13.33 host seconds per simulated second." Don't end at the number.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Simulator** = pure-software emulator of a target CPU + memory + peripherals; runs on a host PC.
- **Three pillars** of internal architecture: **Register File Model, Memory Model, Peripheral Model**, orchestrated by the **Execution Engine**.
- **Fetch–Decode–Execute** loop is the heart of every simulator — always draw it as a **closed cycle**.
- **Simulated time formula:** $T_{sim} = \dfrac{N \times CPI}{f_{clk}}$ (memorize this verbatim).
- **Slowdown factor** $S = 1/R$ is typically $10^3$–$10^6$ for cycle-accurate ISS.
- **Simulator ≠ Emulator ≠ Debugger** — examiners LOVE this comparison question.
- **KTU-mandated lab tool:** Keil µVision Simulator (8051 / ARM), MPLAB X SIM (PIC), Renesas e² Studio Simulator (RL78).
- **Advantages:** Zero hardware risk, full internal visibility, deterministic replay, low cost.
- **Disadvantages:** Slow execution, imperfect peripheral models, no real-time I/O fidelity.
- **Real-world use:** MIL/SIL testing in automotive, IoT firmware prototyping, ISO 26262 / DO-178C certified toolchains.
- **Always declare assumptions** ($CPI$ average, constant $f_{clk}$, no pipeline effects) in derivations to avoid losing marks.
- **Always carry units** through every line of numerical calculation — a top-3 cause of mark loss in KTU ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emulators and Debugging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT523 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Emulators and Debugging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Emulator (In-Circuit Emulator – ICE):** A hardware-assisted debugging tool that physically replaces the target microcontroller/processor in the embedded system, providing real-time visibility and control over CPU registers, memory, and I/O operations without modifying the target hardware.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Debugger:** A software/hardware tool that allows the developer to load programs into target memory, execute them in a controlled fashion (step-by-step or breakpoint-driven), inspect state, and identify logical, timing, or hardware defects in embedded firmware.

> [!NOTE]
> **Emulation vs. Simulation – KTU Standard Distinction:**
> - **Simulator:** A *pure software* model of the processor that runs on a host PC; the *real* hardware is **never** involved. It models instruction execution cycle-by-cycle on the host.
> - **Emulator:** A *hardware device* that physically mimics the target processor's behaviour and is plugged into the actual target board's processor socket.
> - **Debugger:** Operates *with* the target either in-circuit (real silicon) or in-emulation (replacement silicon), and provides a user interface for state inspection.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy – "The Driving School Simulator vs. The Real Car with a Driving Instructor":**
> - A **simulator** is like a driving school's video-game cockpit. It teaches you steering, braking, and traffic rules *without* a real car or real road. Safe, repeatable, but it cannot feel the actual bumps of a Kerala monsoon-potholed road.
> - An **emulator** is like swapping your car's engine with a transparent, instrumented engine that behaves *exactly* like the original, but shows you piston timings, fuel injection, and ignition events in real time. You are driving the *real* car, on the *real* road, with an X-ray view of the engine.
> - A **debugger** is the driving instructor sitting beside you saying: *"Stop here at this red light (breakpoint) – let's pop the bonnet (inspect register), then continue (run/step)."*
> 
> For a B.Tech student, remember: **simulators imitate silicon in software; emulators replace silicon in hardware; debuggers orchestrate the inspection.**

### 1.3 Key Performance & Standard Metrics

- **Real-Time Trace Bandwidth:** typically **100 MHz – 4 GHz** on modern emulators.
- **JTAG Clock (TCK):** Standard boundary-scan operation **≤ 25 MHz** (IEEE 1149.1), high-speed variants go up to **100 MHz**.
- **Breakpoint Hardware Units:** Most ARM Cortex-M cores offer **2–8 hardware breakpoints** + unlimited software breakpoints.
- **Emulation Memory:** Modern ICEs provide **unlimited** emulation memory (subject to pod licensing).
- **Intrusion (Probe Effect):** A good emulator exhibits **0 ns intrusion** into the target's real-time behaviour.

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Embedded Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Module-4 of PECIT523 specifically targets the *software-hardware co-design verification* phase of the embedded product development life-cycle. KTU examiners expect students to:
> 1. Distinguish between simulator, emulator, and debugger (commonly asked for **3 marks**).
> 2. Explain JTAG/boundary-scan architecture and the TAP state machine (commonly asked for **7–14 marks**).
> 3. Describe ICE architecture and compare with on-chip debug (OCD) interfaces.
> 4. Discuss debugging strategies for memory, peripheral, and real-time bugs.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Emulation & Debugging Tools

#### A. By Mode of Operation
1. **Software Simulator (ISS – Instruction Set Simulator)**
   - Runs the executable on a host PC.
   - Models CPU registers, pipeline, memory map, and peripheral registers.
   - **Examples:** QEMU, Keil µVision Simulator, IAR Simulator, Renesas e2 studio Simulator.
   - **Limitation:** *Cannot* model real-time I/O, real electrical noise, or actual peripheral timing.

2. **In-Circuit Emulator (ICE)**
   - A probe with a "bond-out" version of the target CPU plugs into the target's processor socket.
   - Provides **zero-instrusion** real-time trace, unlimited breakpoints, and logic-analyser-style triggering.
   - **Historical vendors:** Nohau, iSystem, Hitex, Lauterbach (TRACE32).
   - **Modern reality:** Largely replaced by On-Chip Debug (OCD) for cost reasons, but still used in automotive/Aerospace (DO-178C certification needs ICE-grade non-intrusive trace).

3. **On-Chip Debugger (OCD)**
   - Debug circuitry is **built into the silicon** of the target MCU.
   - CPU core exposes a debug port (JTAG, SWD, BDM, cJTAG, SWO/ITM, ETM).
   - The host debugger (GDB, IAR, Keil) drives the target through this port.
   - **Examples:** ARM CoreSight, NXP Kinetis MDM-AP, Microchip ICD/X/E, Atmel-ICE.

4. **ROM Emulator**
   - A hardware box that *replaces* the target's program ROM/Flash during development.
   - The host downloads code into the emulator's battery-backed SRAM; the CPU fetches from there at full speed.
   - Useful when the target's flash cannot be reprogrammed frequently.

5. **Logic Analyser / Oscilloscope Assisted Debugging**
   - Non-intrusive observation of external pins, bus transactions, and protocol decoding (I²C, SPI, CAN, UART).
   - **No interaction with the CPU**; observes side-effects.

6. **Background Debug Mode (BDM) & Nexus**
   - BDM is Motorola/Freescale/NXP's proprietary OCD protocol for ColdFire/PowerPC.
   - **Nexus (IEEE-5001)** is the automotive-grade standard for high-bandwidth real-time trace.

#### B. By Host Interface
| Interface | Standard | Speed | Pin Count |
|---|---|---|---|
| JTAG | IEEE 1149.1 | ~25 MHz | 4 (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO) + TRST |
| SWD (Serial Wire Debug) | ARM | ~50 MHz | 2 (SWDIO, SWCLK) |
| BDM | Freescale | ~10 MHz | 3–4 |
| cJTAG (Compact JTAG) | IEEE 1149.7 | ~25 MHz | 2 |
| SWO/ITM | ARM | Asynchronous | 1 (SWO pin) |
| ETM (Embedded Trace Macrocell) | ARM | 4-bit/8-bit/16-bit trace | 4–20 |

### 2.2 JTAG (Joint Test Action Group) – IEEE 1149.1 Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> JTAG was **originally** designed for **boundary-scan testing of PCBs**, not for processor debugging. The same TAP (Test Access Port) was cleverly reused for OCD by silicon vendors.

#### JTAG Pin Set (5 mandatory signals):
1. **TCK (Test Clock)** – Driven by the host; synchronizes all operations.
2. **TMS (Test Mode Select)** – Sampled on TCK rising edge; navigates the TAP state machine.
3. **TDI (Test Data In)** – Serial data into the target.
4. **TDO (Test Data Out)** – Serial data out of the target.
5. **TRST (Test Reset)** – *Optional*, active-low, asynchronously resets the TAP.

#### 16-State TAP State Machine (The Heart of JTAG)
- States: **Test-Logic-Reset → Run-Test/Idle → Select-DR-Scan → Select-IR-Scan → Capture-DR/IR → Shift-DR/IR → Exit1-DR/IR → Pause-DR/IR → Exit2-DR/IR → Update-DR/IR**.
- The TMS pin at each TCK rising edge determines the next state.
- A full transition cycle: `Run-Test/Idle → Select-DR-Scan → Select-IR-Scan → Capture-IR → Shift-IR → Update-IR` (this loads an instruction into the IR).
- A data scan: `Run-Test/Idle → Select-DR-Scan → Capture-DR → Shift-DR → Update-DR`.

#### Instruction Register (IR) – Mandatory JTAG Instructions
- **EXTEST (0000):** Forces boundary-scan cells to drive pins, used for interconnect testing.
- **SAMPLE/PRELOAD (0010):** Samples pin states into the boundary-scan chain without disturbing operation.
- **BYPASS (1111):** Places a 1-bit register between TDI and TDO, used to skip a device in a chain.
- **IDCODE (0001):** Reads the 32-bit Device ID.
- **INTEST (1100):** Applies test vectors to the core's internal logic.
- **User-defined (vendor-specific):** e.g., for ARM CoreSight – `HALT`, `RESTART`, `READ_CORE_REG`, `WRITE_CORE_REG`.

### 2.3 Boundary Scan – PCB Testing Principle

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary-Scan Chain Principle:** Every I/O pin of every JTAG-compliant IC has a 4-state boundary-scan cell (BC) between the core logic and the physical pin. These cells are daisy-chained TDI → TDO across all JTAG devices on the board.

The Boundary Scan Description Language (**BSDL**) file (per IEEE 1149.1) describes the length and pin-mapping of the chain for a given IC.

#### Operation Modes:
1. **SAMPLE:** Capture pin values into the chain while the system runs normally.
2. **EXTEST:** Drive pins from the chain to test interconnects (opens, shorts, bridges).
3. **INTEST:** Apply test patterns to the *internal* core logic of the IC.

### 2.4 ARM CoreSight Debug Architecture (Most Asked in KTU)

Modern ARM Cortex-M/M3/M4/M7 cores use **ARM CoreSight** which combines:
- **SWD** (2-pin debug)
- **SWO** (Serial Wire Output for printf-style ITM tracing)
- **ETM** (Embedded Trace Macrocell – 4/8/16-bit parallel trace)
- **DWT** (Data Watchpoint & Trace unit – 4 comparators)
- **FPB** (Flash Patch & Breakpoint – 2–8 hardware breakpoints)
- **TPIU** (Trace Port Interface Unit)

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory-Mapped Debug Access:** CoreSight exposes a **debug APB bus** mapped into the system address space. The debugger writes to AP registers to:
> - HALT the core (write to **DHCSR** – Debug Halting Control and Status Register).
> - Single-step (set **C_STEP** bit).
> - Set/clear breakpoints (FPB registers).
> - Read/write core registers via **DCRDR/DCRSR**.
> 
> For Cortex-M3, the debug registers occupy addresses **0xE000_xxxx** and **0xE004_xxxx**.

### 2.5 Debugging Techniques

#### A. Software Breakpoints
- Replace the original instruction at the breakpoint address with a **`BKPT #0`** (ARM) or **`BRK`** (RISC-V) or **`0xCC`** debug opcode (x86).
- When the CPU hits this opcode, it traps into the debug exception handler.
- **Cannot be used in ROM/Flash without patch hardware** (because writing is impossible) — solution: use **Hardware Breakpoints (FPB)**.

#### B. Hardware Breakpoints
- Implemented as comparators inside the CPU.
- Each comparator has a **compare address + compare value + mask**.
- When the address matches, the core halts or triggers a watchpoint.
- **Advantage:** Works for ROM, Flash, and *data access* (read/write watchpoints).

#### C. Watchpoints
- A specialised form of breakpoint on *data accesses* (read, write, or both).
- Critical for debugging "who overwrote this variable?" bugs.

#### D. Real-Time Trace
- Records the executed instruction stream or data accesses **without halting** the CPU.
- Sent off-chip via SWO (low bandwidth, ~2 Mbps) or ETM (high bandwidth, hundreds of MB/s).
- **Used in:** AUTOSAR MCAL validation, DO-178C avionics certification.

#### E. Profiling & Code Coverage
- **pc-sampling profiler:** periodically samples PC to identify hot functions.
- **Code coverage:** instruments the binary to record which branches were taken (MC/DC, branch, statement coverage).
- KTU relevance: mentioned in Module-4 in context of testing embedded firmware.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following are *core metrics and relationships* examiners expect for calculation/sizing problems in ESE.

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Trace buffer depth | $T_{depth} = f_{CPU} \times \Delta t_{trace}$ | instructions | Sizing trace RAM |
| SWO bandwidth (Manchester) | $BW_{SWO} = f_{SWO}/2$ | bps | Throughput of ITM printf |
| ETM trace port width | $BW_{ETM} = N_{pins} \times f_{ETM}$ | bps | Selecting probe (4/8/16-bit) |
| JTAG chain length | $L_{chain} = \sum L_i$ where $L_i$ = IR/DR length of device $i$ | bits | Total shift count |
| TAP state transition | Each TCK = 1 TMS sample | clock | Multi-clock instruction load |
| Breakpoint trigger latency | $t_{bp} = N_{pipeline} / f_{CPU}$ | seconds | Real-time debug accuracy |
| Boundary-scan fault coverage | $F_c = 1 - 2^{-L_{chain}}$ | ratio | Test escape probability |
| Watchpoint address mask | $A_{match} = A_{comp} \oplus A_{mask}$ | address | Range watchpoints |

> [!TIP]
> **Always use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `\vert` in tables** (as per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 rule) when writing absolute value expressions to keep Markdown tables valid.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECU validation:** ICE + ETM trace is mandatory for ISO 26262 ASIL-D firmware.
- **IoT firmware OTA debugging:** SWD over Bluetooth/Wi-Fi for field firmware triage.
- **Aerospace (DO-178C):** Requires non-intrusive trace — drives use of Nexus 5001 or ARM ETM.
- **Consumer electronics (smartphones):** JTAG/SWD used for factory provisioning, board bring-up, and JTAG boundary-scan tests.
- **Failure analysis:** Boundary scan locates solder opens/shorts on dense BGA PCBs where X-ray is the only alternative.
- **Reverse engineering:** OpenOCD + GDB + JTAG enables binary inspection on locked-down devices (also a security concern → JTAG lock bits).
