## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Simulator vs Emulator vs On-Chip Debugger

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **high-frequency KTU table**. Memorise it for both 3-mark and 14-mark questions.

| Parameter | Simulator (ISS) | ICE | On-Chip Debug (JTAG/SWD) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Target hardware involved** | No | Yes (CPU replaced) | Yes (real silicon) |
| **Real-time accuracy** | No (host-timed) | **Yes (zero intrusion)** | Mostly (small probe effect) |
| **Cost** | Free–Low | **Very High (₹ 2–20 Lakh)** | Low (₹ 500–10 000 probe) |
| **Breakpoints** | Unlimited | Unlimited | Limited by FPB (2–8) |
| **Trace** | Simulated | Full real-time | ETM (high-end) / SWO (basic) |
| **I/O accuracy** | Modelled | Real | Real |
| **Power analysis** | Not possible | Not possible | Possible with shunt + SWO timestamps |
| **Field upgrade** | N/A | N/A | OTA debugger possible |
| **Best use** | Algorithm bring-up, unit test | Certification, hard real-time | General development |
| **CPU availability** | None needed | Bond-out needed | Built-in debug logic |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step: Halt the Core via SWD (ARM Cortex-M)

This is the canonical KTU 14-mark derivation style — *show all transitions in the SWD/JTAG-DP state machine.*

> [!NOTE]
> **SWD Protocol Line Format:**
> - 3-phased: **Host → Target request (8 bits)**, then **Target → Host acknowledge (3 bits)**, then **Data phase (32 bits)**.
> - Idle state: SWDIO is *high-impedance*, pulled high externally. SWCLK toggles only during transfers.

#### Step 1 — Power Up & Connection

$$
\text{Host asserts } V_{target},\ \text{detects target via } \text{IDCODE}\ \text{read on DP}
$$

The host first writes to the **DP_ABORT** register to clear any stale errors.

#### Step 2 — Read DPIDR to Confirm Chain

$$
\text{DPIDR} = 0x0BA0\_0477 \quad (\text{typical for SW-DP v1})
$$

#### Step 3 — Write to CSW (Control/Status Word) of AHB-AP

$$
\text{CSW} = (0xA2000\text{002}) \quad \Rightarrow \text{select } 32\text{-bit AHB transactions, auto-increment enabled}
$$

Bit fields: **Prot = 0x2 (debug)**, **SIZE = 0x2 (32-bit)**, **MasterType = 1 (debug)**, **AutoIncr = 1**.

#### Step 4 — Write DHCSR (Debug Halting Control & Status Register) at 0xE000_EDF0

$$
\text{DHCSR} = 0xA05F\_0003
$$

Bit breakdown:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DHCSR}[31:16] &= \text{DBGKEY} = 0xA05F &\text{(unlock key, mandatory write mask)} \\
\text{DHCSR}[1]      &= C\_HALT = 1  &\text{(halt request)} \\
\text{DHCSR}[0]      &= C\_DEBUGEN = 1 &\text{(enable debug)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5 — Poll for HALT

The host reads DHCSR repeatedly and checks bit **S_HALT** (bit 17).

$$
S\_HALT = 1 \iff \text{core is fully halted}
$$

#### Step 6 — Read Core Registers via DCRDR/DCRSR

To read R0, the host writes `DCRSR = (0x00000 | R0_bcd)`, then reads `DCRDR`.

$$
DCRSR = (0x00000\ \vert\ 0)\quad\Rightarrow\quad DCRDR\ \text{returns}\ R0
$$

DCRSR encoding: bits `[6:4]` select the register, bit `[16]` is `RnW`, bit `[17]` is `RegWnR`.

#### Step 7 — Single-Step

$$
DHCSR = 0xA05F\_0005 \quad (\text{DEBUGEN}=1,\ HALT=0,\ STEP=1)
$$

After one instruction, S_HALT re-asserts automatically.

#### Step 8 — Resume

$$
DHCSR = 0xA05F\_0001 \quad (\text{DEBUGEN}=1,\ HALT=0,\ STEP=0)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The DBGKEY = 0xA05F is a "magic" 16-bit key to prevent accidental debug writes from stray user code.** This is a favourite KTU trivia question.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step: JTAG Instruction Load (e.g., Load IDCODE)

#### Step 1 — Enter Test-Logic-Reset
Drive TMS = 1 for 5 TCK cycles.
$$
TMS = 1 \times 5 \ \Rightarrow\ \text{TAP} = \text{Test-Logic-Reset}
$$

#### Step 2 — Move to Run-Test/Idle
$$
TMS = 0 \ \text{for 1 TCK} \ \Rightarrow\ \text{TAP} = \text{Run-Test/Idle}
$$

#### Step 3 — Select DR-Scan Path
$$
TMS = 1,\ 0 \ \Rightarrow\ \text{TAP} = \text{Select-DR-Scan}
$$
*Note:* TMS = 0 at this point would select DR-Scan; TMS = 1 selects IR-Scan.

#### Step 4 — Select IR-Scan
$$
TMS = 1,\ 1 \ \Rightarrow\ \text{TAP} = \text{Select-IR-Scan}
$$

#### Step 5 — Capture IR
$$
TMS = 0 \ \Rightarrow\ \text{TAP} = \text{Capture-IR}
$$

#### Step 6 — Shift IR (load the 4-bit instruction)
For a 4-bit IR, shift 4 bits with TMS = 0:
$$
\text{Shift count } n = 4,\ \text{Data} = 0001\ (\text{IDCODE instruction})
$$

#### Step 7 — Exit1-IR
$$
TMS = 1 \ \Rightarrow\ \text{TAP} = \text{Exit1-IR}
$$

#### Step 8 — Update-IR
$$
TMS = 1 \ \Rightarrow\ \text{TAP} = \text{Update-IR}
$$
The instruction is latched into the instruction register and becomes active.

#### Step 9 — Shift DR (Read 32-bit IDCODE)
$$
n_{DR} = 32,\ \text{read IDCODE bit-by-bit on TDO}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total TCK cycles to load and execute IDCODE = 5 (reset) + 2 (select) + 1 (capture) + 4 (shift) + 2 (exit/update) + 1 (capture) + 32 (shift) + 2 (exit/update) = 49 TCK cycles.**
> This is the kind of numerical answer KTU examiners reward.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: Boundary-Scan Fault Diagnosis

> [!NOTE]
> This is a typical 7-mark sub-question in KTU ESE.

**Scenario:** Two ICs U1 and U2 on a board are connected by a 4-bit data bus. A bridging fault is suspected between U1_D0 and U1_D1.

#### Step 1 — BSDL Loading
Host loads BSDL files for U1 and U2. Compiler generates the **Serial Vector Format (SVF)** test vectors.

#### Step 2 — SAMPLE State
Drive TAP to SAMPLE, capture current pin states into the chain.
$$
\text{Scan chain length } L = L_{U1} + L_{U2}
$$

#### Step 3 — EXTEST State
Load boundary-scan cells to drive U1 pins with test pattern:
$$
U1: \ D0=0,\ D1=1,\ D2=0,\ D3=1
$$

#### Step 4 — Capture at U2
Capture the U2 receive pins:
$$
\text{Expected: } U2: \ D0=0,\ D1=1,\ D2=0,\ D3=1
$$
If actual is `D0=0, D1=0, D2=0, D3=1`, then a **short to D2** or **stuck-at-0 on D1** is inferred.

#### Step 5 — Fault Localisation
Using the test response, the algorithm identifies the failing nets and reduces the suspect set. With 4 pins, a bridging fault can be isolated to within **2 pins** by repeating with inverse patterns:
$$
\text{Pattern 1: } 0101\ \vert\ \text{Inverse: } 1010
$$

The conflicting responses pin-point the bridged nets.

### 3.5 Exhaustive Walkthrough: OpenOCD + GDB Debug Flow

This is a KTU favourite for *14-mark part (a)*: "Explain the architecture and operation of a typical JTAG-based OCD system."

#### Step 1 — Hardware Connection
- Host PC ↔ **Debug Probe (e.g., J-Link, ST-Link, CMSIS-DAP)** ↔ **Target Board SWD/JTAG header** ↔ **Target MCU** (with built-in CoreSight).

#### Step 2 — OpenOCD Launch
OpenOCD reads a `board.cfg` and `interface.cfg` to initialise the probe (USB enumeration, SWD clock, target voltage check).

#### Step 3 — TAP Detection
OpenOCD scans TCK with TMS = 1 for 5 cycles, then issues `IDCODE` to enumerate devices. Returns the JTAG Device ID.
$$
\text{Device ID} = 0x4BA0\_0477 \quad (\text{typical ARM Cortex-M device ID})
$$

#### Step 4 — Target Halt
OpenOCD writes to DHCSR = `0xA05F_0003`, then polls S_HALT.

#### Step 5 — GDB Remote Connect
GDB connects to OpenOCD's gdbserver (TCP port 3333):
$$
\text{(gdb) target remote localhost:3333}
$$

#### Step 6 — Memory Map Probe
GDB issues `qXfer:memory-map:read` to obtain target memory map. Typical for STM32F4:
$$
\begin{aligned}
0x0000\_0000 - 0x0001\_FFFF &: \text{Flash (128 KB)} \\
0x2000\_0000 - 0x2001\_FFFF &: \text{SRAM (64 KB)} \\
0x4000\_0000 - 0x400F\_FFFF &: \text{Peripherals} \\
0xE000\_0000 - 0xE00F\_FFFF &: \text{System (NVIC, CoreSight)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 7 — Load Firmware
GDB issues `vFlashErase` and `vFlashWrite` packets to program the binary into Flash.

#### Step 8 — Set Breakpoint
$$
\text{(gdb) break main.c:42}
$$
GDB uses the `Z0` packet to set a software breakpoint (overwrites the instruction at line 42 with `BKPT #0`).

#### Step 9 — Run
$$
\text{(gdb) continue}
$$
GDB issues `vCont;c`. The MCU resumes, executes until the `BKPT`, and re-halts.

#### Step 10 — Inspect & Step
GDB issues `p $r0` (read register), `n` (next), `s` (step), `x/32xw 0x20000000` (dump memory).

#### Step 11 — Watchpoint
$$
\text{(gdb) watch *(volatile uint32\_t *)0x20000000}
$$
GDB uses `Z2` packet, target's DWT comparator is programmed. On any write to that address, the core halts.

#### Step 12 — Resume / Detach
GDB issues `D` (detach), OpenOCD resumes the core and closes the connection.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Breakpoint Comparison

| Feature | Software Breakpoint | Hardware Breakpoint | Watchpoint |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Instruction overwrite | Address comparator | Data comparator |
| Limit | Unlimited (in RAM) | 2–8 (FPB size) | 2–4 (DWT) |
| Works in Flash | No (needs write) | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Detects reads | No | No | **Yes** |
| Detects writes | Implicit (fetch) | Implicit | **Yes** |
| Latency | 1 cycle (BKPT) | 0–1 cycle | 0–1 cycle |
| Use case | RAM debugging | Flash/RTC debugging | Memory corruption hunting |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of an On-Chip Debug System (ARM CoreSight)

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid block diagram showing the host, debug probe, target's TAP, and the core's debug components.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HostPC[HOST PC<br/>GDB / Keil / IAR]
    Probe[DEBUG PROBE<br/>J-Link / ST-Link / CMSIS-DAP]
    SWDPin[SWD / JTAG<br/>2 or 5 pins]
    SWDDP[SW-DP<br/>Debug Port]
    AHBAP[AHB-AP<br/>Access Port]
    APBBus[DEBUG APB BUS<br/>0xE000xxxx]
    DWT[DWT<br/>Data Watchpoint and Trace]
    FPB[FPB<br/>Flash Patch and Breakpoint]
    ITM[ITM<br/>Instrumentation Trace Macrocell]
    ETM[ETM<br/>Embedded Trace Macrocell]
    SWOPin[SWO PIN<br/>1 pin ITM output]
    Core[CORTEX-M CORE<br/>CPU registers, PC, SP, R0-R12]
    Flash[TARGET FLASH<br/>0x08000000]
    RAM[TARGET SRAM<br/>0x20000000]

    HostPC -->|USB / Ethernet| Probe
    Probe -->|SWDIO, SWCLK| SWDPin
    SWDPin --> SWDDP
    SWDDP --> AHBAP
    AHBAP -->|AHB writes| Core
    AHBAP -->|memory R/W| Flash
    AHBAP -->|memory R/W| RAM
    AHBAP --> APBBus
    APBBus --> DWT
    APBBus --> FPB
    APBBus --> ITM
    APBBus --> ETM
    ITM --> SWOPin
    DWT --> SWOPin
    Core --> DWT
    Core --> FPB
    Core --> ITM
    Core --> ETM
```

### 4.2 JTAG TAP State Machine (16-State)

> [!NOTE]
> Each transition is triggered by TMS sampled on rising edge of TCK. Top row of arrows = TMS = 0; bottom row = TMS = 1.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> TLR: TRST or TMS=1x5
    TLR --> RTI: TMS=0
    RTI --> SelDR: TMS=1
    RTI --> SelIR: TMS=1 (with extra pulse)
    SelDR --> CapDR: TMS=0
    SelDR --> SelIR: TMS=1
    SelIR --> CapIR: TMS=0
    SelIR --> SelDR: TMS=0
    CapDR --> ShiftDR: TMS=0
    CapIR --> ShiftIR: TMS=0
    ShiftDR --> ShiftDR: TMS=0
    ShiftIR --> ShiftIR: TMS=0
    ShiftDR --> Exit1DR: TMS=1
    ShiftIR --> Exit1IR: TMS=1
    Exit1DR --> PauseDR: TMS=0
    Exit1DR --> Exit2DR: TMS=1
    Exit1IR --> PauseIR: TMS=0
    Exit1IR --> Exit2IR: TMS=1
    Exit2DR --> ShiftDR: TMS=0
    Exit2DR --> UpdDR: TMS=1
    Exit2IR --> ShiftIR: TMS=0
    Exit2IR --> UpdIR: TMS=1
    PauseDR --> PauseDR: TMS=0
    PauseIR --> PauseIR: TMS=0
    PauseDR --> Exit2DR: TMS=1
    PauseIR --> Exit2IR: TMS=1
    UpdDR --> RTI: TMS=0
    UpdDR --> SelDR: TMS=1
    UpdIR --> RTI: TMS=0
    UpdIR --> SelIR: TMS=1
```

### 4.3 Boundary-Scan Chain Across Multiple ICs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TDI[TDI from Host]
    TDO[TDO to Host]
    U1[U1 IC<br/>BSDL: 100 bit chain]
    U2[U2 IC<br/>BSDL: 64 bit chain]
    U3[U3 IC<br/>BSDL: 256 bit chain]
    TDI --> U1
    U1 --> U2
    U2 --> U3
    U3 --> TDO
    TCK[TCK from Host] --> U1
    TCK --> U2
    TCK --> U3
    TMS[TMS from Host] --> U1
    TMS --> U2
    TMS --> U3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Total chain length** = $L_{U1} + L_{U2} + L_{U3} = 100 + 64 + 256 = 420$ bits.
> A single bit shift of 420 bits per test vector. This is a *bottleneck* for large boards — hence the use of **bypass registers** to skip functional devices during interconnect test of unrelated nets.

### 4.4 Software Debug Flow (GDB + OpenOCD)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([Engineer starts session])
    Connect[OpenOCD connects to target via probe]
    Detect[TAP detect: read IDCODE]
    Halt[Write DHCSR to HALT core]
    Load[Flash firmware via vFlashWrite]
    SetBP[Set breakpoint via Z0 packet]
    Run[Issue continue packet]
    HitBreak{Breakpoint hit?}
    Inspect[Read registers, memory, locals]
    Step[Step next / Step into]
    WatchSet{Set watchpoint?}
    SetWP[Set DWT comparator via Z2]
    Resume[Resume core]
    Detach[Detach GDB session]
    End([Session ends])

    Start --> Connect --> Detect --> Halt --> Load --> SetBP --> Run --> HitBreak
    HitBreak -- Yes --> Inspect
    HitBreak -- No --> WatchSet
    WatchSet -- Yes --> SetWP --> Resume --> HitBreak
    WatchSet -- No --> Resume --> HitBreak
    Inspect --> Step --> Inspect
    Step --> Detach --> End
```

### 4.5 Typical Bring-Up Debug Workflow (Industrial)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Phase 1: Power-on<br/>Verify rails, clock, reset]
    P2[Phase 2: JTAG IDCODE read<br/>Confirm silicon presence]
    P3[Phase 3: Boundary scan<br/>Test PCB interconnects]
    P4[Phase 4: OCD connection<br/>Halt and read PC, SP]
    P5[Phase 5: Memory test<br/>March-C, walk-1/0 on RAM]
    P6[Phase 6: Peripheral loopback<br/>GPIO toggle, UART echo]
    P7[Phase 7: Bootloader flash<br/>Stage 1 via JTAG]
    P8[Phase 8: OS / RTOS debug<br/>Threads, semaphores, heap]
    P9[Phase 9: Application debug<br/>Algorithm, drivers, stacks]
    P10[Phase 10: Certification trace<br/>ETM/Nexus for safety case]

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7 --> P8 --> P9 --> P10
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Differentiate between a software simulator and an in-circuit emulator (ICE) for embedded systems. List two advantages of ICE over a simulator.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

| Aspect | Simulator (ISS) | In-Circuit Emulator (ICE) |
|---|---|---|
| **Hardware involvement** | Pure software on host | Replaces target CPU with bond-out |
| **Real-time accuracy** | Not real-time (host-paced) | **True real-time, zero intrusion** |
| **I/O behaviour** | Modelled | Actual peripherals |
| **Cost** | Free / nominal | ₹ 2 Lakh – ₹ 20 Lakh |

**Two advantages of ICE:**
1. Provides **true real-time** execution and full-speed trace, enabling debugging of timing-sensitive bugs.
2. Captures **electrical behaviour of real peripherals**, not modelled approximations, hence higher fidelity for hardware-software co-debug.

*(3 marks: 1 for table, 1 each for the two ICE advantages.)*

---

#### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **What is JTAG? List the four mandatory signals of the JTAG TAP and state the purpose of the TRST pin.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

> **JTAG** stands for **Joint Test Action Group** (IEEE 1149.1), a 4-wire serial protocol originally for **boundary-scan testing** of PCBs and reused by silicon vendors for **on-chip debugging**.

**Four mandatory TAP signals:**
1. **TCK** – Test Clock (host-driven).
2. **TMS** – Test Mode Select (drives the TAP state machine).
3. **TDI** – Test Data In (serial data into target).
4. **TDO** – Test Data Out (serial data out of target).

**TRST (Test Reset, optional):** Active-low asynchronous reset that forces the TAP into the **Test-Logic-Reset** state without requiring five TMS=1 TCK cycles. Useful for stable, deterministic reset at power-up.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for 4 signals, 1 for TRST purpose.)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write *paragraph-form* answers for 14-mark questions. KTU examiners want **labelled diagrams, tables, and numbered step lists**. A 14-mark question without a diagram typically loses 2–3 marks.

#### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]**

> **(a)** Explain the architecture of a typical **JTAG-based On-Chip Debug (OCD)** system used in modern ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers. With a neat block diagram, show the path from the host debugger to the CPU registers. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Describe the **JTAG TAP (Test Access Port) state machine**. Explain how the **IDCODE** instruction is loaded and executed, computing the total number of TCK cycles required. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

A JTAG-based OCD system consists of three logical blocks:

**1. Host Side** (GDB / Keil / IAR): issues high-level commands (`break main`, `continue`).

**2. Debug Probe** (J-Link / ST-Link / CMSIS-DAP): converts USB/ethernet packets from the host to **SWD or JTAG signals** on the target. The probe also supplies target voltage sensing.

**3. Target Side** (ARM CoreSight):
- **SW-DP (Serial Wire Debug Port)** or **JTAG-DP**: receives the 2/4-wire protocol and decodes packets.
- **AHB-AP (Access Port)**: a memory-mapped AHB master that the DP controls. Writes from the host to AHB-AP registers are translated to **AHB bus transactions** to the target's memory and peripheral space.
- **Debug APB bus** at 0xE000_xxxx: houses **DWT** (watchpoints), **FPB** (breakpoints), **ITM** (printf-style trace), **ETM** (full instruction trace).
- **CPU Core**: halted via DHCSR (Debug Halting Control and Status Register, address 0xE000_EDF0). The DBGKEY = 0xA05F unlocks the register for safety.

**Operation flow (numbered for 7 marks):**
1. Host issues `monitor reset halt`.
2. OpenOCD → probe → SW-DP writes `CSW` to AHB-AP to set transaction size, auto-increment.
3. SW-DP writes `DHCSR = 0xA05F_0003` to halt the core.
4. Host reads S_HALT bit to confirm halt.
5. Host reads `DCRDR` after writing `DCRSR` to fetch core register values.
6. Host writes new PC to `DCRSR` to set execution point.
7. Host writes `DHCSR = 0xA05F_0001` to resume; or `0xA05F_0005` to single-step.

(Include the CoreSight block diagram from Section 4.1.)

**Mark split:** 2 marks for naming all 3 blocks, 2 marks for the CoreSight internals, 2 marks for DHCSR / DBGKEY / DCRDR explanation, 1 mark for the operation flow.

---

**Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**TAP State Machine** (16 states, group into 4 functional groups):
- **Reset group:** Test-Logic-Reset (TLR).
- **Run group:** Run-Test/Idle (RTI).
- **DR scan group:** Select-DR-Scan, Capture-DR, Shift-DR, Exit1-DR, Pause-DR, Exit2-DR, Update-DR.
- **IR scan group:** Select-IR-Scan, Capture-IR, Shift-IR, Exit1-IR, Pause-IR, Exit2-IR, Update-IR.

**Loading the IDCODE instruction (4-bit IR, 32-bit DR):**

Step 1: Drive TMS = 1 for **5 TCK cycles** → state = TLR.
Step 2: TMS = 0 for 1 TCK → state = RTI.
Step 3: TMS = 1, 0 → Select-DR-Scan then **Select-IR-Scan** (2 TCK).
Step 4: TMS = 0 → Capture-IR (1 TCK).
Step 5: TMS = 0 × 4 → Shift-IR, shifting instruction `0001` (4 TCK).
Step 6: TMS = 1 → Exit1-IR (1 TCK).
Step 7: TMS = 1 → Update-IR (1 TCK). Total so far: 5 + 1 + 2 + 1 + 4 + 1 + 1 = **15 TCK**.

**Reading IDCODE (32-bit DR):**
Step 8: TMS = 0 → Run-Test/Idle (1 TCK).
Step 9: TMS = 1, 0 → Select-DR-Scan, Capture-DR (2 TCK).
Step 10: TMS = 0 × 32 → Shift-DR, reading 32 bits on TDO (32 TCK).
Step 11: TMS = 1 → Exit1-DR (1 TCK).
Step 12: TMS = 1 → Update-DR (1 TCK). Data phase total: 1 + 2 + 32 + 1 + 1 = 37 TCK.

**Total TCK cycles for IDCODE load + read:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{TCK} &= N_{IR\_load} + N_{DR\_read} \\
        &= 15 + 37 = 52\ \text{cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note: some implementations exit the IR-load and enter RTI differently; the examiner will accept any correct transition path yielding 49–55 TCK.

**Mark split:** 2 marks for the state-machine diagram, 2 marks for the IR-load sequence, 2 marks for the DR-read sequence, 1 mark for the numerical TCK count.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

> **(a)** Explain the **boundary-scan testing** technique for PCB interconnects using JTAG. With a block diagram, describe how shorts and opens on a multi-IC board are detected. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Compare **hardware breakpoints, software breakpoints, and watchpoints** for embedded debugging. When would you prefer a hardware breakpoint over a software breakpoint? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Boundary-Scan Principle:**
Every JTAG-compliant IC has a 4-state Boundary-Scan Cell (BSC) — *Normal, Capture, Update, Bypass* — inserted between each I/O pin and the core logic. All BSCs are daisy-chained TDI → TDO across the IC. Multiple ICs on a board are daisy-chained together to form a single **board-level scan chain**.

**Detection of Opens and Shorts:**

**Step 1 — Pre-test (BSDL loading):**
The Boundary Scan Description Language (BSDL) file for each IC is loaded by the test tool. It specifies the chain length and the boundary register bit mapping for every pin.

**Step 2 — SAMPLE (non-invasive):**
TAP is driven to SAMPLE state. The tool captures the actual pin states of all ICs *during normal system operation* and compares them with the expected functional pattern. Any mismatch flags a *stuck-at* fault.

**Step 3 — EXTEST (interconnect test):**
TAP is driven to EXTEST. The tool writes test vectors into the boundary cells of the **driving** IC (e.g., U1) and reads the response from the **receiving** IC (e.g., U2).

Example: a 4-bit bus with patterns `0101`, `1010`, `0000`, `1111`.

- **Open fault detection:** If U2 receives `0010` when U1 drives `0110`, the third bit is open (high-impedance pulled to default = 0). Repeating with inverse pattern `1001` confirms the open bit.
- **Short fault detection:** If U2 receives `0000` when U1 drives `0101` and the suspect net is bridged to GND, the test pattern mismatches predictably. Walking-1 algorithm isolates the bridged nets in O(log N) tests.

**Step 4 — BYPASS for efficiency:**
Unrelated ICs in the chain are set to BYPASS mode (instruction `1111`), reducing them to a 1-bit shift register. Only the ICs of interest occupy their full boundary length in the chain.

**Mark split:** 2 marks for BSDL/principle, 2 marks for SAMPLE, 2 marks for EXTEST, 1 mark for open/short detection with example.

---

**Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

| Feature | Software Breakpoint | Hardware Breakpoint | Watchpoint |
|---|---|---|---|
| Implementation | Instruction replaced with `BKPT` | FPB comparator match | DWT comparator match |
| Modifies memory? | **Yes** | No | No |
| Works in Flash/ROM? | **No** (Flash is write-protected mid-run) | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Works in RAM? | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Count | Unlimited | 2–8 (FPB size) | 2–4 (DWT size) |
| Detects data access? | No (only instruction fetch) | No | **Yes** (read/write) |
| Latency | 1 cycle (BKPT) | 0–1 cycle | 0–1 cycle |
| Use | RAM development | Flash / ISR debugging | Memory corruption bugs |

**When to prefer Hardware Breakpoints over Software:**
1. **Debugging code in Flash/ROM** — software breakpoints require patching memory with a `BKPT` instruction; this is impossible if the Flash cannot be written at runtime.
2. **Debugging interrupt service routines (ISRs)** — patching RAM with a breakpoint can corrupt timing-sensitive ISRs; hardware breakpoints halt non-invasively.
3. **Debugging read-only code or OTP** — same reason as Flash.
4. **Code that self-checksums** — patching memory would change the checksum and trigger anti-tamper; hardware breakpoints are invisible to the code.
5. **Debugging optimised code** — compilers may merge or eliminate `BKPT`s; hardware breakpoints operate on physical addresses, unaffected by optimisation.

**Mark split:** 3 marks for the comparison table, 2 marks for the count/Flash/RAM distinctions, 2 marks for the 5 use-case scenarios.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls where KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "emulator" with "simulator"** — KTU examiners deduct 1 full mark if the difference is not explicit.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the DBGKEY = 0xA05F** in the DHCSR write — this is a *favourite* 1-mark trivia.
> 3. **No diagram in 14-mark answers** — minimum 1 labelled diagram is expected per 14-mark question.
> 4. **Writing JTAG pins as "5 wires" without naming them** — naming TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO, TRST is mandatory.
> 5. **Skipping numerical computation in TAP cycle-count questions** — partial credit only if the transition sequence is *tabulated*.
> 6. **Not distinguishing hardware vs software breakpoints** — easy 2-mark loss in 14-mark questions.
> 7. **Mixing up EXTEST (pin drive) with INTEST (core drive)** — boundary-scan question — easy 1-mark loss.
> 8. **Forgetting to mention the optional TRST** when listing "4 mandatory + 1 optional" JTAG signals.

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density revision bullets for the night before the exam:**

- **Simulator** = software only, host-paced, no real hardware; **Emulator** = hardware probe that *replaces* the CPU; **Debugger** = software tool that drives the emulator/OCD and presents a UI.
- **JTAG** = IEEE 1149.1, 4 mandatory + 1 optional signal, 16-state TAP.
- **Mandatory JTAG pins:** TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO; **Optional:** TRST.
- **TAP state machine has 16 states**; navigation is by TMS sampled on rising TCK.
- **Boundary scan** uses SAMPLE (non-invasive observe), EXTEST (drive pins for interconnect test), INTEST (test internal core), BYPASS (1-bit skip).
- **BSDL** is the per-IC file describing the boundary register length and pin mapping.
- **ARM CoreSight** = SWD + SWO + ETM + DWT + FPB + TPIU.
- **SWD** uses only 2 pins (SWDIO, SWCLK); **JTAG** uses 4.
- **DHCSR** = 0xE000_EDF0; **DBGKEY** = 0xA05F; **C_HALT** = bit 1; **C_DEBUGEN** = bit 0; **C_STEP** = bit 2; **S_HALT** = bit 17.
- **Software breakpoints** patch RAM with `BKPT`; **hardware breakpoints** are FPB comparators; **watchpoints** are DWT comparators on data.
- **Hardware breakpoints are required** for Flash, ROM, ISR, and checksummed code.
- **ETM** = parallel high-bandwidth trace; **SWO/ITM** = 1-pin low-bandwidth printf-style trace; **SWV** = Serial Wire Viewer (SWO+ITM+DWT).
- **JTAG chain length** = sum of IR/DR lengths of all devices in the chain; use BYPASS to skip devices.
- **Typical ARM Cortex-M IDCODE** = 0x4BA0_0477 (for debug port v1).
- **JTAG TCK cycles for IDCODE** ≈ 50 (range 49–55) — write the transition table to earn the numerical mark.
- **JTAG clock** ≤ 25 MHz per IEEE 1149.1; high-speed variants go to 100 MHz; SWD up to 50 MHz.
- **Probe effect** = timing intrusion introduced by a debugger; ICEs aim for 0 ns; OCDs have small SWD-pause cycles.
- **OpenOCD** is the open-source OCD driver; pairs with **GDB** for source-level debug.
- **JTAG lock bits** = security fuse on many MCUs to disable boundary-scan from external tools.
- **Boundary-scan fault coverage** $F_c = 1 - 2^{-L_{chain}}$ — larger chains give exponentially higher coverage.
- **DO-178C / ISO 26262** demand non-intrusive trace → drive the use of ETM/Nexus in safety-critical ECUs.
- **Nexus 5001** = IEEE standard for high-bandwidth real-time trace, used in automotive PowerPC and ARM Cortex-R.
- **BDM** = Motorola/Freescale/NXP proprietary 3- or 4-pin debug interface, predecessor to JTAG/SWD on ColdFire/PowerPC.
- **ROM emulator** = a hardware replacement for the target's program memory during development; allows fast iteration without Flash wear.
- **A "magic" 16-bit key (0xA05F)** in DHCSR prevents random writes from user code to the debug register.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module-4 Notes: Emulators and Debugging.**
> For KTU ESE preparation, focus on: (1) simulator vs emulator vs debugger, (2) JTAG TAP + IDCODE cycle count, (3) ARM CoreSight + DHCSR, (4) boundary-scan EXTEST, (5) hardware vs software breakpoints. These five themes together account for ~80% of marks in this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Target Hardware Debugging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT523 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Target Hardware Debugging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🎯 Module 4 — Target Hardware Debugging

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Target Hardware Debugging** is the process of identifying, isolating, and correcting defects (bugs) in embedded firmware while it executes on the **actual physical target hardware** (the real microcontroller/microprocessor board), as opposed to running on a software simulator or host PC. It employs specialized hardware interfaces and tools — such as **JTAG (IEEE 1149.1)**, **Serial Wire Debug (SWD)**, **Background Debug Mode (BDM)**, **In-Circuit Emulators (ICE)**, **logic analyzers**, and **ROM monitors** — to gain controlled, observable access to the CPU core, memory, and peripherals during runtime.

In KTU 2024 scheme parlance, target hardware debugging is the **lowest layer** of the embedded development verification stack, sitting *below* host-side unit testing and *above* pure simulation. It is the only verification method that exposes real-world timing, EMI, and peripheral-interaction bugs.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Car Mechanic and the Engine:**

Imagine you are a mechanic handed a brand-new car engine that occasionally sputters. You have three choices:

1. **Simulator (Software Simulation):** Build a computer model of the engine and run virtual fuel. Fast and safe, but the *real* air, real spark plugs, and real heat are missing.
2. **Target Hardware Debugging:** Bolt the engine onto a test rig, attach sensors (pressure, RPM, exhaust gas analyzers), and *watch the engine run* while injecting controlled fault conditions.
3. **Production (No Debug):** Ship the car and hope.

**Target hardware debugging = the test rig with sensors.** It lets the engineer *pause* the CPU mid-instruction, *peek* inside registers and memory, *inject* values into RAM, and *trace* every bus cycle in real time — something simulators physically cannot do.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Board-Exam Keyword:** "Debugging on the *real* silicon exposes bugs caused by **race conditions, signal integrity, stack corruption, and peripheral handshaking** that a simulator hides."

---

## 1.3 Why Target Hardware Debugging is Unavoidable in KTU Syllabus

| Layer | What it Catches | What it Misses |
|---|---|---|
| **Host PC Unit Test** | Algorithm / logic errors | Hardware timing, real I/O |
| **Simulator / ISS** | Instruction-level behavior | Electrical noise, DMA, cache effects |
| **Target Hardware Debug** | *Everything* above + **real bugs** | — (final authority) |
| **Field / In-Field** | Customer-found escapes | Often too late / too expensive |

**Conclusion:** Only target hardware debugging validates the *complete* embedded system.

---

## 1.4 Core Physical / Protocol Constants You Must Memorize

- **JTAG standard:** **IEEE 1149.1** (also called "Boundary Scan" or "DOT-1")
- **JTAG minimum signals:** **5** — TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO, TRST* (optional)
- **SWD signals (ARM):** **2** — SWDIO, SWCLK
- **JTAG TAP state machine states:** **16**
- **JTAG IR width:** typically **5 bits** (allows 32 instructions)
- **Boundary Scan Chain length:** equals **number of package pins under test**

---

## 1.5 The Three Pillars of Target Hardware Debugging

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Control** — Halt, step, resume the CPU deterministically.
> 2. **Observation** — Read registers, memory, peripherals, and bus events.
> 3. **Injection** — Write to memory, set breakpoints, force I/O pin states.

Every debug tool (JTAG probe, ICE, BDM, ROM monitor) exists to deliver these three capabilities over a physical or logical channel between the host debugger (IDE) and the target CPU.

---

## 1.6 Section 1 Recap

- Target hardware debugging = verifying firmware on **real silicon**.
- Primary interfaces: **JTAG**, **SWD**, **BDM**, **ICE pods**, **ROM monitors**.
- Three debug capabilities: **Control**, **Observation**, **Injection**.
- Always exposed by real hardware, never by simulators.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — Architecture & Methods

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Target Hardware Debugging Tools

Target hardware debug tools fall into **five principal families**. KTU examiners love asking for their **comparative differences**, so this is high-yield.

### 2.1.1 In-Circuit Emulator (ICE)

- **What it is:** A *physical replacement* for the target CPU. The ICE pod has a probe tip that plugs into the CPU socket on the target board. The pod contains a *bonded-out* (special) version of the CPU with extra observation wires brought out.
- **History:** Dominant in 1980s–1990s for 8051, 68HC11, early ARM.
- **Strengths:** Full-speed execution, unlimited breakpoints, real-time trace.
- **Weaknesses:** **Expensive**, **CPU-specific**, cannot keep up with modern QFP/BGA packages.
- **Modern status:** Largely **obsolete** for high-pin-count MCUs; replaced by **On-Chip Debug (OCD)**.

### 2.1.2 On-Chip Debugger / OCD (JTAG, SWD, BDM)

- **What it is:** Debug logic **built into the silicon** of the target MCU. An external **debug probe** (e.g., J-Link, ST-Link, P&E Cyclone, Lauterbach) talks to this on-chip logic via a serial protocol.
- **Strengths:** Cheap (probe ~$20–$500), works with BGA, near-real-time.
- **Weaknesses:** Limited breakpoints (2–8 hardware, unlimited software), trace buffer may be small.
- **Examples:**
  - **ARM Cortex-M:** SWD (2-wire) or JTAG
  - **NXP/Freescale ColdFire / HCS08:** **BDM** (Background Debug Mode)
  - **Infineon TriCore:** DAP (Device Access Port)

### 2.1.3 ROM Monitor / Debug Monitor

- **What it is:** A small **resident debug firmware** burned into a protected region of Flash that the application calls (often via a trap / illegal-instruction exception). The monitor provides read/write/breakpoint services.
- **Strengths:** **Zero extra hardware** — uses only the on-chip UART.
- **Weaknesses:** **Consumes Flash + RAM**; **steals an interrupt vector**; **not transparent** (the CPU is running monitor code, not your code, at breakpoints).
- **Use case:** Bootloader-style development, low-cost MCUs with no JTAG pins.

### 2.1.4 Logic Analyzer + Oscilloscope

- **What it is:** External test equipment that **probes physical pins** or bus lines.
- **Strengths:** Sees the *real electrical* world — voltage levels, glitches, ringing, setup/hold violations.
- **Weaknesses:** **No CPU control**; **pin-limited** for modern fine-pitch packages; needs good test points.
- **Use case:** Hardware bring-up, signal-integrity debugging, peripheral protocol decode (I²C, SPI, UART).

### 2.1.5 Emulation Pods (e.g., QEMU, Renode)

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT confuse "emulator" with "ICE" in KTU answers.** A software emulator (QEMU) is *not* target hardware debugging — it is host-side simulation. State this clearly when answering comparative questions.

---

## 2.2 JTAG (IEEE 1149.1) — The Workhorse Interface

### 2.2.1 Architecture

JTAG was originally designed for **boundary-scan testing** of PCBs. It embeds a **shift-register cell (Boundary Scan Cell, BSC)** between each I/O pin and the core logic. These cells form a chain — the **Boundary Scan Register (BSR)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every JTAG device contains a **TAP Controller** (16-state FSM) that orchestrates the shifting of data and instructions.

### 2.2.2 The Four (Five) Mandatory JTAG Signals

| Signal | Full Name | Direction | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TCK** | Test Clock | In to target | Drives all JTAG state transitions |
| **TMS** | Test Mode Select | In to target | Sampled on TCK ↑ to step TAP FSM |
| **TDI** | Test Data In | In to target | Serial data input to current register |
| **TDO** | Test Data Out | Out from target | Serial data output from current register |
| **TRST*** | Test Reset (optional) | In to target | Asynchronous reset of TAP FSM (active LOW) |

### 2.2.3 TAP Controller — 16-State Finite State Machine

> [!NOTE]
> The TAP FSM has two stable "idle" states: **Test-Logic-Reset** and **Run-Test/Idle**, plus two scan branches: **DR-Scan** (data register path) and **IR-Scan** (instruction register path). Each branch has 7 transient states: Select-DR/IR, Capture-DR/IR, Shift-DR/IR, Exit1-DR/IR, Pause-DR/IR, Exit2-DR/IR, Update-DR/IR.

**State Machine Operation — The "Why" Behind Each Step:**

- **Test-Logic-Reset (TLR):** Normal operation. Whatever the CPU does, JTAG stays out of the way. Asserting TMS=1 for ≥5 TCK cycles forces this state — this is how a host debugger establishes a known starting point.
- **Run-Test/Idle (RTI):** CPU is running normally, JTAG is idle. The host can hold here indefinitely.
- **Select-DR / Select-IR:** The host chooses whether the next transaction operates on a **Data Register** (DR) or the **Instruction Register** (IR).
- **Capture-DR/IR:** On the falling edge of TCK in this state, the device **loads parallel data into the shift register** from its parallel inputs (or from the boundary cells for BSR, or the device ID for IDCODE).
- **Shift-DR/IR:** Data is shifted serially — TDO clocks out the bit shifted from TDI, on TCK's falling edge (TMS=0 to stay here).
- **Exit1-DR/IR:** Transitional state to leave the shift branch.
- **Pause-DR/IR:** Optional hold point if the host is too slow to clock all bits in one burst.
- **Exit2-DR/IR:** Decides whether to re-enter Shift (TMS=0) or go to Update (TMS=1).
- **Update-DR/IR:** On the falling edge of TCK in this state, the **parallel output latch** of the selected register is updated from the shift register. This is the "commit" step.

### 2.2.4 Mandatory JTAG Instructions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per IEEE 1149.1, every compliant device **must** support these two instructions:
> - **BYPASS (8'b11111111):** A 1-bit register that just delays TDI→TDO by one cycle. Used to skip a device in a scan chain.
> - **EXTEST (8'b00000000):** Drives the BSC outputs onto the package pins and captures pin state into BSCs. Used for **interconnect testing** (shorts, opens).

Optional but common:
- **SAMPLE/PRELOAD (8'b00000010 / 8'b00000001):** Snapshot pin state without disturbing it.
- **IDCODE (8'b00000110):** Reads out the 32-bit JTAG Device ID Register (JEDEC manufacturer + part number).
- **USERCODE (8'b00001111):** For programmable devices, returns the user-programmed ID.

### 2.2.5 Multi-Device JTAG Chain

> [!NOTE]
> Multiple JTAG devices on one PCB are wired in a single **daisy chain**: TDI → Device 1 → Device 2 → ... → Device N → TDO. The total IR length is the sum of all IR widths. The host must shift (sum of IR widths) bits to load instructions into *all* devices simultaneously. BYPASS is used to make non-target devices transparent.

---

## 2.3 SWD (Serial Wire Debug) — ARM's 2-Wire Alternative

ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers (the dominant KTU lab platform) often expose **SWD** as a low-pin-count alternative to JTAG.

| Property | SWD | JTAG |
|---|---|---|
| **Pin count** | **2** (SWDIO, SWCLK) + optional SWO | **4–5** |
| **Protocol** | Packet-based (ARM-specific) | Bit-stream + TAP FSM |
| **Data width per transfer** | Up to 32 bits per packet | 1 bit per TCK |
| **Throughput at 50 MHz** | Higher | Lower |
| **Backward compat with JTAG** | No (separate pins usually) | — |

**SWD Packet Format (High-Yield):**

A SWD transfer is a **3-phase packet sequence**:
1. **Request phase (8 bits):** Host → Target packet header
2. **Acknowledge phase (3 bits):** Target → Host response (OK, WAIT, FAULT)
3. **Data phase (32 bits):** Bidirectional, parity-protected

This is why SWD is faster for register reads/writes — you get **32 bits per packet** instead of JTAG's **1 bit per TCK**.

---

## 2.4 Background Debug Mode (BDM) — Freescale / NXP Legacy

> [!NOTE]
> BDM is a **single-wire debug interface** (plus BKGD pin) used in Freescale HCS08, ColdFire, HC(S)12 families. It uses a proprietary synchronous serial protocol where the host sends commands (READ, WRITE, READ_BYTE, WRITE_BYTE, GO, BACKGROUND) and the target responds. It can force the CPU into background mode (halted) by asserting the BKGD pin LOW during reset.

---

## 2.5 Hardware vs Software Breakpoints (Frequently Asked!)

| Property | Hardware Breakpoint | Software Breakpoint |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | CPU has internal **address comparators** (2–8 typically) | Original instruction is **patched** with a `BKPT` / `BRK` opcode |
| **Code modification** | **None** | **Yes** — Flash must be writable |
| **Can halt in Flash** | ✅ Yes | ⚠️ Requires Flash with self-programming |
| **Can halt in ROM** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (can't write ROM) |
| **Can halt in RAM** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Number available** | Limited (2–8) | Unlimited (one per address) |
| **Speed of hit** | 1 cycle | 1 cycle (same) |

> [!WARNING]
> A common exam trap: "Software breakpoints cannot be set in Flash on a microcontroller without self-programming capability." Know this.

---

## 2.6 Watchpoints (Data Breakpoints)

A **watchpoint** halts execution when a specific **memory address is read, written, or both**. Implemented via the same address comparator hardware as breakpoints but with the **data bus** monitored instead of the **instruction fetch bus**.

**Use case:** Finding which routine corrupts a global variable.

---

## 2.7 ROM Emulators

A **ROM emulator** is a hardware device that **plugs into the target's ROM socket** and presents itself as the ROM, but is actually a **RAM** controlled by the host PC. The host downloads new code to the emulator's RAM in seconds — bypassing the slow Flash-erase-and-program cycle of the target.

**Pros:** Fast iteration; no Flash wear.
**Cons:** Mechanical socket needed (rare on modern SMT MCUs), limited capacity, no real-time trace.

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula / Cheat-Sheet Table

> [!TIP]
> Memorize this table — it directly answers the most common 3-mark and 7-mark KTU questions on this topic.

| Quantity / Concept | Formula / Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| JTAG mandatory signals | **5** (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO, TRST\*) | TRST* optional |
| JTAG TAP states | **16** | IEEE 1149.1 |
| SWD signals | **2** (SWDIO, SWCLK) + optional SWO | ARM |
| BDM signals | **2** (BKGD, RESET*) | Freescale |
| SWD data bits per packet | **32** | Plus header + ACK + parity |
| JTAG data bits per TCK | **1** | Serial shift |
| JTAG total chain IR length | $L_{IR} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{IR}_i$ | Sum across all N devices |
| JTAG total chain DR length (BYPASS mode) | $L_{DR} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} 1 \text{ bit}$ (non-target) + target DR | For multi-device boundary scan |
| Mandatory JTAG instructions | **BYPASS, EXTEST** | Per IEEE 1149.1 |
| EXTEST opcode | `8'b00000000` | Standard |
| BYPASS opcode | `8'b11111111` | Standard |
| IDCODE register width | **32 bits** | JEDEC ID |
| Tap-reset TCK cycles | **≥ 5** | TMS held HIGH |
| Typical debug probe cost | **\$20 – \$500** | Segger J-Link, ST-Link |

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECU development:** JTAG/BDM used to flash and debug ECUs on the production line.
- **Medical device firmware:** Trace + watchpoints are FDA-audit evidence of verification.
- **IoT / Consumer:** SWD on Cortex-M is the de-facto standard for ARM-based IoT products.
- **Aerospace:** Boundary-scan (JTAG EXTEST) is mandatory for board-level interconnect test per IPC-9252.

---

## 2.10 Section 2 Recap

- Five tool families: **ICE, OCD (JTAG/SWD/BDM), ROM Monitor, Logic Analyzer, Emulator (software, NOT target debug).**
- JTAG = IEEE 1149.1, 5 signals, 16-state TAP FSM.
- SWD = ARM's 2-wire, 32-bit-packet alternative.
- BDM = Freescale 1-wire debug.
- **Hardware breakpoints** don't modify code; **software breakpoints** do.

# 🧮 3. Step-by-Step Analysis, Derivation & Worked Examples

> [!NOTE]
> This topic is more architectural than numerical, so the "derivations" below are protocol-state analyses. Every transition is shown explicitly — no skipped steps.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bringing the TAP Controller to a Known State

**Problem:** A host debugger (e.g., OpenOCD + J-Link) is connected to a target MCU whose TAP state is unknown (could be mid-shift from a previous session, or in test mode). Compute the **minimum number of TCK cycles** needed to deterministically reach the **Test-Logic-Reset** state.

### Step 1 — Identify the worst-case starting state
The TAP FSM has **16 states**. The two states that, if stuck in, take the longest to escape are the **Pause-DR/IR** and **Shift-DR/IR** states. Worst case, the FSM is in **Shift-DR** and we must walk back up the tree.

### Step 2 — Write the state-transition path
Holding **TMS = 1** (logic HIGH) for **N consecutive TCK cycles** walks the FSM toward Test-Logic-Reset regardless of the starting state. This is because the transition table for TMS=1 always leads "upward" toward TLR.

$$
\text{Path}_{\text{generic}} : \text{Shift-DR} \xrightarrow{\text{TMS}=1} \text{Exit1-DR} \xrightarrow{\text{TMS}=1} \text{Update-DR} \xrightarrow{\text{TMS}=1} \text{Select-DR} \xrightarrow{\text{TMS}=1} \text{Select-IR} \xrightarrow{\text{TMS}=1} \text{Test-Logic-Reset}
$$

That is **5 transitions** (5 TCK cycles) with TMS held HIGH.

### Step 3 — Verify against all 16 states
Apply TMS=1 to every one of the 16 states. In every case, within **5 TCK cycles** the FSM reaches Test-Logic-Reset. This is a **proven IEEE 1149.1 property**.

$$
\boxed{N_{\min} = 5 \text{ TCK cycles with TMS} = 1}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why every JTAG spec and probe driver explicitly commands a 5-cycle "TLR reset" on connect.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Multi-Device JTAG Chain IR Length

**Problem:** A PCB has three JTAG devices daisy-chained:
- **Device A** (FPGA): IR width = **6 bits**
- **Device B** (ARM MCU): IR width = **4 bits**
- **Device C** (CPLD): IR width = **8 bits**

Compute the **total instruction bits** that must be shifted into the chain to load distinct instructions into each device simultaneously.

### Step 1 — Conceptual explanation
In a JTAG daisy chain, all devices share the same TCK and TMS. TDI of the probe enters Device A's TDI pin; TDO of Device A connects to TDI of Device B; and so on. Therefore, **one long shift** loads instructions into **all** devices in parallel — each device capturing its own slice.

### Step 2 — Form the summation
Total chain IR length is the arithmetic sum of the per-device IR widths:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{IR,\text{total}} &= L_{IR,A} + L_{IR,B} + L_{IR,C} \\
&= 6 + 4 + 8 \\
&= 18 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Bit-ordering convention
On the **first TCK of the shift**, the MSB of the *last* device's instruction enters the chain at TDI. The convention is **closest to TDO gets shifted in first**. The host debugger software (e.g., OpenOCD) handles this byte ordering automatically.

### Step 4 — Tap-state sequence to load
To shift a new IR into the chain:
1. From **Test-Logic-Reset**, assert TMS=0 for 1 TCK → **Run-Test/Idle**.
2. TMS=1 for 1 TCK → **Select-DR**.
3. TMS=1 for 1 TCK → **Select-IR**.
4. TMS=0 for 1 TCK → **Capture-IR**.
5. TMS=0 for **18 TCK** → **Shift-IR** (load 18 bits).
6. TMS=1 for 1 TCK → **Exit1-IR**.
7. TMS=1 for 1 TCK → **Update-IR** (commit).
8. TMS=0 for 1 TCK → **Run-Test/Idle**.

$$
\boxed{\text{Total TCK cycles for one IR load} = 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 18 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 25}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — SWD Turnaround / Acknowledge Timing

**Problem:** For a SWD read transaction at clock frequency $f_{\text{SWCLK}}$ and packet data width $N = 32$ bits, compute the minimum number of SWCLK cycles for one full 32-bit register read.

### Step 1 — Identify the three SWD phases
Each SWD transaction has three phases: **Request (8 bits)**, **Acknowledge (3 bits)**, and **Data Transfer (32 bits)**.

$$
T_{\text{req}} = \frac{8}{f_{\text{SWCLK}}}, \quad T_{\text{ack}} = \frac{3}{f_{\text{SWCLK}}}, \quad T_{\text{data}} = \frac{32}{f_{\text{SWCLK}}}
$$

### Step 2 — Add 1 turnaround bit (line-direction switch)
SWDIO is bidirectional. The bus must **turn around** between request and acknowledge. The ARM SWD spec inserts **1 idle bit** of turnaround.

$$
T_{\text{turn}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{SWCLK}}}
$$

### Step 3 — Sum
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{tx,read}} &= T_{\text{req}} + T_{\text{turn}} + T_{\text{ack}} + T_{\text{turn}} + T_{\text{data}} \\
&= \frac{8 + 1 + 3 + 1 + 32}{f_{\text{SWCLK}}} \\
&= \frac{45}{f_{\text{SWCLK}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{read}} = \frac{45}{f_{\text{SWCLK}}}}
$$

**For example**, at $f_{\text{SWCLK}} = 50\,\text{MHz}$:

$$
T_{\text{read}} = \frac{45}{50 \times 10^{6}} = 900\,\text{ns}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Hardware Breakpoint Resource Limit

**Problem:** An ARM Cortex-M3 has **4 hardware breakpoint comparators** (FPB units, Flash Patch and Breakpoint). How many distinct code addresses can be breakpointed simultaneously?

### Step-by-step solution
Each FPB comparator can match **one** instruction fetch address. With 4 comparators:

$$
N_{\text{HW-BP,max}} = 4
$$

To halt on more than 4 simultaneous locations, the debugger must use **software breakpoints** (patch the Flash with a `BKPT #0` instruction). However, software breakpoints:

- **Cannot** be placed in read-only Flash (without Flash-Patch hardware) on some MCUs.
- **Consume** 2 bytes of Flash per breakpoint (the `BKPT` opcode).
- **Modify** the application image — unsuitable for release builds.

$$
\boxed{N_{\text{simultaneous breakpoints}} = N_{\text{HW}} + N_{\text{SW,available}}}
$$

---

## 3.5 Comparison Matrix — Debug Tool Selection

> [!TIP]
> This matrix is gold for 14-mark KTU questions. Memorize the bolded entries.

| Tool | Visibility | Real-Time | Cost | Modern Use | BGA-Compatible |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ICE (In-Circuit Emulator)** | Total | ✅ Yes | **High (>\$5k)** | Legacy | ❌ No |
| **JTAG Probe (OCD)** | High | Mostly | **Low (\$20–\$500)** | **Standard** | ✅ Yes |
| **SWD Probe (OCD)** | High | Mostly | **Low (\$20–\$100)** | **Cortex-M standard** | ✅ Yes |
| **BDM (OCD)** | High | Mostly | **Low (\$50–\$300)** | Freescale | ✅ Yes |
| **ROM Monitor** | Medium | ❌ No (intrusive) | **Free** | Bootloader dev | ✅ Yes |
| **ROM Emulator** | Medium | ⚠️ Partial | **Medium** | Legacy / hobby | ❌ Socket only |
| **Logic Analyzer** | External pins only | ✅ Yes | **Medium–High** | HW bring-up | ✅ (probes) |
| **Software Simulator** | Total ISA | ❌ No | **Free** | Algorithm test | N/A |

---

## 3.6 Step-by-Step Debug Session Workflow (Typical)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU loves flow-based questions. This is a model answer.

**Step 1 — Hardware Setup**
Connect the debug probe (e.g., ST-Link V2) to the target's SWD header. Connect USB to the host. Verify VCC, GND, SWDIO, SWCLK.

**Step 2 — Probe Enumeration**
Host sends TLR reset (5× TMS=1 on TCK). Sends IDCODE IR. Shifts out the 32-bit IDCODE register of every device in the chain. If IDs match expected JEDEC IDs, chain is healthy.

**Step 3 — Halt on Reset**
Host issues `HALT` debug command. CPU enters Debug state at the reset vector (`0x0000_0004` on ARM Cortex-M).

**Step 4 — Load Image**
Host writes the firmware `.elf` to Flash via SWD. The MEM-AP (Memory Access Port) is used to write each 32-bit word.

**Step 5 — Set Breakpoints**
Host programs hardware breakpoint comparators at addresses of interest, or patches Flash with `BKPT #0x00` for software breakpoints.

**Step 6 — Run / Step / Observe**
Host sets `GO` command. CPU executes. On breakpoint hit, CPU halts. Host reads R0–R15, memory, and peripheral registers.

**Step 7 — Iterate**
Modify code, rebuild, re-flash, repeat.

---

## 3.7 Section 3 Recap

- TAP reset = **5 TCK cycles** with TMS=1.
- Chain IR length = **sum of per-device IR widths**.
- SWD read transaction = **45 SWCLK cycles** at 32-bit data width.
- Hardware breakpoints are **limited** by on-chip comparators; software breakpoints scale by Flash size.
