# 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions are modeled on **KTU End-Semester Examination (ESE)** patterns for the 2024 Scheme. Mark distribution follows the official Part-A (3 marks) and Part-B (14 marks) split. Two **independent Part-B alternatives** are provided per KTU module-level internal-choice rule.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define target hardware debugging. Why is it essential in embedded system development even when a software simulator is available?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Target hardware debugging is the process of identifying and correcting defects in embedded firmware while it executes on the actual physical target hardware, using interfaces such as JTAG, SWD, BDM, or ICE.

**Why essential (2 marks):**
1. **Real timing** — simulators miss interrupt latency, DMA races, and cache effects.
2. **Real I/O** — actual peripheral handshaking, signal integrity, voltage levels are only observed on real silicon.
3. **Electrical reality** — EMI, ground bounce, and noise-induced glitches appear only on the target board.
4. **Final verification** — field deployment demands validation on production hardware; simulation alone is not auditable proof of correctness.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**List the four mandatory signals of JTAG and state the role of the TAP controller.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **TCK (Test Clock):** Provides the synchronous clock that drives all JTAG state transitions. **(0.5)**
- **TMS (Test Mode Select):** Sampled on the rising edge of TCK; its sequence moves the TAP FSM through its 16 states. **(0.5)**
- **TDI (Test Data In):** Serial data input to the currently selected JTAG register. **(0.5)**
- **TDO (Test Data Out):** Serial data output from the currently selected JTAG register. **(0.5)**

**Role of TAP controller (1 mark):** The TAP (Test Access Port) controller is a 16-state finite state machine that orchestrates which register (IR, BSR, BYPASS, IDCODE) is connected between TDI and TDO, and when the parallel output of that register is updated or captured. It is the "traffic cop" of the JTAG interface.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Question Choice A (14 Marks)

> [!TIP]
> **Internal Choice:** In KTU ESE, a 14-mark module question typically has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The student answers **either** the entire Q-A **or** the entire Q-B.

### Question A (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
**Explain the IEEE 1149.1 JTAG architecture in detail. With a neat block diagram, describe the TAP controller state machine and the role of each of its major states. List the two mandatory JTAG instructions.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**JTAG Architecture (2 marks):**
- JTAG = Joint Test Action Group, standardized as **IEEE 1149.1**.
- Adds a 4-wire (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO) test access port to every compliant IC.
- Inside each device: a **TAP controller** (16-state FSM), an **Instruction Register (IR)**, and several **Data Registers (DRs)** — minimum: **Bypass Register (1 bit)**, **Boundary Scan Register (BSR)** of length equal to the number of package pins, and **Device ID Register (32 bits)**.
- The BSR inserts a scan cell (BSC) between each I/O pin and the core, forming a serial chain around the chip boundary.

**TAP Controller (3 marks):**
- 16 states, two stable idle states (**Test-Logic-Reset**, **Run-Test/Idle**), two scan branches:
  - **DR branch:** Select-DR → Capture-DR → Shift-DR → Exit1-DR → Pause-DR → Exit2-DR → Update-DR.
  - **IR branch:** Select-IR → Capture-IR → Shift-IR → Exit1-IR → Pause-IR → Exit2-IR → Update-IR.
- **Test-Logic-Reset:** Normal operation; CPU/JTAG idle.
- **Capture-DR/IR:** Parallel data loaded into the shift register.
- **Shift-DR/IR:** Serial shifting between TDI and TDO.
- **Update-DR/IR:** Shifted data committed to the parallel output latch.
- Transitions are driven by **TMS sampled on rising TCK**.

**Mandatory Instructions (1 mark):**
- **BYPASS (8'b11111111):** 1-bit register that delays TDI→TDO by one cycle; used to skip a device in a chain.
- **EXTEST (8'b00000000):** Drives BSR contents onto package pins and captures pin state into BSRs for **interconnect testing** (shorts, opens, stuck-at faults).

**Diagram (1 mark):** As shown in Section 4.2 (TAP FSM) and Section 4.7 (Boundary Scan Architecture).

---

### Question A (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare hardware breakpoints and software breakpoints in embedded debugging. Under what conditions must a hardware breakpoint be used?**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Hardware Breakpoint | Software Breakpoint |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Internal address comparator in CPU debug logic | Original instruction replaced with `BKPT` opcode |
| **Code modification** | None | Required |
| **Halt in Flash (read-only)** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **Halt in ROM** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **Halt in RAM** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Number available** | Limited (2–8 comparators) | Unlimited (subject to Flash) |
| **Setup speed** | Fast (write a register) | Slow (erase + reprogram Flash page) |
| **Flash wear** | None | One erase cycle per new breakpoint location |

**When MUST a hardware breakpoint be used (4 marks):**

1. **Code in ROM** — software breakpoints cannot be inserted because the ROM is read-only at runtime.
2. **Code in Flash without runtime self-programming** — without a Flash-Patch unit (like ARM's FPB), patching requires erasing a Flash page, which is destructive and slow.
3. **Code in OTP (One-Time Programmable) memory** — write-once memory cannot accept `BKPT` patches.
4. **When the application image must remain pristine** — for certification builds (DO-178C, IEC 61508) where inserting `BKPT` opcodes corrupts the certified binary.
5. **Debugging bootloaders or interrupt vector tables** — these early-stage code regions are often read-only and must be debugged with hardware comparators.

---

## 5.3 Part B — Question Choice B (14 Marks) — Alternative

### Question B (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]**
**Describe the Serial Wire Debug (SWD) protocol used in ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers. Compare it with JTAG in terms of pin count, data throughput, and packet format.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**SWD Overview (2 marks):**
- SWD = Serial Wire Debug, ARM's 2-wire alternative to JTAG for Cortex-M debug access.
- Two signals: **SWDIO** (bidirectional data) and **SWCLK** (host-driven clock). Optional **SWO** (Serial Wire Output) for trace.

**Packet Format (3 marks):**
- Three-phase packet per transfer:
  1. **Request (8 bits)** from host to target: header containing AP/DP select, R/W, address.
  2. **Acknowledge (3 bits)** from target: OK, WAIT, or FAULT.
  3. **Data (32 bits)** bidirectional, parity-protected.
- Plus **1 turnaround bit** at each line direction switch.

**Comparison with JTAG (2 marks):**

| Feature | SWD | JTAG |
|---|---|---|
| **Pin count** | 2 (+ optional SWO) | 4 (+ optional TRST) |
| **Bits per TCK/SWCLK** | 32 per packet | 1 per TCK |
| **Cycles for 32-bit read** | **45** (Section 3.3) | **32 + overhead** ≈ 35–40 |
| **Pin-mux on MCUs** | Often shares JTAG pins | Dedicated |
| **Boundary scan** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Adoption** | Cortex-M (ARM) only | Universal |

---

### Question B (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the role of a ROM monitor in embedded debugging. What are its advantages and limitations? When is it preferred over JTAG?**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**ROM Monitor — Concept (2 marks):**
A ROM monitor is a small, privileged piece of **resident firmware** stored in a protected region of Flash. When the application triggers a debug exception (e.g., a trap on a `BKPT` instruction, or a `printf` to a debug UART), control transfers to the monitor. The monitor then communicates with the host debugger over a **serial link (UART)**, exposing services such as read-memory, write-memory, set-breakpoint, and continue.

**Advantages (2 marks):**
1. **Zero external hardware** — uses only the on-chip UART (or USB-CDC). No JTAG probe required.
2. **Very low cost** — ideal for low-volume products and educational kits.
3. **Portable across MCUs** — works on any MCU with a UART, no special debug pins needed.
4. **Easy to deploy in field** — useful for post-production diagnostics and firmware updates.

**Limitations (2 marks):**
1. **Intrusive** — at each breakpoint, the CPU executes monitor code, not application code, so **real-time behavior is broken**. Hard real-time systems cannot use it.
2. **Consumes Flash and RAM** — the monitor occupies several KB of Flash and a small RAM buffer.
3. **Steals an interrupt vector** — the BKPT or trap exception must be reserved for the monitor.
4. **No boundary scan** — cannot test interconnects.
5. **Slower than JTAG** — UART baud rate limits throughput (typically 115200 to 921600 bps).

**When preferred over JTAG (1 mark):**
- When the MCU has **no JTAG/SWD pins** exposed on the production board (cost-optimized designs),
- For **field firmware updates** and **remote diagnostics**,
- For **educational platforms** (Arduino, basic 8051 kits) where adding a JTAG connector is impractical.

---

## 5.4 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes KTU examiners explicitly penalize:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "emulator" with "emulator software"** — QEMU/Renode is *host-side simulation*, NOT target hardware debugging. Writing "we use an emulator for target hardware debugging" loses 1–2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Wrong JTAG signal count** — JTAG has **5 signals** (with TRST*), not 4. Omitting TRST* is half-correct, but listing "5 including GND" is wrong.
> 3. **TAP state machine answer is "sketchy"** — Examiners expect at least the 6 main states (TLR, RTI, Capture, Shift, Update, plus Select) to be named. Saying "JTAG has states like shift and capture" is worth partial credit only.
> 4. **Mixing up BDM with JTAG** — BDM is Freescale-specific, 1-wire; JTAG is IEEE 1149.1, 4-wire. Confusing them = 0 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting mandatory instructions** — BYPASS and EXTEST **must** be mentioned. Omitting both = lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Not explaining why software breakpoints fail in ROM** — this is the single most common hardware-vs-software breakpoint question. If you don't say "because you cannot write to ROM at runtime," you will lose 2 marks.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> Read this section **the night before the exam** — it is a 60-second top-up.

### 🧠 Concept Recall
- [ ] **Target hardware debugging** = debugging firmware on the **real target board** (not simulator).
- [ ] **Three debug capabilities** = **Control, Observation, Injection.**
- [ ] **Five tool families** = ICE, OCD (JTAG/SWD/BDM), ROM Monitor, Logic Analyzer, ROM Emulator.

### 🔌 Interface Pin Counts
- [ ] **JTAG** = 4 mandatory + 1 optional TRST* = **5 signals** (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO, TRST*).
- [ ] **SWD** = 2 signals (SWDIO, SWCLK).
- [ ] **BDM** = 2 signals (BKGD, RESET*).

### ⚙️ JTAG Specifics
- [ ] **IEEE 1149.1** standard.
- [ ] **TAP Controller = 16-state FSM**.
- [ ] **Mandatory instructions: BYPASS, EXTEST.**
- [ ] **EXTEST opcode = 8'b00000000; BYPASS opcode = 8'b11111111.**
- [ ] **IDCODE width = 32 bits.**
- [ ] **TAP reset = 5 TCK cycles with TMS=1.**
- [ ] **Multi-device chain IR length = Σ of per-device IR widths.**

### 🐞 Breakpoint Rules
- [ ] **Hardware breakpoints** = address comparator; **no code modification**; works in ROM/Flash/RAM.
- [ ] **Software breakpoints** = `BKPT` opcode patched in; **needs writable Flash**; unlimited count.
- [ ] **Watchpoint** = breakpoint on **data** (R/W), not code fetch.

### 📊 Numerical High-Yield Results
- [ ] SWD 32-bit read transaction = **45 SWCLK cycles** (8 req + 1 turn + 3 ack + 1 turn + 32 data).
- [ ] JTAG minimum chain reset = **5 TCK cycles**.
- [ ] JTAG total IR bits for 3-device chain (6+4+8) = **18 bits**.

### 🏭 Real-World Tags (for context-rich answers)
- [ ] **Automotive** ECU: JTAG/BDM on production line.
- [ ] **ARM Cortex-M IoT**: SWD is the de-facto standard.
- [ ] **Boundary scan (EXTEST)**: mandatory for **IPC-9252** board-level testing.
- [ ] **DO-178C / IEC 61508**: hardware breakpoints preserve certified binary integrity.

### 🚫 Examiner Traps to Avoid
- [ ] Don't call QEMU an "emulator for target hardware debugging."
- [ ] Don't say JTAG has 4 signals without qualifying TRST*.
- [ ] Don't confuse BDM with JTAG.
- [ ] Don't forget the BYPASS and EXTEST mandatory instructions.
- [ ] Don't claim software breakpoints work in ROM.

---

## 5.6 Final KTU Score-Maximization Tip

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In 14-mark answers, always:**
> 1. Start with a **one-sentence formal definition** (1 mark).
> 2. Provide a **labeled block diagram or table** (1–2 marks).
> 3. List **at least 3–4 sub-points** covering the "Why" and "How" (3–4 marks).
> 4. End with a **practical real-world example** (1 mark).
> 5. **Compare** with at least one alternative (1–2 marks).
>
> This structure aligns with KTU's Outcome-Based Education (OBE) assessment rubrics and reliably scores 12+/14.

---

**End of Module 4 — Target Hardware Debugging Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boundary Scan
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT523 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Boundary Scan

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition

**Boundary Scan** is a standardized testability technique defined by the **IEEE 1149.1** standard (commonly known as **JTAG — Joint Test Action Group**) that facilitates the testing of interconnects on printed circuit boards (PCBs) and integrated circuits (ICs) without requiring physical test probes. It works by adding a dedicated shift register (the **Boundary Scan Register — BSR**) around the boundary of every chip, allowing test vectors to be shifted in and out via a 4-wire (or 5-wire) **Test Access Port (TAP)**.

In embedded systems, Boundary Scan is used to verify:
- Inter-chip connections on a PCB
- Internal chip logic (using the INTEST instruction)
- Interconnect faults such as opens, shorts, and stuck-at faults

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Boundary Scan is tested under the broader concept of *Design for Testability (DFT)* in embedded hardware. Expect a 7-mark or 14-mark question linking JTAG with TAP controller, BSR, and Bypass mode.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a city with **dozens of buildings** connected by **bridges (wires)**. Normally, to check whether each bridge is intact, you would need a helicopter to physically inspect it — expensive, slow, and sometimes impossible.

Now imagine each building has a **robot sentry at every doorway (boundary scan cell)**. These robots can:
1. **Capture** a snapshot of who is walking in/out (capture mode).
2. **Pass messages** along a chain of sentries (shift mode).
3. **Take over the door** to act as a controllable gate (update/external test mode).

A **control center (TAP controller)** issues simple commands ("shift", "capture", "update") to all sentries simultaneously. By sending a known pattern through the sentries and reading it back at the end, we can instantly tell whether any bridge is broken — **no helicopter needed**. That is Boundary Scan in essence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standards & Constants to Memorize:**
> - **IEEE 1149.1** — Standard Test Access Port and Boundary-Scan Architecture
> - **IEEE 1149.4** — Mixed-Signal Test Access Port
> - **IEEE 1149.7** — Reduced Pin Count TAP
> - **TCK** frequency: typically **1 MHz – 100 MHz** depending on DUT
> - **IR size**: minimum **2 bits** (for Bypass); common implementations use **4–8 bits**

## Physical Signal Pins of the TAP (5 Mandatory/Optional Pins)

| Pin | Full Name | Direction | Purpose |
|-----|-----------|-----------|---------|
| **TCK** | Test Clock | Input | Drives the state machine and register shifts |
| **TMS** | Test Mode Select | Input | Controls state transitions (sampled on TCK ↑) |
| **TDI** | Test Data In | Input | Serial data input to IR/DR |
| **TDO** | Test Data Out | Output | Serial data output from IR/DR |
| **TRST** | Test Reset | Input (optional) | Asynchronous reset of TAP controller |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of an IEEE 1149.1 Compliant Device

Every JTAG-compliant IC contains the following four mandatory hardware blocks:

1. **TAP Controller** — A 16-state finite state machine (FSM) that orchestrates operations.
2. **Instruction Register (IR)** — Holds the current test instruction.
3. **Data Registers (DRs)** — At minimum two:
   - **Boundary Scan Register (BSR)** — ring of cells around every functional pin.
   - **Bypass Register (BR)** — a 1-bit register that shortens the scan chain.
   - **Device Identification Register (IDCODE)** — optional, encodes manufacturer/part.
4. **TAP Port Pins** — TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO (and optional TRST).

## 2.2 The Boundary Scan Cell (BSC) — Heart of the Mechanism

Each boundary scan cell has **two memory elements** (capture + update) and operates in one of several modes:

- **Normal Mode** — transparent; functional data passes through (MUX selects system I/O).
- **Scan Mode** — data shifted along the BSR chain.
- **Capture Mode** — latches the actual pin value into the cell.
- **Shift Mode** — shifts data from TDI toward TDO.
- **Update Mode** — latches shifted data into the parallel output register, which then drives the pin.

## 2.3 TAP Controller — 16-State State Machine

The TAP controller transitions based on **TMS values sampled at the rising edge of TCK**. The 16 states are:

| # | State | Purpose |
|---|-------|---------|
| 1 | **Test-Logic-Reset** | Normal functional mode of the device |
| 2 | **Run-Test/Idle** | Idle state between scan operations |
| 3 | **Select-DR-Scan** | Choose a data register scan |
| 4 | **Capture-DR** | Parallel-load DR from internal logic |
| 5 | **Shift-DR** | Shift data serially TDI → TDO |
| 6 | **Exit1-DR** | Possible exit from DR scan |
| 7 | **Pause-DR** | Hold DR while doing other operations |
| 8 | **Exit2-DR** | Re-enter shift or update |
| 9 | **Update-DR** | Latch shifted data into DR output |
| 10 | **Select-IR-Scan** | Choose an instruction register scan |
| 11 | **Capture-IR** | Load default instruction |
| 12 | **Shift-IR** | Shift new instruction in serially |
| 13 | **Exit1-IR** | Possible exit from IR scan |
| 14 | **Pause-IR** | Pause IR shifting |
| 15 | **Exit2-IR** | Re-enter shift or update IR |
| 16 | **Update-IR** | Latch new instruction into IR |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Test-Logic-Reset is reached when TMS = 1 for 5 consecutive TCK cycles.** This is a high-yield KTU fact.

## 2.4 Mandatory JTAG Instructions (IEEE 1149.1)

| Instruction | Binary Code (example) | Function |
|-------------|----------------------|----------|
| **EXTEST** | `0000` | Tests off-chip interconnects; BSR cells drive output pins |
| **SAMPLE/PRELOAD** | `0001` | Samples I/O without interfering; preloads test data |
| **BYPASS** | `1111` | Connects TDI → TDO via 1-bit bypass register |
| **INTEST** | `0010` | Tests internal core logic via BSR |
| **IDCODE** | `0110` | Reads 32-bit device ID register |
| **HIGHZ** | `0111` | Places all outputs in high-impedance state |

## 2.5 Test Operation Flow (EXTEST Example)

The standard procedure to test board interconnects is:

1. **Preload** — Shift known pattern into BSR via Shift-DR (in SAMPLE/PRELOAD mode).
2. **Switch to EXTEST** — Update-IR loads EXTEST instruction.
3. **Capture** — Capture-DR reads actual board response.
4. **Shift out** — Shift-DR extracts captured values via TDO.
5. **Update** — Update-DR applies next test pattern.
6. **Compare** — Compare shifted-out values with expected responses.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula / Expression | Unit / Notes |
|----------|----------------------|--------------|
| Total test vector length | $L = \sum_{i=1}^{N} L_i$ | bits, where $L_i$ is BSR length of chip *i* and *N* is number of JTAG devices in chain |
| Test application time | $T_{\text{test}} = L \cdot T_{\text{TCK}}$ | seconds |
| TCK period | $T_{\text{TCK}} = 1 / f_{\text{TCK}}$ | seconds |
| Effective test throughput | $R = f_{\text{TCK}} / L$ | vectors/second |
| Stuck-at fault coverage (theoretical) | $C = 1 - (1/2^n)$ | for *n*-bit test vector |
| Chain length with Bypass | $L_{\text{chain}} = L_{\text{BSR, target}} + \sum_{j \neq \text{target}} 1$ | each non-target chip contributes **1 bit** via Bypass |
| Minimum IR length | $m \geq \lceil \log_2 k \rceil$ | *k* = number of instructions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always use `\vert` (or `\mid`) instead of the literal pipe symbol `|` inside markdown tables to prevent table-rendering breakage. For example, write $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$, **not** $|x|$.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Boundary Scan is critical in:
- **BGA / QFN packages** — physical probing is impossible; only JTAG works.
- **Multi-layer PCBs** with buried vias.
- **FPGA / CPLD in-system programming** — every Xilinx, Altera, and Lattice device uses JTAG.
- **ARM Cortex-M debug** — uses JTAG (or SWD) for flashing and debugging firmware.
- **Automotive ECUs, Aerospace, Medical devices** — high-reliability boards require structural testability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum IR Length Required

IEEE 1149.1 mandates at minimum the instructions **BYPASS, SAMPLE/PRELOAD, and EXTEST**. The minimum number of distinct instruction codes is:

$$k_{\min} = 3 \text{ (BYPASS, SAMPLE/PRELOAD, EXTEST)}$$

For *k* distinct instructions, the minimum number of bits *m* required to encode them uniquely is:

$$m = \lceil \log_2 k \rceil$$

Substituting $k = k_{\min} = 3$:

$$m_{\min} = \lceil \log_2 3 \rceil = \lceil 1.585 \rceil = 2 \text{ bits}$$

> **Conversion logic:** $\log_2 3 = 1.5849\ldots$; the ceiling (smallest integer $\geq$ value) is **2**, hence 2 bits suffice to encode 3 mandatory instructions (the fourth code `11` is reserved/unused).

For a typical microcontroller implementing 6 instructions (e.g., BYPASS, EXTEST, SAMPLE/PRELOAD, INTEST, IDCODE, HIGHZ):

$$m = \lceil \log_2 6 \rceil = \lceil 2.585 \rceil = 3 \text{ bits}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Bypass Shortening of a JTAG Chain

**Given:** A JTAG chain contains 4 chips. Their BSR lengths are $L_1 = 200, L_2 = 350, L_3 = 180, L_4 = 400$ bits. The user wishes to test only **chip 3**.

**Step 1 — Total chain length without bypass:**

$$L_{\text{total}} = L_1 + L_2 + L_3 + L_4 = 200 + 350 + 180 + 400 = 1130 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Bypass the three non-target chips.** Each bypass register is only 1 bit, so:

$$L_{\text{eff}} = 1 + 1 + L_3 + 1 = 1 + 1 + 180 + 1 = 183 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — Speedup factor:**

$$S = \frac{L_{\text{total}}}{L_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{1130}{183} \approx 6.174$$

> **Conversion logic:** By programming chips 1, 2, 4 with the BYPASS instruction, their data contribution to the chain shrinks from hundreds of bits to a single bit each — yielding a **~6.17× reduction** in test-application time.

## 3.3 Derivation: Test Application Time

**Given:** A board has $N = 10$ JTAG devices in a single chain. Average BSR length $\overline{L} = 300$ bits. The TCK frequency is $f_{\text{TCK}} = 10 \text{ MHz}$. The number of test vectors is $V = 50$.

**Step 1 — Total chain length:**

$$L_{\text{chain}} = N \cdot \overline{L} = 10 \times 300 = 3000 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Clock period:**

$$T_{\text{TCK}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{TCK}}} = \frac{1}{10 \times 10^6} = 100 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3 — Time per vector:**

$$T_{\text{vec}} = L_{\text{chain}} \cdot T_{\text{TCK}} = 3000 \times 100 \times 10^{-9} = 3 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 300 \text{ μs}$$

**Step 4 — Total test time:**

$$T_{\text{total}} = V \cdot T_{\text{vec}} = 50 \times 300 \text{ μs} = 15000 \text{ μs} = 15 \text{ ms}$$

> **Conversion logic:** Each bit of the 3000-bit chain requires one TCK cycle (100 ns). 50 vectors are applied sequentially, multiplying the per-vector time accordingly.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Boundary Scan Cell Modes

| Mode | MUX Select | Function | KTU High-Yield Point |
|------|------------|----------|----------------------|
| Normal (Functional) | 0 | System I/O flows unaltered | Device operates as if JTAG absent |
| Capture | 01 | Pin value latched into first FF | Used in EXTEST to read board response |
| Shift | 01 | Data shifts serially through cell | TDI → cell$_1$ → cell$_2$ → ... → TDO |
| Update | 10 | Second FF latches shifted value | Drives pin with new test stimulus |
| High-Z | 11 | Output driver disabled | Used in HIGHZ instruction |

## 3.5 Quick-Reference: TAP State Transitions (TMS values)

To go from **Test-Logic-Reset** to **Shift-DR**:

$$\text{Test-Logic-Reset} \xrightarrow{\text{TMS}=0} \text{Run-Test/Idle} \xrightarrow{\text{TMS}=1} \text{Select-DR-Scan} \xrightarrow{\text{TMS}=0} \text{Capture-DR} \xrightarrow{\text{TMS}=0} \text{Shift-DR}$$

To go from **Shift-DR** to **Update-DR**:

$$\text{Shift-DR} \xrightarrow{\text{TMS}=1} \text{Exit1-DR} \xrightarrow{\text{TMS}=1} \text{Update-DR}$$

> **Conversion logic:** The TAP FSM is a Mealy machine; TMS = 0 advances the "main path" (Reset → Idle → Select → Capture → Shift), while TMS = 1 steers toward the "exit → update" path. **Five consecutive TMS = 1 always returns to Test-Logic-Reset** — a frequently asked KTU concept.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IEEE 1149.1 Device Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    TCKpin[TCK Pin] --> TAPctrl[TAP Controller FSM 16 states]
    TMSpin[TMS Pin] --> TAPctrl
    TRSTpin[TRST Pin optional] --> TAPctrl
    TDIpin[TDI Pin] --> MUXIR{MUX IR or DR}
    MUXIR --> IRreg[Instruction Register IR]
    MUXIR --> DRmux{DR MUX}
    DRmux --> BSR[Boundary Scan Register BSR]
    DRmux --> BYPASS[Bypass Register 1 bit]
    DRmux --> IDREG[IDCODE Register 32 bit]
    TAPctrl --> MUXIR
    TAPctrl --> DRmux
    IRreg --> IRdecode[IR Decoder]
    IRdecode --> DRmux
    BSR --> TDOpin[TDO Pin]
    BYPASS --> TDOpin
    IDREG --> TDOpin
    IRreg --> TDOpin
```

## 4.2 TAP Controller State Machine (Simplified)

```mermaid
graph TD
    TLR[Test Logic Reset] -->|TMS 0| RTI[Run Test Idle]
    RTI -->|TMS 1| SDS[Select DR Scan]
    SDS -->|TMS 1| SIR[Select IR Scan]
    SDS -->|TMS 0| CDR[Capture DR]
    SIR -->|TMS 1| TLR
    SIR -->|TMS 0| CIR[Capture IR]
    CDR -->|TMS 0| SHDR[Shift DR]
    CDR -->|TMS 1| E1DR[Exit1 DR]
    SHDR -->|TMS 0| SHDR
    SHDR -->|TMS 1| E1DR
    E1DR -->|TMS 0| PDR[Pause DR]
    E1DR -->|TMS 1| UDR[Update DR]
    PDR -->|TMS 0| PDR
    PDR -->|TMS 1| E2DR[Exit2 DR]
    E2DR -->|TMS 0| SHDR
    E2DR -->|TMS 1| UDR
    UDR -->|TMS 1| TLR
    UDR -->|TMS 0| RTI
    CIR -->|TMS 0| SHIR[Shift IR]
    CIR -->|TMS 1| E1IR[Exit1 IR]
    SHIR -->|TMS 0| SHIR
    SHIR -->|TMS 1| E1IR
    E1IR -->|TMS 0| PIR[Pause IR]
    E1IR -->|TMS 1| UIR[Update IR]
    PIR -->|TMS 0| PIR
    PIR -->|TMS 1| E2IR[Exit2 IR]
    E2IR -->|TMS 0| SHIR
    E2IR -->|TMS 1| UIR
    UIR -->|TMS 1| TLR
    UIR -->|TMS 0| RTI
```

## 4.3 Boundary Scan Cell Internal Structure

```mermaid
graph TD
    SysIn[System Logic Input] --> MUXA{MUX A}
    PinIn[Pin Value] --> CaptureFF[Capture FF]
    CaptureFF --> MUXA
    MUXA --> ShiftFF[Shift FF]
    ShiftFF --> MUXB{MUX B}
    UpdateFF[Update FF] --> MUXB
    MUXB --> PinOut[Pin Output Driver]
    ShiftFF --> NextCell[To Next Boundary Cell]
    PrevCell[From Previous Cell] --> ShiftFF
```

## 4.4 Multi-Chip JTAG Chain with Bypass Shortening

```mermaid
graph LR
    TDI[TDI Tester] --> CH1[Chip 1 BSR 200 bits]
    CH1 --> CH2[Chip 2 BYPASS 1 bit]
    CH2 --> CH3[Chip 3 BSR 180 bits TARGET]
    CH3 --> CH4[Chip 4 BYPASS 1 bit]
    CH4 --> TDO[TDO to Tester]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Chips 1, 2, 4 are placed in BYPASS mode (1-bit each) while chip 3 is exercised in EXTEST — the effective chain length drops from **1130 bits to 183 bits**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Boundary Scan. List the mandatory pins of the Test Access Port as per IEEE 1149.1.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Boundary Scan is a Design-for-Testability (DFT) technique standardized by **IEEE 1149.1** (JTAG) that embeds a shift-register path around the boundary of an IC to enable testing of interconnects and core logic without physical probes. **(1.5 marks)**

Mandatory TAP pins: **(1.5 marks)**

1. **TCK** — Test Clock input
2. **TMS** — Test Mode Select input
3. **TDI** — Test Data In (serial input)
4. **TDO** — Test Data Out (serial output)
5. **TRST** — Test Reset (optional, asynchronous)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the function of the BYPASS instruction in a JTAG chain.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **BYPASS** instruction (e.g., binary `1111`) is a mandatory IEEE 1149.1 instruction that, when loaded into the Instruction Register, connects the **TDI pin directly to the TDO pin through a 1-bit Bypass Register**, effectively removing that chip from the active scan path. **(2 marks)**

**Use case:** In a multi-chip JTAG chain, BYPASS is loaded into all non-target devices so that test vectors can be shifted past them quickly (each contributes only 1 bit to the chain), drastically reducing test application time. **(1 mark)**

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of an IEEE 1149.1 compliant device. Identify the role of each block.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the EXTEST instruction in detail. Show how a typical EXTEST-based board interconnect test is executed step by step.

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

The IEEE 1149.1 architecture comprises four mandatory blocks:

1. **TAP Controller** — a 16-state FSM synchronized to TCK, driven by TMS. It generates control signals for register selection and shifting. **(1.5 marks)**
2. **Instruction Register (IR)** — a parallel-load, serial-shift register that holds the currently active test instruction (minimum 2 bits). Its output is decoded by the **Instruction Decoder** to select the active data register. **(1.5 marks)**
3. **Data Registers** — at minimum two:
   - **Boundary Scan Register (BSR)** — chain of boundary scan cells, one per functional pin, used to capture and apply test data. **(1.5 marks)**
   - **Bypass Register** — a 1-bit register providing a short TDI→TDO path. **(1 mark)**
4. **TAP Pins (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO, optional TRST)** — the external 4-/5-wire interface to the test controller. **(1 mark)**

A block diagram is shown in Section 4.1 above. **(0.5 mark)**

#### Model Solution for (b):

**EXTEST (External Test)** is the principal instruction used to test **off-chip interconnections** (board-level wires) between JTAG-compliant devices. It causes every boundary scan cell to operate in its **test mode**, driving the device's output pins with the contents of the BSR's update latches, and capturing the actual pin values into the BSR's capture latches. **(2 marks)**

**Step-by-step EXTEST procedure:**

1. **Enter Test-Logic-Reset** by holding TMS = 1 for 5 TCK cycles. The device is reset; functional logic is isolated. **(0.5 mark)**
2. **Load SAMPLE/PRELOAD instruction** into the IR (Shift-IR then Update-IR). **(0.5 mark)**
3. **Preload test stimulus** into the BSR: shift desired values along TDI through Shift-DR, then latch with Update-DR. **(1 mark)**
4. **Load EXTEST instruction** into the IR. The BSR update latches now drive the output pins of the chip. **(0.5 mark)**
5. **Capture board response** by issuing Capture-DR. The actual values appearing on the input pins (driven by the neighbouring chip) are latched into the BSR capture latches. **(1 mark)**
6. **Shift out response** via Shift-DR and read from TDO. **(0.5 mark)**
7. **Apply next pattern** by loading new stimulus into BSR (Update-DR). **(0.5 mark)**
8. **Compare** shifted-out values with expected responses. Any mismatch indicates a fault (open, short, or stuck-at) on the interconnect. **(0.5 mark)**

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Draw and explain the 16-state TAP controller state machine. Why is it called a "Test Access Port"?

**(b) [7 marks]** A JTAG chain consists of 5 chips with BSR lengths [250, 400, 150, 320, 280] bits. If chip 3 is the target, calculate (i) the chain length with all chips in BSR mode, (ii) the effective chain length when chips 1, 2, 4, 5 are placed in BYPASS, and (iii) the test application time per vector at $f_{\text{TCK}} = 20 \text{ MHz}$.

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

The TAP controller is a 16-state finite state machine defined by IEEE 1149.1. It is called a **Test Access Port** because the four/five external pins (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO, TRST) constitute the *port* through which an external tester *accesses* the internal test logic of the IC. **(1 mark)**

The 16 states are organized in two parallel paths: one for the **Instruction Register (IR)** and one for **Data Registers (DR)**. The state diagram is shown in Section 4.2. **(3 marks for diagram)**

Key states:

- **Test-Logic-Reset** — entry/reset state. The device is in normal functional mode. Reached by 5 consecutive TMS = 1. **(0.5 mark)**
- **Run-Test/Idle** — quiescent state between operations. **(0.5 mark)**
- **Capture-DR / Shift-DR / Update-DR** — three core states for DR operations. Capture loads parallel data, Shift moves it serially, Update latches it to outputs. **(1 mark)**
- **Capture-IR / Shift-IR / Update-IR** — analogous states for IR operations. **(0.5 mark)**
- **Pause-DR / Pause-IR** — hold registers while other operations occur. **(0.5 mark)**

State transitions are governed by TMS values sampled on the **rising edge of TCK**. **(Optional note for full marks)**

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** BSR lengths $L = [250, 400, 150, 320, 280]$ bits; target = chip 3; $f_{\text{TCK}} = 20 \text{ MHz}$.

**(i) Total chain length with all BSRs:**

$$L_{\text{total}} = 250 + 400 + 150 + 320 + 280 = 1400 \text{ bits}$$ **(2 marks)**

**(ii) Effective chain length with BYPASS on chips 1, 2, 4, 5:**

Each non-target chip contributes 1 bit, target chip 3 contributes $L_3 = 150$ bits:

$$L_{\text{eff}} = 1 + 1 + 150 + 1 + 1 = 154 \text{ bits}$$ **(2.5 marks)**

**(iii) Test application time per vector:**

Compute the TCK period:

$$T_{\text{TCK}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{TCK}}} = \frac{1}{20 \times 10^6} = 50 \text{ ns}$$ **(0.5 mark)**

Per-vector time:

$$T_{\text{vec}} = L_{\text{eff}} \cdot T_{\text{TCK}} = 154 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} = 7700 \text{ ns} = 7.7 \text{ μs}$$ **(2 marks)**

> **Conversion logic:** Each bit of the effective chain consumes one TCK cycle. 154 bits × 50 ns/bit = 7.7 μs per vector — a **~9.09× speedup** over the un-bypassed chain.

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Boundary Scan):**
> 1. **Forgetting that the IR is selected via Shift-IR / Update-IR, not directly by TAP pins.** Many students write "load instruction into TCK" — wrong. **TCK is a clock, not data.**
> 2. **Confusing EXTEST with INTEST.** EXTEST tests inter-chip wiring; INTEST tests internal core logic. Examiners specifically look for the distinction.
> 3. **Stating "5 TCK cycles" without specifying "TMS = 1 for 5 cycles"** to reach Test-Logic-Reset. Half-mark deduction if you omit the TMS condition.
> 4. **Forgetting TRST is OPTIONAL** — saying "5 mandatory pins" loses 0.5 mark.
> 5. **In numerical problems, students often forget to convert MHz to Hz** when computing $T_{\text{TCK}}$, leading to wrong units and lost marks.
> 6. **Drawing TAP state diagram with 8 or 12 states** instead of 16 — full diagram is mandatory for 7-mark sub-questions.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Boundary Scan = IEEE 1149.1 = JTAG** — synonymous terms; one question, three names.
- **Five TAP pins:** TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO, (optional) TRST.
- **Two mandatory data registers:** Boundary Scan Register (BSR) and Bypass Register (1-bit).
- **Minimum IR size = 2 bits** (encodes BYPASS + SAMPLE/PRELOAD + EXTEST).
- **Three mandatory instructions:** BYPASS, SAMPLE/PRELOAD, EXTEST.
- **TAP controller has 16 states** organized in two parallel paths (IR and DR).
- **5 consecutive TMS = 1 → Test-Logic-Reset** — a frequently asked KTU fact.
- **EXTEST** tests inter-chip wiring; **INTEST** tests on-chip core logic.
- **BYPASS shortens the chain** — each non-target chip contributes only 1 bit.
- **Test time formula:** $T_{\text{vec}} = L_{\text{chain}} \times T_{\text{TCK}}$.
- **Boundary scan cell** has two FFs (capture + update) and a 2:1 / 3:1 MUX.
- **JTAG is the only viable test method** for BGA, QFN, and high-density packages where physical probing is impossible.
- **SDR mode:** data changes on one TCK edge; **DDR mode:** data changes on both edges (advanced topic, rarely asked).
- **TRST** is asynchronous; the FSM-reset via TMS = 1 (5 cycles) is synchronous.
- **BSDL (Boundary Scan Description Language)** is the VHDL-like language that describes a chip's boundary scan behavior — used by automatic test pattern generators (ATPG).
- **KGU (Kerala Global University) / KTU students:** remember to draw the TAP FSM in **any 14-mark question** that mentions JTAG — even partial credit demands a diagram.
